Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
Issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  yeai  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy.

ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number 


How  the  Library  Serves  the  Public 


I.    l Agencies  and  Activities. 

It  is  not  generally  understood  that  the 
Chicago  Public  Library  utilizes  many  agen- 
cies for  the  distribution  and  use  of  books 
in  addition  to  the  central  building  on  Wash- 
ington street,  and  twenty-six  branches  and 
eighty-seven  delivery  stations  located  in 
many  other  sections  of  the  city.  Nor  have 
the  general  public  more  than  an  incom- 
plete knowledge  of  its  system  of  work 
whereby  more  than  3,000,000  issues  of  books 
for  home  use  are  annually  effected,  and 
200,000  reference  volumes  are  used  a  mil- 
lion times  a  year.  An  average  of  15,532 
persons  for  every  day  of  the  year  repre- 
sents the  patronage  of  the  Library,  and 
relatively  a  small  number  of  these  users 
know  the  full  extent  of  its  resources  of  spe- 
cial interest  to  themselves.  In  the  series  of 
articles  which  will  follow  in  succeeding 
numbers  of  the  Book  Bulletin  the  agencies 
and  activities  of  the  Library  will  be  de- 
scribed, not  in  minute  detail,  but  with  suffi- 
cient elaboration  to  give  to  the  readers  of 
Chicago  a  more  intimate  touch  with  the  in- 
stitution and  its  scope  and  methods  than 
now  obtains. 

The  total  number  of  places  where  books 


are  issued  for  use  at  home  is  488,  and  the 
distribution  of  all  of  these  agencies  with 
the  exception  of  school  deposits  is  shown 
on  the  accompanying  map.  The  agencies 
represented  in  the  service  of  the  Library 
are  the  following: 

1  Central  building. 
26  Branches. 
87  Delivery  stations. 
23  Deposit  stations. 
9  Traveling  library  stations. 
342  School  deposit  stations. 
Of  the  twenty-six  branches,  two  are  lo- 
cated    in     well-equipped     buildings     con- 
structed  for   the    purpose;    fifteen    in   park 
field  houses;  one  in  a  building  designed  as 
a   suburban  railway   station   and   converted 
to   library   use;   two   in   institutional   build- 
ings; five  in  rented  quarters;  one  in  a  school 
building. 

The  branches  in  each  geographical  divi- 
sion of  the  city  number: 

North   Side    2 

South  Side  11 

West  Side   13 

A  glance  at  the  accompanying  map  shows 
that  some  of  the  remoter  sections  of  the 
city  are  seventeen  miles  distant  from  the 
central  district,  and  that  the  agencies  of 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


the  Library  farthest  from  the  central  build- 
ing are  located  at  distances  of  fourteen 
miles  south,  nine  miles  north,  nine  miles 
west  and  thirteen  miles  southwest.  The 
Library  maintains  six  automobile  delivery 
trucks  in  serving  the  numerous  agencies. 

The  activities  of  the  Library,  broadly 
grouped,  embrace  educational,  informational 
and  recreational  features.  Emphasis  is 
placed  on  the  first  feature,  especially  in  co- 
operation with  the  schools  and  through 
local  expedients  such  as  story  hours,  ex- 
hibits and  kindred  by-activities.  In  its  serv- 
ice to  business  men  also,  the  Library  is 
seeking  to  meet  and  to  anticipate  needs  and 
demands. 


The  Story  of  the  Year  in  Figures 

Substantial  gains  were  made  in  every  de- 
partment of  the  Library  during  the  year 
1913. 

The  circulation  again  showed  a  large  in- 
crease over  the  year  preceding,  the  total 
being  3,118,367  as  compared  with  2,907,094 
in  1912;  gain,  211,273. 

Visitors  at  the  Central  building,  includ- 
ing all  departments,  and  at  the  branches, 
numbered  5,672,326.  The  count  was  nec- 
essarily incomplete,  this  number  being 
doubtless  less  than  the  actual  total. 

More  than  50,000  men  and  women  used 
the  Civics  Room,  the  recorded  number 
being  50,016.  The  material  asked  for  com- 
prised the  following:  Pamphlets,  101,730; 
magazines,  19,231;  directories,  20,832;  bound 
newspaper  files,  8,763;  books,  16,415.  The 
Patents  Room  record  included  29,7.57  vis- 
itors, and  126,125  volumes  were  issued  for 
their  consultation. 

The  Main  reference  room  had  a  record 
year,  with  a  total  use  over  the  counter  of 
377,637  volumes  by  144,188.  No  count  was 
kept  of  the  thousands  of  volumes  on  the 
open  shelves  which  were  used  by  patrons. 

In  the  Art  Room  44,173  volumes  were  is- 
sued to  11,782  applicants. 

Applicants  for  cards  required  to  secure 
books  for  home  use  numbered  60,137. 

At  the  close  of  the  year  there  were  118 
delivery  stations.  Through  this  source 
811,190  books  were  distributed,  and  131,370 
of  these  went  to  branches,  which  gave  out 
from  their  own  shelves  1,367,329  volumes 
in  addition.  The  26  branches  therefore  cir- 
culated 1,498,699  books  in  all. 

Ten  Deposit  stations,  or  Traveling  libra- 
ries, had  a  total  circulation  of  73,802,  and 
fourteen  Business  house  deposits  added 
130,619  to  the  number  of  volumes  circulated 
by  the  Library. 

School  collections  again  showed  an  ap- 
preciable gain  in  the  class  rooms  whose 
teachers  supervised  their  use.  The  total 
circulation  was  112,992  as  compared  with 


79,994  in  1912,  53,000  in  1911,  25,000  in  1910, 
and  606  in  1909. 


A   New   Library   Catalogue 

A  new  and  completely  revised  Finding 
List  of  books  in  this  Library  on  the  Social 
Sciences  has  been  prepared,  and  is  now  in 
press  and  will  be  issued  on  January  31. 
This  list,  it  is  hoped,  will  serve  as  a  help- 
ful guide  to  the  voluminous  mass  of  ma- 
terial in  the  Library  dealing  with  the  great 
variety  of  subjects  comprehensively  classed 
under  the  term  Social  Sciences.  Broadly 
the  classes  included  are:  Sociology,  together 
with  statistics  and  auxiliary  subjects;  po- 
litical science,  government,  constitutional 
law  and  history,  politics  and  foreign  rela- 
tions; economics,  capital,  wealth,  labor,  pro- 
duction and  distribution,  works  on  eco- 
nomic conditions  and  history  in  various 
countries,  and  allied  topics.'  Applied  or 
practical  sociology  forms  the  third  general 
division,  embracing  social  pathology,  pov- 
erty, crime,  and  preventive  and  meliorative 
methods  and  organizations.  Education  oc- 
cupies very  large  space  in  the  list,  all 
branches  of  the  science,  theory  and  prac- 
tice of  education  being  represented  by  cred- 
itable collections.  Social  life  and  customs, 
including  class  and  group  divisions  of  so- 
ciety and  the  wide  and  interesting  field  of 
popular  customs,  habits  and  costumes, 
makes  up  the  concluding  part. 

This  publication  is  not  intended  to  serve 
as  a  complete  and  exhaustive  catalogue  of 
all  the  material  in  the  Library  upon  these 
numerous  subjects.  It  is  merely  a  finding 
list  of  the  books  and  does  not  cover  maga- 
zine articles  nor  chapters  in  books  or  other 
references  which  will  still  have  to  be  sought 
through  the  card  catalogue  and  the  other 
well-known  aids  and  guides.  It  will  not 
even  be  a  complete  list  of  all  the  books, 
as  under  many  subjects  only  the  more  im- 
portant and  modern  material  could  be  in- 
cluded, and  numerous  titles  which  were 
clearly  out  of  date  were  omitted.  Not- 
withstanding this  policy  of  omission  and 
exclusion  the  list  comprises  nearly  four 
hundred  pages  and  will,  it  is  hoped,  prove 
a  useful  guide  to  patrons  of  the  Library.  It 
will  be  on  sale  after  February  1,  at  the  main 
library  and  branches,  at  25  cents  per  copy. 


"Books   of   1913" 

The  annual  compilation,  "Books  of  1913," 
comprising  the  cumulation  of  titles  printed 
in  the  monthly  Book  Bulletin,  will  be  ready 
Saturday,  January  24th,  and  will  be  on  sale 
at  10  cents  per  copy.  This  is  a  classified 
list,  with  author,  subject  and  title  indexes 
and  embraces  titles  of  all  books  added  to 
the  Library  during  the  year  1913.

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate ;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those '  marked  Doc.  are  in  the 
Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Fire  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
corer  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy.

How  the  Library  Serves  the  Public 


//.    Industrial  and  Commercial  Deposits. 

For  the  convenient  use  of  the  wage- 
earners  living  in  the  remoter  sections  of 
the  city,  who  crowd  the  surface  and  ele- 
vated cars  to  their  maximum  capacity  dur- 
ing the  morning  hours,  and  again  at  dusk, 
the  Chicago  Public  Library  has  installed 
in  the  places  where  they  work  collections 
of  books  numbering  from  300  to  1,500 
volumes.  These  can  be  drawn  for  home 
use  and  exchanged  as  desired  in  the  same 
manner  and  with  like  privileges  as  in  the 
branch  libraries  open  to  the  general  pub- 
lic. 

Cordial  cooperation  on  the  part  of  the 
business  managers  controlling  these  indus- 
trial and  commercial  establishments  has 
made  possible  the  administration  of  the 
business  house  branches  on  a  broad  and 
generous  basis.  The  employees  in  the 
several  factories  or  other  establishments 
number  from  1,000  to  10,000;  without  the 
facilities  thus  offered,  many  thousands  of 
them  would  be  unable  to  use  the  Library 
because  long  distances  between  their  homes 
and  the  nearest  library  agencies  would  prac- 
tically debar  them  from  such  use.  The 
agreement  entered  into  toy  the  Library 
and  each  of  the  business  houses  concerned 
contemplates  the  following  division  of 
service  and  expense: 


The  firm  supplies  adequate  quarters  with 
necessary  equipment  of  shelving  and  furni- 
ture, and  employs  a  competent  librarian  to 
issue  the  books  drawn  for  home  use  by 
their  employees,  and  to  aid  the  latter  in 
such  reference  work  as  their  interest  in 
correspondence  school  courses,  and  evening 
school  courses  or  courses  of  instruction 
financed  by  the  firm,  may  demand.  For 
books  not  represented  in  the  deposit  col- 
lection, choice  may  be  made  of  books  in 
the  Central  collection  of  the  Library,  the 
firm  providing  the  transportation  thereof 
by  one  of  their  wagons  (usually  a  daily 
call). 

The  Library  selects  and  provides  the 
books,  making  exchanges  at  frequent  in- 
tervals to  keep  the  collection  up-to-date 
and  attractive,  and  supplies  sets  of  printed 
finding  lists  to  enable  selection  of  books 
supplemental  to  the  local  collection.  An 
added  service  is  that  of  reference  research 
at  the  main  library  on  questions  of  busi- 
ness information  and  kindred  topics. 

Not  including  the  collections  used  lo- 
cally by  the  Eleanor  Club  and  the  Women's 
Trade  Union  League,  whose  membership 
is  made  up  of  working  girls,  there  are  now 
nineteen  deposit  collections  of  the  charac- 
ter described.  Although  some  of  them 
were  in  operation  but  a  fraction  of  the 


18 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


year,  the  total  circulation  of  books  during 
1913,  excluding  hall  use,  reached  the  large 
total  of  160,812  issues. 

Many  of  the  firms  have  considerably  en- 
larged the  space  devoted  to  their  libraries 
since  these  were  first  installed,  and  have 
shown  great  interest  in  rendering  them  at- 
tractive as  well  as  convenient  for  the  use  of 
their  workers. 

Their  house  organs  devote  space  month- 
ly to  features  of  library  interest,  and  to 
lists  of  books  that  appeal  to  those  who  are 
endeavoring  to  equip  themselves  for  more 
responsible  positions — including  accounting, 
salesmanship,  efficiency  methods,  and  the 
special  information  required  in  departments 
devoted  to  textiles,  ceramics,  electrical  ap- 
pliances, interior  decoration,  window  dis- 
plays, advertising  and  allied  subjects.  Two 
of  the  firms  also  issue  monthly  library  bul- 
letins for  distribution  among  their  em- 
ployees, and  one  of  the  firms  utilizes  the 
back  of  the  menu  cards  in  their  house  lunch 
room  for  like  bulletin  purposes.  Some  of 
the  firms  have  added  to  the  general  col- 
lection encyclopedias,  handbooks,  diction- 
aries and  other  reference  sets. 

From  the  annual  summary  furnished  by 
the  business  house  branch  which  has  the 
largest  deposit  (1,800  volumes),  the  follow- 
ing figures  are  derived: 

Total  circulation  of  books,  32,354. 

Total  magazine  circulation,  11,262. 

Number  of  new  cards  issued,  1,616. 

One  of  the  mercantile  houses  compiles 
a  monthly  summary  of  statistics.  For  the 
final  month  of  the  year,  the  figures  were 
as  follows: 

Books  issued  during  the  month,  4,180. 

Books  issued  during  same  month  last 
year,  3,382. 

Gain  for  the  month,  798. 

Library  Training  Class 

The  sixth  Training  Class  for  preparing 
candidates  for  positions  in  the  Library  serv- 
ice will  open  on  March  16th,  next. 
The  Chicago  Public  Library  is,  of  course, 
a  branch  of  the  municipal  civil  service,  em- 
ployment in  which  is  obtainable  only 
through  examinations  conducted  by  the 
Chicago  Civil  Service  Commission.  Ex- 
aminations for  library  service  are  technical, 
and  presuppose  some  knowledge  of  library 
work,  gained  through  experience,  through 
library  schools,  or  in  training  classes. 

The  course  is  divided  between  instruc- 
tion and  study,  and  practice  work  in  the 
different  departments  of  the  Library  and 
its  branches. 

Candidates  for  this  Training  Class  must 
be  residents  of  Chicago,  and  must  have 
had  at  least  the  equivalent  of  a  high  school 
education.  They  must  be  in  good  physical 
condition,  and  must  possess  satisfactory 


personal  qualifications.  Those  between  the 
ages  of  twenty  and  thirty-five  will  be  given 
preference.  Applicants  must  pass  an  en- 
trance examination  in  history,  literature, 
and  general  information;  this  examination 
will  be  held  at  the  Public  Library  Thurs- 
day, March  5th,  at  nine  o'clock. 

Only  those  are  expected  to  enter  the 
class  who  intend  to  accept  positions  here, 
after  passing  the  Civil  Service  examination 
and  receiving  appointment.  They  must  also 
be  willing  to  take  such  assignments  as 
are  given  them,  even  though  these  may  be 
long  distances  from  home  and  involve  eve- 
ning hours. 

Members  of  the  class  receive  no  com- 
pensation during  the  period  of  training. 
The  salaries  paid  those  who  enter  the  serv- 
ice through  the  official  examinations  usually 
begin  at  $480  a  year  and  are  subject  to 
automatic  increases  based  upon  efficiency. 

Application  blanks,  which  must  be  filled 
out  and  returned  not  later  than  March  1st, 
will  be  supplied  upon  request.  It  is  very 
desirable  that  each  applicant  have  a  per- 
sonal interview  before  that  date  with  the 
Director  of  the  Training  Class,  Miss  Faith 
E.  Smith,  with  whom  appointments  may 
be  made  for  any  hour  between  9:00  a.  m. 
and  5:30  p.  m.  daily,  in  the  Training  Class 
room,  fourth  floor,  Library  building. 

A  New  Branch  for  the  North  Side 

On  or  about  March  1st,  the  Sheridan 
Branch  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  will 
be  opened  to  the  public.  Its  location  at  4734-36 
Racine  avenue,  near  the  junction  of  Broad- 
way and  Lawrence  avenue,  renders  it  of 
convenient  access  to  a  large  and  compactly 
settled  territory.  Commodious  and  well- 
equipped  reading  rooms,  both  for  adults 
and  young  people,  will  be  provided,  and 
there  will  be  a  well-balanced  collection  of 
books  for  home  use. 

Notice  to  Users  of  Books 
Patrons  of  the  Library  who  withdraw 
books  for  use  at  home  are  cautioned  to 
examine  the  volumes  upon  securing  them, 
and  to  call  immediate  attention  to  any 
mutilations,  or  damaged  condition,  of  any 
book  so  obtained.  The  last  user  of  a  book 
is  held  responsible  for  all  damages  noted 
upon  its  return. 

Legal  Holidays 

On  Lincoln's  Birthday,  Thursday,  Feb. 
12th  and  on  Monday,  Feb.  23d,  the  day  set 
apart  for  the  commemoration  of  Washing- 
ton's Birthday,  the  circulating  department 
of  the  Main  Library  will  be  closed  and  the 
reference  and  reading  rooms  will  be  open 
from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

The  branches  will  be  open  from  2  to 
6  p.  m.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:80  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


How  the  Library  Serves  the  Public 


///.•     Books  and  Readings  for  the  Blind 

In  providing  reading  matter  for  the  pub- 
lic, the  Public  Library  does  not  leave  out  of 
account  those  who  have  been  denied  the 
blessing  of  sight.  It  is  estimated  that  there 
are  in  Chicago  between  2,000  and  2,500 
blind  persons.  For  their  enjoyment,  the 
Library  has  a  collection  of  1,370  volumes  in 
raised  letters.  While  there  is  maintained, 
in  the  north  wing  of  the  main  building  on 
Michigan  Avenue  and  Randolph  Street,  a 
reading  room  where  applicants  are  given 
accommodations,  most  of  the  blind  readers 
use  the  books  in  their  homes.  The  federal 
government,  by  special  enactment  a  few 
years  ago,  exempted  from  cost  of  postage, 
books  for  the  blind  sent  through  the  mails 
by  public  libraries  for  the  use  of  their  pa- 
trons. In  this  way,  the  Chicago  Public 
Library  sends  out  every  year  to  all  parts  of 
the  city  hundreds  of  books  having  the 
raised  characters  which  are  read  by  sense 
of  touch  instead  of  sight. 

Unfortunately,  there  are  seven  or  eight 
different  systems  of  letters  in  raised  form, 
and  as  most  blind  readers  have  acquired 
familiarity  with  but  one,  the  others  are 
sealed  volumes  in  the  case  of  the  individual 
user.  The  systems  principally  used  in  Illi- 
nois are  the  Moon,  which  more  nearly  cor- 


responds to  the  Roman  type,  with  certain 
variations;  and  the  American  Braille,  rep- 
resented by  a  combination  of  embossed 
dots.  Most  of  those  who  have  become 
blind  after  reaching  adult  years  readily  ac- 
quire use  of  the  Moon  system.  Students  of 
statistics  referring  to  the  blind  are  im- 
pressed with  the  fact  of  the  great  pre- 
ponderance of  adults  among  them.  It  is 
estimated  that  there  are  about  80,000  blind 
persons  in  the  United  States.  At  least  64,- 
000  of  these,  80  per  cent,  are  over  21  years 
of  age.  The  great  majority  of  the  adult 
blind  are  living  in  their  own  homes,  yet 
their  condition  renders  them  pathetically 
isolated  even  when  surrounded  by  those 
near  and  dear  to  them,  and  it  is  almost  im- 
possible to  overestimate  the  benefit  they 
derive  from  reading  for  themselves  by 
means  of  embossed  letters. 

In  the  institutions  for  the  blind,  usually 
some  system  of  embossed  dots  is  taught. 
Unfortunately,  however,  several  dot  sys- 
tems, diverse  in  underlying  principles  of 
construction  in  many  of  the  characters 
used,  and  sometimes  even  in  meanings  as- 
signed to  the  same  characters,  have  become 
firmly  established  in  this  country  and 
abroad.  Several  great  items  of  loss  are  ex- 
perienced by  blind  readers  as  a  result: 


38 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


First:  The  lessening  of  available  litera- 
ture through  the  duplication  of  titles  in 
different  systems. 

Second:  The  further  limitation  to  readers 
familiar  with  only  one  system,  by  their  in- 
ability to  use  other  systems. 

To  provide  for  as  many  blind  readers  as 
possible,  the  Chicago  Public  Library  has 
acquired  books  embossed  in  the  following 
systems: 


American  Braille, 
Moon, 

American  Line, 
New  York  Point, 


607  volumes 

459  volumes 

227  volumes 

77  volumes 


The  Jackson  bequest  received  by  the  Li- 
brary a  few  years  ago,  amounting  to  one 
thousand  dollars,  permits  the  purchase  of 
a  few  additional  volumes  annually  from  this 
fund. 

Last  year  there  were  circulated  for  home 
use  2,620  volumes,  and  93  were  used  in  the 
Room  for  the  Blind  at  the  main  Library. 

A  few  months  ago,  through  the  generous 
volunteer  efforts  of  the  Conference  of  Jew- 
ish Women's  Clubs,  comprising  forty-five 
organizations,  free  readings  for  the  blind 
were  instituted  in  the  branch  libraries  on 
two  Saturdays  of  each  month. 


One  Day's  Questions 

During  the  month  of  February,  the  Civics 
Room  was  crowded  to  its  utmost  capacity. 
Although  additional  chairs  have  been  pro- 
vided, the  Saturday  demands  have  far  ex- 
ceeded the  capacity  of  the  room.  The  num- 
ber and  variety  of  questions  upon  which 
material  is  sought  have  taxed  the  best 
efforts  of  the  reference  staff.  A  few  exam- 
ples selected  at  random  from  many  times 
the  number  of  queries  propounded  during 
the  afternoon  of  the  last  day  of  the  month, 
upon  which  information  was  furnished,  will 
serve  to  indicate  their  general  character: 

I.  Regulation  of  building  heights  in  the 
principal  cities  of  the  United  States. 

2.  Number  of  unemployed  in  Chicago. 

3.  Housing  conditions  at  Pullman. 

4.  Extent  of  financial  interests  by  Eng- 
lish investors  in  Mexico. 

5.  Is  the  child  of  an  ambassador  a  citi- 
zen of  the  United  States? 

6.  Number    of    wage    earners    in    the 
United  States. 

7.  The   Mann   act. 

8.  Restriction  of  immigration. 

9.  Cooperative   agriculture  and   credits. 
10.     Enlargement  of  the  Navy. 

II.  Proceedings    of   the    school   hygiene 
congress. 

12.  Municipal  markets. 

13.  Attempted     legislation     in     various 
states  for  voting  by  mail. 


14.  Syndicalism;  an  unbiased  discussion. 

15.  White  lists. 

16.  Economic  growth  of  cities. 

17.  To   what   extent   will   ocean   freight 
rates  be   affected   by   Panama   canal   tolls? 


A  New  North  Side  Branch 

The  second  branch  library  in  the  north 
division  of  the  city  was  opened  to  the  pub- 
lic on  Saturday,  February  28th,  at  noon.  It 
is  situated  in  that  part  of  the  twenty-fifth 
ward  known  as  Sheridan  Park,  and  has  been 
named  the  Sheridan  Branch.  The  Board 
of  Directors  was  fortunate  in  securing  ex- 
ceptionally well  adapted  quarters  in  a 
double  store  at  4734-4736  Racine  avenue,  at 
the  intersection  of  Broadway,  and  but  a  few 
doors  south  of  Lawrence  avenue.  The  new 
branch  is  thus  located  in  the  heart  of  one 
of  the  most  populous  residence  districts, 
and  is  easily  accessible  from  any  point  on 
the  north  shore. 

Sheridan  Branch  is  handsomely  furnished 
and  equipped  with  about  3,000  volumes,  al- 
most equally  proportioned  between  books 
for  children  and  for  adults.  An  adequate 
and  capable  staff  has  been  placed  in  charge 
and  it  is  confidently  expected  that  Sheri- 
dan will  soon  take  its  place  among  the 
busiest  and  largest  of  the  Library  branches. 
Hours  of  opening  are  from  9:00  a.  m.  to 
9:00  p.  m.  on  week  days,  and  from  2:00 
p.  m.  to  6:00  p.  m.  on  Sundays.  Books  may 
be  drawn  for  home  use  from  the  branch 
shelves  or  from  the  main  library  through 
the  delivery  station  service.  Borrowers' 
cards  will  be  issued  to  applicants,  and  a 
complete  list  of  catalogues  is  on  sale. 


Reading  Lists  and  Finding  List 

Two  special  lists  on  subjects  of  interest 
are  included  in  this  number  of  the  Bulle- 
tin beginning  at  page  54.  The  first  is  a 
list  of  plays  suitable  for  production  by 
amateurs  and  young  people.  The  second 
is  a  continuation  of  the  reading  list  on 
American  artists. 

The  long  delayed  finding  list  of  books 
of  the  Social  Sciences  is  now  ready.  It 
comprises  three  hundred  and  seventy 
pages  and  forms  a  complete  catalogue  of 
the  resources  of  the  library  in  the  classes 
of  sociology,  economics,  political  science, 
law,  charity,  and  correction,  criminology, 
educations,  social  problems,  social  rela- 
tions, and  numerous  cognate  topics.  Full 
author  and  title  indexes  and  an  exhaustive 
table  of  the  classification  are  added.  The 
list  is  for  sale  at  the  Main  Library  and  all 
branches  and  stations  at  twenty-five  cents 
per  copy.

Plays  for  Young  People  and  Amateurs 


The  natural  inclination  of  children  to 
"make  believe"  may  be  turned  to  good  ac- 
count by  giving  them  plays  to  act  which 
will  establish  wholesome  tastes  and  high 
ideals,  as  well  as  entertain.  This  list  com- 
prises acting  dramas  having  historical  back- 
grounds, others  chosen  from  great  litera- 
ture or  teaching  ethical  truths. 

These  books  may  be  called  for  by  the  num- 
bers given,  or  they  may  be  consulted  in  the 
Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
and  drawn  for  home  use  from  there. 

Alcott,  L.  M.     Comic  tragedies.     1907. 

E9008 

Little  plays  acted  by  "Little  women,"  who  as- 
sumed  grand  and  tragic  parts  in  most  ludicrous 
fashion.  For  girls  15  to  16  years  old. 

Bloxam,  E.  E.  Little  pageant  plays  for 
children.  1912.  E 11842 

Plays  for  little  children,  to  be  given  indoors  or 
out.  Each  one  has  some  ethical  teaching. 

Bryce,  C.  T.  Child  lore  dramatic  reader. 
1908.  J  6180 

Stories,  fables  and  rhymes  in  dialogue  form  for 
little  children. 

Bullivant,  C.  H.     Home  plays.     1913. 

E 11875 

Plays  based  on  fairy  tales  and  myths.  Full  di- 
rections concerning  scenery  and  costumes  are 
given.  For  boys  and  girls  about  18  years  old. 

Comstock,  F.  A.  A  dramatic  version  of 
Greek  myths  and  hero  tales.  1912. 

J6192 

Designed  for  a  reading  book  as  well  as  for 
dramatic  representation  in  the  upper  grammar 
grades. 

Dalkeith,  L.    Little  plays.    1909.          H  7354 
A  King  Arthur  play,  a  Robin  Hood  play,  a  fairy 
tale,   ana  a  scene  from  Uncle  Tom's  Cabin.     For 
children  from  10  to  14  years  old. 

Dickens,  C.  A  Dickens  dramatic  reader; 
scenes  from  Pickwick,  scenes  from 
Nicholas  Nickleby,  The  cricket  on  the 
hearth,  A  Christmas  carol,  by  Fanny 
Comstock.  1913.  J6198 


Short  plays  from  Dickens  for  the  use 

of  amateur  and  school  dramatic  societies.

ludes; being  6  one-act  dramas.  1910.

four  acts.  1908.  E  12011 

Parts  for  four  men  and  four  women.  A  full

classics;  a  dramatic  reader  for  grammar

dren. 1910.  E  12551

Chicago  public  Hibrarp,  ^licfjigan  Sbemie  anb  &)a$fjingtcm  Street 

Departments! 


Eulcs  anb  information  for  patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  imediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

p.  m.,  those  marked  t  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to

days and  holidays  open  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Secretary 


Henry    E.    Legler Librarian 

Carl    B.    Roden Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


Consulting  the  Card  Catalogue 


In  addition  to  the  official  card  catalog  for 
use  of  the  staff  in  the  Order  and  Catalog 
departments,  there  are  at  the  Main  building 
of  the  Library  the  following  card  catalogs 
and  indexes  for  the  use  of  patrons: 

Public  Card  Catalog — Complete  diction- 
ary catalog  of  all  the  books  in  the  Library. 
Located  on  fourth  floor,  adjacent  to  Refer- 
ence Room. 

Open  Shelf — Catalog  (author  and  title) 
of  books  in  this  department;  also  catalog 
of  new  books  added,  supplementary  to  the 
latest  printed  finding  lists. 

Information  Desk — Duplicate  of  last 
named  catalog. 

Art  Room — Catalog  of  books  in  this  de- 
partment, with  special  index  to  artists  and 
paintings. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room — Catalogs  of 
books,  in  Intermediate  department  with 
special  index  to  fairy  tales. 

Reference   Room — In  addition   to   Public 


Card  Catalog,  index  to  portraits  and  illus- 
trations in  foreign  and  domestic  periodicals; 
index  (first  lines)  to  poems  of  James  Whit- 
comb  Riley  and  Eugene  Field;  catalog  of 
books  in  the  John  Crerar  library. 

Civics  Room — Catalog  (author,  title  and 
subject)  of  books  on  social  and  economic 
topics;  catalogs  of  documentary  material 
(in  process  of  completion);  index,  author 
and  subject,  of  bills  introduced  in  the  Illi- 
nois legislature;  index  of  ordinances  pend- 
ing in  the  City  Council,  with  index  to  im- 
portant measures  under  consideration  in 
Councils  of  other  cities;  index  to  bulletins 
of  city  clubs,  commercial  organizations  and 
organs  of  associations  interested  in  ques- 
tions of  current  public  import. 

Room  for  the  Blind — Catalog  of  books 
in  embossed  type  (Braille,  Moon,  New 
York  Point  and  Line). 

The  following  placard  has  been  prepared 
for  the  information  of  users  who  wish  to 
consult  the  Public  Card  Catalog: 


58 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  Card  Catalog 

This  is  a  catalog  of  all  the  books  in  the 
Chicago  Public  Library.  Each  book  is  rep- 
resented by  two  or  more  cards,  viz: 

(1)  Under  the  surname  of  its  author. 

(2)  Under  the   first   word   of  its   title   if 

that  is  striking  or  fanciful. 

(3)  Under    the    subject    or    subjects    of 

which  the  book  treats. 

The  arrangement  is  generally  alpha- 
betical like  that  of  a  dictionary  or  encyclo- 
pedia. 

Under  the  name  of  an  author,  however, 
the  various  editions,  translations  and 


adaptations  of  each  of  his  works  are 
grouped  together  following  the  title  of  the 
original  work  without  regard  to  strict 
alphabetical  order. 

Under  some  of  the  subjects,  also,  alpha- 
betical arrangement  has  been  modified  or 
changed  to  chronological  or  systematic 
grouping  as  being  better  adapted  to  the 
needs  of  students. 

The  letter  and  number  in  the  upper  left 
hand  corner  of  the  catalog  card  is  the  call 
number  by  which  books  may  be  drawn.  A 
star  (*)  in  the  call  number  denotes  that 
the  book  so  marked  is  for  reference  use 
only  and  cannot  be  withdrawn  from  the 
library  building. 


How  the  Library  Serves  the  Public 


IV:    Book  Needs  of  the  Foreign  Born 

Out  of  a  total  of  882,516  minors  enumer- 
ated in  the  last  school  census  of  Chicago, 
only  278,461  were  children  of  American- 
born  fathers.  According  to  the  last  federal 
census,  there  were  resident  in  Chicago  at 
the  time  1,693,918  white  persons  of  foreign 
birth  or  parentage. 

While  practically  all  the  children  em- 
braced in  these  groups  prefer  to  read  books 
in  English  and  seldom  take  books  in  other 
languages  from  the  Library  except  for  the 
use  of  their  parents,  there  are  thousands  of 
adult  men  and  women  whose  familiarity 
with  the  English  language  is  so  limited 
that  they  would  be  precluded  from  using 
the  Library  if  books  in  their  own  tongues 
were  not  provided.  The  same  census  lists 
the  population  by  countries  of  nativity,  and 
includes  twenty-three  of  them.  This  classi- 
fication gives  no  complete  view  of  the  poly- 
glot character  of  the  population,  for  the 
reason  that  many  sub-divisions  of  countries 
are  merged  with  the  larger  governmental 
units,  and  certain  distinctive  racial  groups 
are  therefore  missing  altogether  from  this 
list;  for  instance,  Bohemians  are  counted 
under  Austria,  Poles  under  Russia,  etc. 
Croatians,  Lithuanians,  Letts,  Slovaks,  and 
a  number  of  other  dialectic  groups  largely 
represented  in  Chicago  do  not  have  sepa- 
rate enumeration.  There  are  more  Bo- 
hemians in  Chicago  than  Prague  can  mus- 
ter; there  are  more  Italians  here  than  can 
be  counted  in  Florence  or  Venice;  there 
are  doubtless  more  Irish  than  Dublin  pos- 
sesses, and  the  Poles  in  Chicago  outnum- 
ber those  in  the  leading  city  of  Poland. 
The  Swedes  of  Chicago,  if  gathered  to- 
gether, would  nearly  duplicate  Stockholm's 
population. 

Books  in  seventeen  different  languages 
are  obtainable  from  the  central  collection 
of  the  Library,  or  by  delivery  at  the 
branches  or  stations.  At  the  Main  building. 


one  section  of  the  Open  Shelf  department 
is  given  over  to  special  collections  in  Ger- 
man, French,  Swedish,  Dano-Norwegian, 
Yiddish,  Italian,  Bohemian,  Polish  and 
Lettish.  In  addition  there  are  in  the  Li- 
brary, books  in  the  following  languages: 
Spanish,  Portuguese,  Dutch,  Russian,  Hun- 
garian, Lithuanian,  Welsh,  Japanese  and 
Chinese.  Altogether,  the  books  in  foreign 
languages,  including  special  deposit  collec- 
tions in  some  of  the  branches,  number 
62,241. 

Deposit  collections  placed  in  some  of  the 
branches  are  taken  from  the  main  collec- 
tion and  are  selected  with  special  reference 
to  the  foreign  groups  to  be  served  in  locali- 
ties tributary  to  the  particular  branches 
where  these  smaller  collections  may  be 
drawn  upon.  The  principal  deposit  collec- 
tions are  located  in  branch  libraries  as  fol- 
lows: 

Polish — *Bessemer,  Burr,  Davis,  Dvorak, 
Eckhart,  Holstein,  Mark  White,  Sherman, 
West  Twenty-sixth  street — 9. 

Bohemian  —  Bessemer,  Cornell,  Davis 
Dvorak,  Mark  White,  Sherman,  West 
Twenty-sixth  street — 7 

Lithuanian — Bessemer,  Burr,  Davis,  Mark 
White,  Stanford— 5. 

Russian — Bessemer,  Burr,  Hebrew  Insti- 
tute, Stanford — 4. 

Hungarian — Armour,   Eckhart — 2. 

Yiddish  —  Bessemer,  Burr,  Humboldt, 
Stanford,  Hebrew  Institute — 5. 

German — Bessemer,  Burr,  Cornell,  Eck- 
hart, Fuller,  Hamlin,  Hebrew  Institute, 
Holstein,  Humboldt,  Lewis,  Logan,  Mark 
White,  Ogden,  Sherman,  Stanford,  West 
Twenty-sixth  street — 16. 

Norwegian  and  Danish — Humboldt,  Lo- 
gan, West  Twenty-sixth  street — 3. 

Swedish — Armour,  Bessemer,  Humboldt, 
Logan — 4. 


*A    list    of    the    branches    with    locations    will    be 
found  on  the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


59 


Italian— Bessemer,  Hebrew  Institute— -2. 
In  a  number  of  the  branches,  leading 
daily  and  weekly  papers  in  these  languages 
are  kept  on  file,  and  also  a  number  of 
magazines.  In  the  Reading  Room  of  the 
Main  library,  there  are  leading  magazines 
and  newspapers  representative  of  the  fol- 
lowing foreign  languages: 

Periodicals— German,  80;  French,  27; 
Bohemian,  5;  Italian,  13;  Spanish,  2;  Nor- 
wegian, 5;  Swedish,  8;  Danish,  6;  Dutch, 
3;  Hungarian,  2;  Russian,  5;  Polish,  3; 
Lithuanian,  1;  Hebraic,  1. 

Newspapers — Spanish,  1;  German,  14; 
French,  3;  Bohemian,  9;  Polish,  11;  Dutch, 
4;  Norwegian  and  Danish,  11;  Swedish,  10; 
Italian,  5;  Hungarian,  1;  Portuguese,  1; 
Greek,  3;  Lithuanian,  3;  Slovak,  1;  Croa- 
tian, 1. 

So  great  is  the  demand  at  some  of  the 
branches,  that  the  shelves  set  aside  for 
foreign  books  are  nearly  always  empty, 
volumes  being  borrowed  as  fast  as  they 
are  returned.  The  Library  is  preparing  to 
add  about  6,000  volumes  in  foreign  lan- 
guages during  1914,  but  this  supply  will 
fall  far  short  of  the  demand.  It  is  also 
planned  to  issue  the  following  finding  lists 
during  the  year: 
German — complete  revision. 
French — additions. 

Slavic  languages — a  consolidated  revision. 
Yiddish — revision. 
Russian — a  new  list. 
Modern  Greek — a  new  list. 
Lettish — a  new  list. 

Some  of  the  minor  deposit  collections  in 
commercial  and  industrial  establishments 
also  include  books  in  foreign  languages, 
and  all  of  them  are  used  a  great  deal.  At 
the  headquarters  of  the  Woman's  Trade 
Union  League,  similar  collections  are  on 
deposit,  many  of  the  young  women  who 
frequent  these  quarters  being  wholly  unable 
to  read  English.  Here,  as  elsewhere,  the 
books  in  foreign  tongues  have  a  liberal  in- 
clusion of  material  on  citizenship,  natural- 
ization, etc.,  and  there  are  volumes  dealing 
with  the  history  and  customs  of  the  United 
States.  Books  of  this  character  are  eagerly 
read. 

The  need  for  the  distribution  of  the  for- 
eign books  through  the  branch  and  other 
deposits  is  shown  by  interesting  population 
maps — one  prepared  by  the  survey  stu- 
dents of  the  McCormick  Theological  sem- 
inary; another  by  Miss  Renee  Stern,  libra- 
rian of  the  School  of  Civics,  and  a  third 
for  local  use  at  the  Library.  These  show 
in  striking  form  the  varied  population  of 
Chicago,  like  islands  of  different  nation- 
alities scattered  over  the  area.  The  most 
numerous  are  the  Yiddish  groups,  while  the 
largest  are  the  Bohemian,  Polish  and  Italian 
groups.  In  the  three  geographical  divisions 


of   the   city,   there   are   distinct   population 
colonies  as  follows: 

West  Side — German  and  Scandinavian, 
Polish,  Bohemian,  Jewish,  Italian,  Greek, 
mixed  foreign,  Armenian,  Bulgarian. 

North  Side — German,  Scandinavian  and 
German,  mixed  foreign,  Italian,  Servian, 
Polish,  Hungarian. 

South  Side — Polish,  Jewish,  Lithuanian, 
Italian,  mixed  foreign,  Scandinavian,  Ger- 
man, Hungarian,  Croatian,  Greek,  Servian. 

There  are  also  a  number  of  well-defined 
negro  districts  in  the  three  divisions,  the 
colored  people  numbering,  according  to  the 
last  count,  44,103,  of  whom  25,760  are  listed 
as  black,  and  18,343  as  mulatto.  This  is 
almost  as  large  a  colored  population  as 
may  be  found  in  Atlanta,  Georgia,  or  Rich- 
mond, Virginia.  The  negroes  are  numer- 
ically stronger  in  Chicago  than  in  Louis- 
ville, Ky.,  Nashville,  Tenn.,  or  St.  Louis, 
Mo.  No  special  provision  for  them  has 
been  undertaken  by  the  Library,  but  many 
of  them  avail  themselves  of  the  privileges 
at  the  Main  and  a  number  of  the  branch 
libraries.  . 

A  Portfolio  on  Matters  Theatrical 

Interest  in  dramatic  material  as  developed 
in  the  Study  Room  for  Women,  fourth 
floor  of  the  Main  building,  has  suggested 
keeping  on  file  there  a  portfolio  of  current 
comment  on  theatrical  and  dramatic  sub- 
jects. These  are  extracted  from  various 
sources,  including  articles  by  Clayton  Ham- 
ilton, Norman  Hapgood,  Percy  Hammond, 
Mantle  Burns,  James  O'Donnell  Bennett, 
Chas.  H.  Collins,  Frederic  Hatton,  and  ma- 
terial from  English  sources  by  and  about 
Barrie,  Galsworthy,  Shaw  and  Chesterton. 

The  current  bundle  in  the  portfolio  in- 
cludes the  following  articles: 

"The  Peril  of  the  Repertory,"  by  Cecil 
Chisholm.  From  T.  P.'s  Weekly. 

"Silhouetting  a  Scene."  Maude  Adam's 
Experiment  with  photography  in  the  the- 
atre. From  the  Boston  Transcript. 

"Chesterton's  First  Play,"  by  Bernard 
Lintot. 

"Shaw's  Technical  Method  in  the  Shew- 
ing-up  of  Blanco  Posnet."  From  the 
American  Playwright. 

"Revival  of  the  Tyranny  of  Tears"  by 
Desmond  MacCarthy  in  the  New  States- 
man.   • 

A  Branch  for  Pullman 
On  Sunday,  April  5,  the  new  Palmer  Park 
branch  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  was 
opened  to  the  public. 

If  plans  are  realized,  the  new  Stanton 
and  Seward  Park  branches  on  the  North 
Side  will  be  opened  about  May  1.  The 
Sumner  branch  on  the  West  Side  will  prob- 
ably be  ready  about  May  15.

Secretary 


-    -    -    -    Librarian 
Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:80  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  0  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:80  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:80  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


A  Department  of  Music 


Music  lovers  will  welcome  the  announce- 
ment that  the  Board  of  Directors  of  the 
Public  Library  has  ordered  the  installa- 
tion of  a  Department  of  Music  to  comprise 
a  practical  selection  of  orchestral  and  vocal 
scores  and  of  the  standard  compositions  and 
classical  collections  for  various  instruments. 
The  subject  was  presented  at  the  meeting 
of  April  13th  by  Director  Oscar  G.  Mayer 
in  the  following  resolutions,  which  were 
unanimously  adopted,  and  which  are  re- 
printed here  because  of  the  interesting  and 
comprehensive  statement  of  the  plan,  and 
of  local  musical  conditions  and  needs,  con- 
tained in  the  preamble: 

"The  importance  of  the  public  interest 
in  music  has  grown  with  such  rapidity 
during-  the  past  generation  that  its  special 
needs  are  demanding  attention  from  the 
public  libraries.  Everybody  knows  some- 
thing of  music  as  an  art,  but  music  as  a 
business  has  recently  begun  to  force  con- 
sideration from  commercial  bodies  be- 
cause they  have  discovered  that  its  vol- 
ume represents  too  large  a  sum  to  be 
ignored.  Several  investigations  have  been 
made  of  the  subject  by  different  agencies, 


including  the  Commercial  Association  of 
Chicago,  and  though  some  important  fac- 
tors are  lacking,  through  the  impossibility 
of  gathering  definite  statistics,  yet  enough 
has  been  found  so  that  all  are  in  substan- 
tial agreement  as  to  the  sum  total:  that  is 
more  than  thirty  millions  each  year  in  the 
City  of  Chicago. 

"As  a  manufacturing  center  of  musical 
instruments  Chicago  is  the  second  city 
in  the  country,  producing  each  year  one 
hundred  thousand  pianos,  to  the  value 
of  $14,000,000. 

"The  people  of  Chicago  spend  each 
year  for  instruction  in  music  something 
like  $2,000,000;  for  the  purchase  of  the 
printed  notes,  $1,500,000 ;  for  the  opera,  sym- 
phony concerts,  recitals  of  artists  and 
choral  societies,  $1,000,000.  Instruments 
of  all  kinds,  pianos,  violins,  talking  ma- 
chines, etc.,  are  purchased  by  our  people 
for  their  own  use  to  the  amount  of  about 
$10,000,000. 

"The  standard  classics  of  the  literature 
of  music  have  been  published  in  such 
form  as  has  made  the  price  moderate. 
The  classic  literature  of  the  piano  from 
Bach  to  Brahms,  including  all  the  im- 


78 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


portant  masters,  Handel,  Haydn,  Mozart, 
Beethoven,  Schubert,  Schumann,  Chopin, 
Liszt  and  Grieg  would  not  cost  above 
$250.00.  A  casual  list  was  made  for  piano 
solo,  piano  arrangements  of  four  hands, 
of  overtures  and  symphonies,  piano  and 
violin  and  piano  with  other  instruments — an 
excellent  practical  music  library,  which 
amounted  to  more  than  six  hundred 
volumes,  and  would  cost  but  $300.00. 

"The  value  of  such  a  library  to  the 
music  lovers  can  be  judged  from  the 
manner  in  which  they  have  availed  them- 
selves of  the  opera  scores  which  are 
now  at  their  command. 

"An  excellent  library  of  the  song 
classics  would  not  cost  more  than  a  hun- 
dred dollars,  and  the  standard  oratorios 
would  amount  to  about  as  much  more. 

"For  $2,000.00  a  music  selection  of  the 
utmost  practical  value  could  be  pur- 
chased, and  this  would  include  a  large 
number  of  full  orchestral  scores  of  some 
of  the  most  important  works  in  the  art. 
To  the  Library  this  would  be  relatively  a 
small  sum,  but  it  would  give  to  the  student 
an  opportunity  of  the  greatest  impor- 
tance. It  may  be  doubted  whether  there 
be  any  way  in  which  the  Library  by  the 


expenditure  of  such  a  sum  of  money 
could  perform  a  vital  service  for  so  large 
a  part  of  the  community. 

"Resolved,  That  the  Committee  on  Li- 
brary be,  and  the  same  is  hereby  re- 
quested to  use  such  portion  of  the 
Beecher  fund  as  was  set  aside  for  book 
purchases  in  the  January  apportionment 
for  the  nucleus  of  a  collection  of  books 
and  material  on  music,  embracing  operas, 
orchestral  scores,  piano  scores  and  kin- 
dred printed  material. 

"Resolved  Further,  That  such  unappro- 
priated balance  as  remains  in  the  Beecher 
income  fund  be  added  to  the  amount 
above  contemplated. 

"Resolved  Further,  That  in  making  ini- 
tial selection  the   Committee  on  Library 
be   requested  to   call   into   counsel   such 
representative  musicians  and  music  lovers 
as   in   their  judgment  will  best  promote 
the  purpose  of  these  resolutions." 
A  favorable   report  on  these  resolutions 
by  the  Committee  on  Library  at  the  next 
meeting,  April  27th,  led  to  the  action  creat- 
ing the   music  library  and  making  an  ap- 
propriation therefor.     Steps  will  be   taken 
in   the   near   future   to   carry   out   the  pro- 
visions of  this  order. 


Library  Books  by  Parcel  Post 


By  authorization  of  the  Board  of  Direc- 
tors the  Chicago  Public  Library  has  insti- 
tuted a  parcel  post  delivery  available  to 
any  patron  desirous  of  having  books  sent 
directly  to  his  home.  The  conditions  ap- 
plicable to  this  system  have  been  made  as 
simple  as  possible,  with  due  regard  to  effi- 
ciency and  the  proper  care  of  the  books. 
They  may  be  briefly  stated  as  follows: 

Any  registered  borrower,  upon  deposit- 
ing one  dollar  to  cover  the  cost  of  mailing 
books,  may  participate  in  this  service,  sub- 
ject to  the  general  rules  of  the  Library 
governing  book  loans.  A  receipt  card  will 
be  furnished,  and  the  various  charges  for 
postage  will  be  punched  on  this  card  so 
that  it  will  at  all  times  show  the  balance  to 
the  credit  of  the  borrower. 

Loan  period  (two  weeks)  begins  on  date 
of  mailing,  not  date  of  arrival  at  destina- 
tion. In  like  manner  loan  period  ends  on 
date  of  return  mailing,  said  date  in  case  of 
doubt  to  be  determined  by  the  postmark. 
Fines  for  over-due  books  will  be  assessed 
in  accordance  with  this  rule. 

In  all  cases  of  doubt  or  dispute  the  Library 
records  must  be  accepted  as  accurate  and 
conclusive  evidence. 

Responsibility  for  losses  or  damages  inci- 
dental to  transportation  must  be  borne  by 
the  borrower. 

Shipment  of  books  will  be  made  only 
when  the  card  accompanies  the  order.  One 


renewal  for  additional  two  weeks  is  per- 
mitted. When  renewal  is  desired,  books 
need  not  be  sent  in,  but  borrower's  card 
must  be  mailed  for  restamping. 

Books  returned  by  parcel  post  must  be 
securely  wrapped  and  tied,  but  not  sealed. 
Damages  or  excess  postage  charges  caused 
by  careless  wrapping  will  be  assessed 
against  the  sender.  Sender's  name  and  ad- 
dress preceded  by  the  word  "From"  must 
appear  on  the  wrapper. 

No  book  will  be  mailed  from  the  Library 
unless  balance  remaining  on  deposit  is  suffi- 
cient to  cover  all  charges.  When  credit 
balance  falls  below  ten  cents,  borrower  will 
be  notified,  to  permit  prompt  remittance 
for  renewal  of  deposit.  Postage  for  corre- 
spondence relating  to  parcel  post  ship- 
ments will  be  charged  against  deposit. 

The  borrowers  are  requested  to  state  on 
their  orders  the  limit  of  time  in  which 
books  will  be  of  use,  in  case  it  is  not  pos- 
sible to  send  them  promptly  because  in 
circulation  at  the  time  of  request.  Books 
may  be  sent  from  or  to  Library  by  parcel 
post  at  the  following  rates: 

Weight                          Local  rate  Zone  rate 

1  Ib $0.05  $0.05 

2  Ibs 06  .06 

3  Ibs 06  .07 

4  Ibs 07  .08 

5  Ibs.                                       .07  .09

How  the  Library  Serves  the  Public 


V.  Department  of  Arts  and  Crafts 
On  the  fifth  floor  of  the  Central  build- 
ing, Chicago  Public  Library,  is  located  a 
department  which,  for  want  of  a  more  de- 
scriptive term,  is  called  the  Art  Room. 
Here  between  covers  of  leather  or  of  buck- 
ram may  be  found  a  wealth  of  graphic 
material  dealing  with  the  fine  arts,  with  his- 
toric ornaments,  industrial  design  and  the 
related  topics  that  make  an  appeal  to  the 
art  student,  the  architect,  the  craftsman,  the 
designer  and  the  average  citizen  who  is 
interested  in  such  subjects  as  book-making, 
old  time  furniture,  jewelry,  rugs,  the  de- 
velopment of  painting  and  sculpture,  the 
theatre  and  the  arts  and  letters  in  their 
varied  specialized  departments.  The  books 
range  in  size  from  fat  little  duodecimos  to 
stately  elephant  folios,  and  there  are  also 
many  hundreds  of  portfolios  containing  in  de- 
tached form  reproductions  from  the  master- 
pieces of  the  world  and  modern  adaptations  rep- 
resenting all  the  fine  arts  and  the  art  crafts. 
It  is  a  varied  collection  as  well  as  a  valua- 
ble one,  for  here  are  grouped  most  of  the 
rare  and  costly  works  which  the  Library 
possesses.  A  bequest  of  ten  thousand  dol- 
lars from  the  Rev.  Dr.  W.  H.  Ryder  has 
enabled  the  Library  to  acquire  these  fine 
examples  of  book-making  and  rare  treas- 
ures in  graphic  form  by  applying  the  in- 
come derived  annually  from  the  investment 
of  this  fund  towards  the  purchase  of  books 
for  this  special  department.  There  has 
been  built  up  in  consequence  a  collection 
which  is  particularly  strong  in  the  lines 
of  industrial  design  and  ornament,  and 
which  is  used  constantly  by  students,  archi- 
tects and  craftsmen  who  realize  the  com- 
mercial possibilities  contained  therein. 
During  the  past  year  a  thousand  persons  a 
month  made  use  of  the  material  in  this  de- 
partment, some  of  them  spending  many 
hours  in  tracing  designs,  in  copying  from 
the  reproductions  or  in  noting  suggestions 
applicable  to  their  own  work.  A  total  of 
44,173  volumes  was  issued  to  these  appli- 
cants during  the  past  year.  In  connec- 
tion with  the  Art  Room,  accommodations 
have  been  provided  for  those  who  desire  to 
use  cameras  to  copy  pictures  or,  as  is  fre- 
quently done,  to  secure  facsimiles  of  printed 
pages  of  some  of  the  rare  works  in  the 
Library. 

While  the  current  numbers  of  the  lead- 
ing magazines  and  illustrated  periodicals 
dealing  with  art  subjects  are  kept  on  file, 
the  main  body  of  the  material  is  to  be  found  in 
the  large  collection  of  books  which  are 
available  upon  request,  subject  to  such  con- 
ditions as  will  safeguard  the  use  of  rare 
and  costly  volumes  such  as  these.  A  few 
of  the  subjects,  broadly  grouped,  which 


find  representation  are  listed  below: 

FINB  AKTS 

Painting  Ornament 

Sculpture  Architecture 

Engraving  Mural  painting 

Etching  Book  illustration 

Drawing  Portraits 

Caricatures  Art  galleries 

Cartoons  Art    anatomy 

Miniatures  Theatre 

Music  Dancing 

Opera  scores  Antiquities 

Numismatics  Archaeology 

ART  CRAFTS 

Goldsmithing  Palaeography 

Silversmithing  Lithography 

Jewelry  Printing 

Ivory  carving  Color  printing 

Wood  carving  Alphabets 

Metal  work  Monograms 

Textiles  Costume 

Rugs  Leather  work 

Tapestry  Gems 

Needlework  Glass  painting 

Lace  China  painting 

House   decoration  Pottery 

Furniture  Enamelling 

Wall   decoration  Mosaics 

Illuminated      manu- 
scripts 

One  of  the  most  interesting  sections  of 
the  department  is  that  which  deals  with 
the  development  and  progress  of  book-mak- 
ing from  the  manuscript  era  to  the  pres- 
ent day.  There  are  facsimiles  exquisitely 
reproduced  in  color  of  some  of  the  famous 
manuscripts  of  the  world,  including  Queen 
Anne's  Prayer  Book,  and  the  Grimani 
Breviary.  There  is  also  a  facsimile  of  the 
Gutenberg  Bible  with  its  remarkable  orna- 
mentation showing  the  exact  appearance  of 
the  first  book  printed  from  movable  types 
nearly  five-hundred  years  ago. 

Many  business  houses  have  availed  them- 
selves of  the  possibilities  of  the  material 
to  be  found  here  to  secure  suggestions  for 
window  dressing,  for  advertising,  for  manu- 
facturing novelties  and  otherwise.  Lecturers 
have  also  found  the  material  useful  for  their 
purposes,  many  thousands  of  slides  having  been 
reproduced  from  the  illustrations  selected  by 
them.  The  practical  application  of  this  ma- 
terial to  the  use  of  business  men  may  be  gath- 
ered from  the  following  brief  list  noted 
within  a  recent  period  by  the  custodians  of 
the  room. 

Designers  of  Fashionable  Clothing — Cos- 
tumes of  the  18th  century. 

Furniture  Manufacturers — Color  schemes 
for  interiors:  stained  glass  designs. 

Another  Furniture  Manufacturer — Chip- 
pendale, Heppelwhite  and  Sheraton  designs 
of  furniture. 


80 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Several  Retail  Stores — Suggestions  for 
window  trimming. 

Theatre  Scene  Painters — Designs  for 
backgrounds,  landscape,  and  interior  views. 

Author  of  a  Book  on  the  Bible — Four 
hundred  photographs  made  from  hundreds 
of  books  illustrating  text. 

Art  Connoisseur — Studies  of  Japanese 
prints,  allegorical  designs  and  Beardsley 
sketches. 

Dress  Form  Co. — Copy  of  frame  such  as 
was  used  on  old  miniature  portraits. 

Silversmith — Jewelry  designs  of  the  16th 
and  17th  centuries. 

Excellently  lighted,  commodious  and 
quiet,  this  room  is  in  every  detail  admirably 
adapted  to  its  functions,  and  forms  one  of 
the  most  interesting  departments  of  the 
Library. 

Many   Questions,  and  a  Few  Answers 

From  many  hundreds  of  questions  which 
pour  regularly  into  the  Reference  Depart- 
ment of  the  Library,  a  selection  is  herewith 
made  of  some  which  call  for  answers  of 
interest  to  others  than  the  individual  querist. 
Owing  to  the  heavy  demands  upon  the 
time  of  the  reference  staff,  it  has  been 
found  necessary  to  restrict  search  for 
elusive  poetical  quotations  to  the  printed 
indexes  and  concordances,  as  otherwise 
hours  of  time  would  have  to  be  expended 
in  tracing  a  couplet  or  quatrain  to  its 
obscure  lair.  Even  when  the  author  is 
known,  it  is  a  formidable  task  to  locate 
the  exact  place  whence  a  certain  line  or 
two  has  been  extracted.  In  the  hope,  how- 
ever, that  some  reader  of  the  Bulletin  may 
identify  the  authorship  or  place  of  fugitive 
rhymes  and  verses,  appeals  for  aid  of  this 
nature  will  be  printed  in  these  columns,  and 
replies  will  be  forwarded  to  inquirers. 
Among  the  orphaned  rhymes  seeking  par- 
entage which  have  been  received  recently 
are  the  following: 
1.  Poem  with  the  refrain: 

The  air  is   full  of  sunshine 
And  the  flag  is  full  of  stars. 
2. 

Who    flings   the   stones    from    out    the 

traveled  road 
Or   feeds   a   starving  creature   not   his 

own, 

His  name  is  whispered  to  the  gods 
above. 

3.  Old  poem  wanted,  first  stanza  of  which 
quoted  from  memory  is  as  follows: 

My  eyes  are  dim,  I  cannot  see, 

I  left  my  specs  at  home. 
I  left  them  above  the  door — 

A  thing  I   never   did   before. 

4.  Source   of   the   following: 

It  is  a  far  call 

From  the  woman  of  savagery,  the  com- 
mon property  of  the  horde — the 
burden-bearer; 


From  the  woman  of  medievalism,  the 

wife,  who  was  made  a  little  better 

than  a  slave; 
From  the  woman,  who,  in  the  sixteenth 

century,  sought  for  recognition  and 

was   refused,   etc. 

The  following  are  a  few  of  the  many 
questions  upon  which  information  was  fur- 
nished in  the  Civics  Room  during  the  last 
two  weeks  of  March: 

A  constitution  of  a  co-operative  organ- 
ization. 

Building  code  of  Chicago. 

Organization  of  the  Board  of  Pardons 
of  Illinois. 

Number  of  lynchings  in  111.  in  1913. 

Election  laws  of  Ind.,  Nev.,  Mont,  and 
New  Hamp. 

Monroe  Doctrine. 

Roosevelt's  message  on  the  Panama 
Canal  in  1906. 

Can  first  cousins  marry  in  Kentucky. 

Lever  bill. 

Montessori   method. 

American    Consul-general    to    Berlin. 

List  of  Indian  agencies  and  reservation 
superintendents. 

Family  entrances  of  saloons  in  relation 
to  juvenile  delinquency. 

What  mayor  of  Chicago  has  served  the 
longest  period  of  time?  Who  has  served 
next  longest? 

Something  about  the  office  of  Public  De- 
fender in  Los  Angeles. 

Municipal  abattoirs. 

Amount  of  money  spent  for  golf  and 
yachting  by  the  idle  rich. 

Trust  legislation. 

Currency  legislation. 

How  many  people  in  the  U.  S.  are  gram- 
mar school  graduates? 

Brazil  and  Argentine  Republic:  (a) 
Population,  (b)  Cattle  statistics,  (c)  Ex- 
ports and  imports. 

A  Gift  from  Mayor  Harrison 

Included  in  a  bundle  of  books  sent  to  the 
Library  by  Mayor  Carter  H.  Harrison  as 
a  gift,  are  two  volumes  of  special  associa- 
tion interest.  On  the  fly-leaf  are  the  auto- 
graphs of  John  Breckinridge,  who  was 
Attorney  General  of  the  United  States  un- 
der President  Jefferson,  and  of  his  son, 
Robert  J.  Breckinridge,  who  was  the 
greatest  Presbyterian  minister  of  the  gos- 
pel Kentucky  has  ever  known. 

Memorial  Day 

The  circulating  department  of  the  Main 
Library  will  be  closed  all  day  on  Memorial 
Day,  Saturday,  May  30th.  The  reference 
and  reading  rooms  will  be  open  from  nine 
until  six  o'clock.  The  branches  will  be 
open  from  two  until  six  p.  m.

Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Henry    E.    Legler Librarian 

Carl    B.    Roden *     Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:!0  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  0  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:80  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:80  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  newt- 
papers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
0  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


How  the  Library  Serves  the  Public 


VI:  Civics  Room,  and  Document  Department 
Millions  of  public  documents  are  printed 
every  year.  Tons  of  them  are  never  taken 
from  their  wrappers  by  the  recipients;  addi- 
tional tons  are  in  storage  in  the  basements 
of  public  buildings  in  Washington,  and  are 
sent  to  the  paper  mills  as  they  accumulate 
beyond  storage  capacity.  Single  copies, 
and  in  some  instances  duplicates,  of  these 
thousands  of  publications,  are  ranged  on 
the  shelves  of  libraries.  It  is  in  these  in- 
stitutions that  the  best  and  the  largest  use 
is  made  of  the  valuable  material  which 
Uncle  Sam  puts  into  print,  and  it  is  here 
that  by  means  of  indexes  and  catalogs,  in- 
formation which  would  otherwise  be  buried 
is  rendered  accessible. 

Today  the  government  of  the  United 
States  is  the  greatest  publisher  in  the 
world.  The  government  printing  office  is 
the  finest  and  best-equipped  in  the  world, 
and  no  nation  approaches  this  country  in 
the  effort  to  enlighten  its  people  through 
the  medium  of  printer's  ink.  Nearly  six 
million  dollars  has  been  appropriated  this 
year  to  maintain  the  government  printing 
office.  At  enormous  additional  cost  for  ex- 
pert service,  the  government  secures  the 


material  for  its  publications  on  all  sorts  of 
subjects.  Some  of  the  publications  are  in 
the  form  of  portly  books,  some  are  bulle- 
tins, some  are  circulars,  and  an  important 
section  comprises  maps.  Vital  discoveries 
and  experiments  of  interest  to  farmers, 
manufacturers,  educators,  and  business  men 
are  included. 

The  Chicago  Public  Library,  being  a 
designated  depository,  receives  the  govern- 
ment publications  as  issued.  The  states 
also  issue  documents,  and  the  important 
ones  on  subjects  concerning  labor,  indus- 
trial interests  and  public  utilities,  the  Li- 
brary makes  special  efforts  to  secure.  This 
is  also  the  case  with  important  municipal 
documents,  dealing  with  a  multiplicity  of 
topics  which  are  the  outgrowth  of  the  com- 
plex problems  now  before  the  large  city 
communities. 

In  addition  to  the  federal,  state,  munici- 
pal and  foreign  documents,  the  Civics 
Room  collections  embrace  a  great  mass  of 
related  material  in  the  form  of  pamphlets, 
magazine  references  and  clippings  from  the 
leading  newspapers  of  the  world.  Of  the 
topics  which  are  well  represented  in  the 
pamphlet  boxes  which  line  the  shelves  on

upon  which  no  books  have  been  written,

go  to  pamphlets,  mayor's  messages,  news-

recently:

During  the   past  year,   the   patrons  who 

used  the  Civics  Room  numbered  50,016. 
Their  demands  embraced  the  following  ma- 
terial: City,  'business  and  special  direc- 
tories consulted,  20,832;  magazines  on  edu- 
cation, economics  and  sociology  issued, 
19,231;  books  on  these  subjects  issued,  16,- 
415;  pamphlets  issued,  110,850.  No  count 
was  kept  of  the  clippings  used,  but  very 
considerable  use  was  made  of  them. 


One   Question  Answered 

A  post  card  received  from  an  interested 
reader  of  the  Book  Bulletin  gives  the  an- 
swer to  one  of  the  questions  listed  in  the 
May  issue,  namely  the  origin  of  the  line: 

"The  air  is  full  of  sunshine  and  the  flag 
is  full  of  stars." 

This  occurs  in  Dr.  Henry  Van  Dyke's 
poem,  "America  for  Me,"  to  be  found  in 
his  "Poems,"  collected  edition,  p.  167.  The 
Library  call  number  of  this  book  is  E  11643. 


This  is  the  last  issue  of  the  Book  Bulletin 
until  September,  publication  being  sus- 
pended during  July  and  August.  The  next 
number  will  appear  about  September  10th. 


Two  legal  holidays  occurring  before  that 
time  give  rise  to  the  following  announce- 
ment: 

The  Library  will  'be  closed  in  all  of  its 
departments  and  branches  on  Independ- 
ence Day,  Saturday,  July  4th. 

On  Labor  Day,  Monday,  Sept.  7th,  the 
reference  and  reading  rooms  will  be  open 
from  nine  a.  m.  until  six  p.  m.,  the  circu- 
lating department  being  closed.  The 
branches  will  be  open  from  two  until  six 
p.  m.  on  that  day. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


95 


Vacation  and  Summer  Cards 
Beginning  June  15th,  and  available  until 
September  1st,  the  Chicago  Public  Library 
will  issue  a  Vacation  Card  to  any  regis- 
tered reader  who  makes  personal  applica- 
tion therefor  at  the  Main  Library  or  at  any 
of  the  twenty-six  branches  maintained  by 
the  Library  Board.  The  privileges  repre- 
sented by  this  card,  which  is  intended  pri- 
marily for  readers  who  expect  to  spend  a 
prolonged  period  on  vacation  trips  outside 
the  city,  entitle  the  holder  to  the  use  of  ten 
books  at  one  time  (not  more  than  half  of 
which  may  be  novels),  for  such  time  prior 
to  the  expiration  of  the  limit  as  may  be  re- 
quired. No  renewal  is  necessary  within 
such  period,  but  the  books  must  be  returned 
before  the  final  limit  set  by  the  regulations 
covering  their  use. 

In  order  that  residents  of  Chicago  who 
are  unable  to  leave  their  daily  work  for  the 
country,  or  otherwise  find  it  impossible  or 
inconvenient  to  spend  their  vacations  else- 
where, may  enjoy  to  the  fullest  extent  the 
resources  of  the  Library  with  the  least  in- 
convenience in  the  procurement  of  the 
books  from  the  Main  Library  or  its 
branches,  arrangements  have  been  made  for 
the  first  time  to  issue  to  those  who  apply 
in  person  for  such  privilege  the  use  of  what 
is  to  be  known  as  a  Summer  Card.  On  this 
card  there  may  be  drawn  at  one  time  not  to 
exceed  five  books  in  any  department  of 
literature  whether  fiction  or  non-fiction  and 
these  may  be  retained  one  full  calendar 
month  without  the  customary  renewal  fea- 
ture after  having  had  the  book  two  weeks. 
As  this  new  feature  of  ready  use  of  books 
with  the  least  attachment  of  red  tape  prom- 
ises to  be  popular,  and  as  the  consequent 
use  of  these  cards  will  probably  be  consid- 
erable, a  special  attendant  will  be  provided 
at  the  Information  Desk  to  respond  to  such 
demands  as  may  come  from  this  source. 
The  books  obtained  on  this  card,  inasmuch 
as  they  may  be  retained  the  full  calendar 
month,  may  not  be  renewed  and  in  justice 
to  other  patrons  seven-day  books  will  not 
be  given  out  on  the  Summer  Card. 

These  special  privileges  are  limited  to  the 
less  busy  seasons  in  the  Library,  as  it  would 
not  be  possible  to  extend  the  duration  of 
the  period  to  the  winter  months  for  lack 
of  a  sufficient  number  of  books  to  meet 
the  demand  at  that  time.  The  largest  cir- 
culation, as  shown  by  an  experience  of 
forty  years,  is  recorded  during  the  months 
of  December,  January,  February  and  March, 
while  less  reading  appears  to  be  done  dur- 
ing July  and  August  than  at  any  other 
season  of  the  year. 


During  July  and  August  all  of  the  branch 
libraries  will  remain  closed  all  day  on  Sun- 
days. 


Some    Questions    Answered 

1.  A    list    of    expositions   in    the    United 
States  held  since  the  Centennial,  or  planned 
for  the  near  future,  is  requested. 

Centennial  International  Exhibition,  Phila- 
delphia, 1876. 

World's  Columbian  Exposition,  Chicago, 
1893. 

California  Mid-Winter  Exhibition,  San 
Francisco,  1894. 

Cotton  States  and  Industrial  Exposition, 
Atlanta,  1895. 

Tennessee  Centennial  Exposition,  Nash- 
ville, Tenn.,  1897. 

Trans-Mississippi  Exposition,  Omaha, 
Neb.,  1898. 

Pan-American  Exposition,  Buffalo,  1901. 

South  Carolina  Inter-State  and  West 
Indian  Exposition,  Charleston,  1901-2. 

Louisiana  Purchase  Exposition,  St.  Louis, 
1904. 

Lewis  &  Clark  Exposition,  Portland, 
Oregon,  1905. 

Jamestown  Ter-centennial  Exposition, 
Hampton  Roads,  1907. 

Alaska- Yukon-Pacific  Exposition,  Seattle, 
1909. 

Panama-Pacific  International  Exposition, 
San  Francisco,  1915. 

Panama-California  Exposition,  San  Diego, 
Cal.,  1915. 

2.  "Who  was  the  'clubfooted  man'  spoken 
of  in  the  Clansman  by  Thos.  Dixon,  Jr.?" 

The  character  in  the  book,  Austin  Stone- 
man,  is  drawn  from  Thaddeus  Stevens, 
member  of  the  House  of  Representatives 
from  1861-68. 

3.  "Who    are    the    three   greatest   women 
novelists  of  today,  (American  or  otherwise)  f" 

Perhaps  most  critics  of  authority  would 
agree  on  the  following  names:  Mrs.  Hum- 
phrey Ward,  Mrs.  Edith  Wharton,  Selma 
Lagerlof. 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  receives  a 
large  number  of  requests  for  information 
by  mail  from  correspondents  who  seek  data 
on  questions  for  debate,  or  who  want  eval- 
uated lists  of  books  on  a  great  variety  of 
topics.  Staff  members  in  the  Reference  de- 
partment are  glad  to  assist  all  inquirers 
who  come  to  the  Library  to  secure  such 
data,  but  cannot  undertake  research  work 
by  correspondence.  It  would  require  the 
services  of  a  staff  many  times  that  which 
the  Library  now  has  to  respond  even  in 
a  modest  way  to  such  demands.  To  do  so 
with  present  equipment  would  be  at  the 
expense  of  the  reference  workers  who  make 
calls  in  person  for  aid  in  research  work.
