SEPTEMBER,  1914. 


No.  7. 


Board  of  Directors 

Antonio  Lagorio,  President  -  -  812  Dearborn  Ave. 
Max  Henius,  Vice-President  -  1135  Fullerton  Ave. 
Philip  M.  Ksycki  ....  53  W.  Washington  St. 

Samuel    Despres 224    S.    Market    St. 

Charles    C.    Breyer       ....  1545    W.    Division    St. 

James    I.    Healy 80   N.    Dearborn  St. 

Frank  J.  Pokorney  -  -  -  1601  S.  Crawford  Ave. 
T.  Frank  O'Connell  -  -  -  138  W.  Washington  St. 
Oscar  G.  Mayer 1241  Sedgwick  St. 

Harry    G.    Wilson Secretary 

Henry    E.    Legler Librarian 

Carl   B.    Roden Assistant   Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  Is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


Notes 


The  Board  of  Directors  has  authorized 
the  opening  of  a  Foreign  Room  which  will 
make  possible  the  installation  of  a  collec- 
tion of  25,000  volumes  in  the  various  conti- 
nental languages  for  open  access  and  con- 
venient examination,  similar  to  the  much- 
appreciated  Open  Shelf  section  on  the  De- 
livery Floor.  Unfortunately,  the  space  avail- 
able will  not  be  sufficient  for  the  entire  for- 
eign section  of  the  Library,  which  now 
numbers  some  80,000  volumes,  but  a  repre- 
sentative collection  in  each  language,  the 
classics  and  standards,  together  with  the 
latest  accessions,  will  be  placed  in  the  new 
room,  which  is  located  on  the  fourth  floor 
on  the  west  side  of  the  south  corridor  ad- 
joining the  Thomas  Hughes  Room  for 
Young  People.  Further  announcement  will 
be  made  when  the  room  is  ready  for  open- 
ing, which  will  be  as  soon  as  the  necessary 
shelves  and  furniture  are  received. 


During  the  summer  large  collections  of 
the  newest  books  in  German,  French,  Rus- 
sian and  Polish  have  been  received  and 
these  are  being  prepared  for  circulation  as 
rapidly  as  possible.  Order  lists  were  also 
in  preparation  for  similar  purchases  of  Bo- 
hemian and  Yiddish  books,  but  the  receipt 
of  these  will  be  seriously  delayed,  if  not 
indefinitely  postponed  by  the  unsettled  con- 
ditions in  Europe.  All  importations  of 
books  have  practically  stopped,  for  the 
present,  and  only  a  few  of  the  European 
newspapers  and  periodicals  are  being  re- 
ceived, and  these  only  irregularly  and  after 
much  delay.  Among  other  things,  this 
state  of  affairs  will  cause  the  postponement 
of  the  issue  of  a  revised  finding  list  of  Ger- 
man books,  which  was  in  progress,  but 
which  cannot  be  finished  until  numerous 
additional  volumes  can  be  secured  to  fill 
gaps,  to  replace  worn-out  and  obsolete 
works,  and  to  bring  the  collection  com- 
pletely up  to  date. 


110 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


It  will  be  necessary,  also — and  mainly  for 
the  above  mentioned  reasons — to  defer  the 
opening  of  the  Music  Room  for  at  least 
one  month  from  September  first,  the  date 
originally  fixed.  Only  about  one-half  of  the 
splendid  and  comprehensive  list  selected  by 
the  musical  critics  of  three  local  newspapers 
could  be  secured  in  this  country,  and  not 
all  of  that  portion  has  yet  been  received. 


The  cataloging  and  binding  of  the  volumes 
at  hand  is  going  forward  with  all  possible 
speed  and,  if  it  transpires  that  the  orders 
placed  in  Europe  cannot  be  filled  within  a 
reasonable  time,  the  room  will  be  opened 
with  the  part  of  the  collection  now  avail- 
able, the  outstanding  portion  being  added 
as  received. 


The  European  War 

Below  is  a  brief  list  of  books  on  some  phases  of  the  European  situation,  which 
are  offered  as  suggestions  to  the  student  and  general  reader.  The  present  list  is  largely 
composed  of  works  on  the  general  aspects  of  war  and  peace,  and  of  European  inter- 
national relations  and  politics.  Only  a  few  of  the  newer  books  descriptive  of  life  and 
conditions  in  the  several  countries  involved  have  been  included.  The  Library  is,  of 
course,  amply  equipped  with  the  literature  of  travel  and  description  and  patrons  are 
referred  to  the  finding  lists  and  to  the  card  catalogs  for  a  complete  survey  of  this 
class.  Efforts  have  been  made  to  prepare  for  the  suddenly  increased  interest  in  these 
subjects  by  the  addition  of  many  duplicate  copies  in  the  main  library  as  well  as  in  all 
of  the  branches,  but  these  have  been  only  partially  successful  because  the  stocks  of  the 
booksellers  themselves  have  been  quickly  exhausted  under  the  heavy  and  unanticipated 
demands  of  the  general  public,  and  library  orders  have  in  some  cases  been  returned  un- 
filled. The  indulgence  and  consideration  of  readers  must  therefore  be  invoked  and  it 
is  for  this  reason  that  attention  is  called  to  the  catalogs,  with  the  aid  of  which,  it  is 
hoped,  they  will  be  able  to  find  material  which,  if  not  their  first  choice,  will  still  prove 
useful  and  adequate.

Department* 


anb  information  for  $atron* 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be  once 
renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be  re- 
newed but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

p.  ai.,  those  marked  j  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  9

days and  holidays  open  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Assistant   Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  Is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:80  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


A  Budget  of  Facts  and  Figures 


Floating  in  oceans  of  printer's  ink,  the 
reader  may  encounter  many  facts  and  fig- 
ures of  value  and  interest  that  would  prove 
of  permanent  worth  if  they  were  indexed. 
For  want  of  indexes,  they  are  difficult  to 
find  when  wanted.  To  supply  this  want,  in 
part,  a  card  file  has  been  begun  in  the  Civics 
Room  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  con- 
taining in  brief  compass  a  variety  of  infor- 
mation on  all  sorts  of  subjects  in  form  that 
can  be  consulted  readily. 

The  "Facts  and  Figures  Index"  was  sug- 
gested by  experience  growing  out  of  de- 
mands for  out-of-the-way  information  not 
readily  procurable  from  usual  sources,  and 
the  finding  of  which  has  in  some  instances 
involved  hours  of  search.  Some  sample 
questions  chosen  at  random  from  a  week's 
list  of  inquiries  will  serve  to  indicate  the 
difficulty  of  quickly  supplying  answers  from 
the  standard  reference  books: 

1.  How  much  food  the  nations  at  war  con- 
sume and  how  much  the  United  States 
uses  of  its  own  crops  and  how  much  it 
sends  to  warring  nations. 

2.  Socialism  in  America. 

3.  Rise  of  real  estate  values. 


4.  Preparation  of  peanuts  for  market. 

5.  Number    of   buildings    put   up    in    large 
cities  in  1913. 

6.  What  states  have  the  two-cent  railway 
fare? 

7.  What   are   the    regulations   for   packing 
motion  picture  films  for  shipping? 

8.  Number    of    deaths    from    fire    in    the 
United  States. 

9.  Number  of  deaths  from  alcohol. 
10.  Parent-teacher  associations. 

The  material  in  the  "Facts  and  Figures 
Index"  is  kept  on  cards  5x8  in  size,  indexed 
by  means  of  suitable  subject  headings.  On 
these  cards  are  pasted  clippings  and  ex- 
tracts derived  from  many  sources — news- 
papers, magazines,  pamphlets  and  othjer 
printed  ephemera.  In  all  cases  there  are 
added  source  of  clipping  and  date  of  pub- 
lication, with  notation  of  location  where  ad- 
ditional facts  may  be  found  if  the  card  space 
is  insufficient  to  contain  the  entire  article 
or  extract.  Something  of  the  range  em- 
braced may  be  gathered  from  the  few  sam- 
ples here  given  by  title: 

City  expenditures  for  police,  fire  depart- 
ment, health,  schools,  etc.,  embracing  Lon- 


134 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


don,  Paris,  Berlin,  Vienna,  Glasgow,  New 
York,  Chicago,  Philadelphia,  St.  Louis  and 
Boston. 

War  strength  of  battling  nations. 

Importation  of  eggs  into  Great  Britain. 

The  Japanese  navy  since  the  war  with 
Russia. 

Specimen  city  blocks  having  a  population 
of  1,000  or  more  persons. 

Financial  power  of  nations  drawn  into  the 
European  war. 

Figures  showing  the  standing  of  Chicago 
as  a  dry  goods  center. 

Annual  destruction  by  rats. 

Development  of  the  railways  and  growth 
of  the  country's  wealth  from  1850  to  1910. 

Annual  expenditures  on  the  German  army 
and  German  navy  from  1901  to  1910. 

United  Kingdom,  Germany,  and  France 
migration  deduced  from  census  and  vital 
statistics. 

Express  rates  for  packages  weighing  100 
pounds. 

Cost  of  American  storage  reservoirs. 

Cost  of  rehabilitating  the  Illinois  and 
Michigan  canal  between  Joliet  and  La  Salle. 

Where  the  money  comes  from  in  support 
of  the  federal  government. 

Fire  losses  at  home  and  abroad. 

Some  figures  as  to  world  railway  mileage. 

World's  great  ship  canals,  their  dimen- 
sions and  cost. 

Women  elected  to  office  in  Illinois. 

Farm  buildings  in  various  countries. 

Growth  of  the  no-license  territory. 

Bank  figures  on  which  Secretary  McAdoo 
based  choice  of  locations  for  federal  re- 
serve banks. 

Royal  persons  assassinated  since  1800. 

Marine  disasters  of  recent  years. 

Chicago's  sky-scraper  record  for  the  year 
1914. 

Rank  of  Illinois  in  the  traction  industry, 
with  comparisons. 

Ten  years'  development  in  public  utilities 
— electric  railways,  light  and  power,  munici- 
pal electric  stations,  telephones,  gas  plants. 

Daddy  Dearborn's  dollar,  and  how  he 
spends  it. 

Chicago's  proximity  to  center  of  food 
supply:  corn,  wheat,  butter,  milk,  potato, 
live  stock. 

Nationalities  that  go  to  make  up  Chi- 
cago's labor  market. 

Facts  about  railway  transportation  and 
water  transportation  in  Chicago. 

Restriction  of  building  heights  in  cities  of 
the  United  States. 

Expenditures  per  family  in  699  normal 
families  in  the  state  of  Illinois. 

Number  of  trespassers  killed  and  injured 
on  the  railroads  of  this  country  during  the 
last  twenty  years. 

School  savings  banks  in  the  Chicago  pub- 
lic schools. 


Cost  of  the  country's  wasted  eggs. 
Roman   Catholics  and   Protestants  in  the 
counties  of  Ulster.

War  Literature. 

A  few  of  the  more  interesting  of  the  pam- 
phlets dealing  with  questions  arising  out  of 
the  present  war  in  Europe  have  been  pre- 
pared for  circulation  and  placed  on  the 
Open  Shelf  desk  where  they  will  be  issued 
for  home  use.  Among  them  are  copies  of 
the  famous  "white  papers"  presenting  the 
cases  of  England  and  Germany,  respec- 
tively. 

Everyman's  Library. 

Attention  is  called  to  the  new  volumes 
added  to  the  collection  entitled  Everyman's 
Library  in  the  Open  Shelf  Department. 
This  excellent  and  popular  series  now  num- 
bers over  six  hundred  volumes  and  com- 
prises a  selection  of  the  best  in  the  litera- 
tures of  all  ages  and  tongues.  A  list  of  the 
additions  is  printed  at  p.  144.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Book*  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  are  in  the 
Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Eugenics 

Eugenics:  twelve  university  lectures,  by 
Morton  A.  Aldrich  and  others.  1914. 

L  11592 

"The  lectures  contained  in  this  volume  were  se- 
lected from  among  a  number  given  in  various 
universities  and  colleges  throughout  the  country 
in  the  scholastic  year  of  1912-1913. 

Contents:  The  eugenics  programme  and  prog- 
ress in  its  achievement,  by  C.  B.  Davenport.— 
Eugenics  as  viewed  by  the  zoologist,  by  K.  tt. 
Wolcott. — Eugenics  from  the  point  of  view  of  tne 
physician,  by  V.  C.  Vaughan.— Eugenics  as  viewed 
Ey  the  physiologist,  by  W.  H.  Howell.— Eugenics: 
its  data,  scope  and  promise,  as  seen  by  the  anato- 
mist, by  H.  E.  Jordan.— Eugenics  from  the  point 
of  view  of  the  geneticist,  by  H.  J.  Webber.— The 
first  law  of  character-making,  by  A.  Holmes.— The 
eugenics  movement  from  the  standpoint  of  sociol-

Chicago  public  library,  tfticfngan  jatoenue  anb  Bto*fnngton  Street 

JBepartment* 


anb  Information  (or  patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be  once 
renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be  re- 
newed but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  book*  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  mutt  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian. 


BRANCHES.

p.  01.,  those  marked  t  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  0 
p.  m.,  all  others  are  open  from  1  to  9  p.  m.  Sun- 
days and  holidays  open  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Secretary 


Henry     E.     Legler Librarian 

Carl    B.    Roden Assistant   Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  Is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday.

Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday.

open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room  and  Foreign  Room,  fourth  floor,  Wash- 
ington street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 


The  Music  Room 


The  Music  Room  is  now  open  to  the 
public.  It  is  situated  in  the  southwest  cor- 
ner of  the  fourth  floor  of  the  Main  Library 
building,  adjoining  the  Thomas  Hughes 
Room  for  young  people.  It  is  equipped 
with  suitable  shelving  and  tables,  and  at 
present  contains  about  2,100  bound  volumes 
of  music  scores  and  about  one  thousand 
pieces  of  sheet  music  reinforced  and  sewed 
in  flexible  covers.  All  of  this  music  is  avail- 
able for  home  circulation  on  practically 
the  same  conditions  as  govern  the  issue 
of  books  from  other  departments,  with  the 
following  variations:  A  special  music  card 
will  be  issued  on  demand  to  every  card- 
holder of  record,  and  every  applicant  for 
library  cards  is  entitled  to  the  music  card 
in  addition  to  the  fiction  and  non-fiction 
cards  »  >w  issued.  Music  cards  will,  how- 
ever, be  issued  in  the  Music  Room,  not  at 
the  Registration  Desk,  and  patrons  will  be 
required  to  show  their  book  cards  when 
applying  for  the  special  card  in  the  Music 
Room.  The  room  is  open  from  9  a.  m.  to 
8:30  p.  m. 

One  bound  volume  of  music  will  be   is- 
sued  on   each   music   card   for  a  period   of 


four  weeks,  but  without  renewal.  The  usual 
fine  of  three  cents  a  day  will  be  charged 
for  failure  to  return  within  this  period. 

Sheet  music  will  be  issued  at  the  rate 
of  five  pieces  on  each  music  card,  and  for 
two  weeks  only,  without  renewal.  A  fine  of 
one  cent  a  day  will  be  charged  for  each 
piece  overdue.  Sheet  music  and  bound 
volumes  will  not  be  issued  at  the  same  time 
on  the  same  card. 

It  is  a  pleasure  to  record  here  the  names 
of  the  three  eminent  musical  critics  to 
whose  expert  knowledge  and  active  co-op- 
eration the  music  lovers  of  Chicago  are  in- 
debted for  the  excellent  collection  which 
has  been  assembled.  They  are: 

Felix  Borowsky,  Esq.,  of  the  Chicago 
Herald. 

Karleton  Hackett,  Esq.,  of  the  Chicago 
Evening  Post. 

Glenn  Dillard  Gunn,  Esq. 

When  the  Music  Room  was  first  planned, 
last  spring,  it  was  decided  to  invite  the 
assistance  of  these  representative  musicians 
in  selecting  the  works  to  be  included.  They 
responded  to  the  invitation  with  enthusiasm 
and  entered  upon  the  task  with  the  great- 


154 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


est  interest.  After  several  conferences  in 
which  the  general  scope  of  a  music  library 
for  popular  use  was  definitely  fixed,  lists 
were  compiled  by  each  of  these  three  gen- 
tlemen independently,  and  these  were 
brought  together,  copied  and  tabulated  and 
out  of  them  grew  the  official  order  list,  the 
purchase  of  which  was  authorized  by  the 
Board  of  Directors  in  June.  All  of  the 
titles  recommended  were  ordered  pur- 
chased, but  only  that  part  of  the  entire  or- 
der procurable  in  this  country  has  been 
received,  since  conditions  in  Europe  have 
prevented  the  importation  of  those  works 
which  had  to  be  ordered  abroad.  Thus  the 
present  collection  comprises  about  three- 
fourths  of  the  whole  recommended  list  and 
some  serious  gaps  may  still  be  noticed, 
which  are  attributable  to  the  circumstances 
mentioned.  The  splendid  judgment  and 
taste  of  the  selectors  will,  however,  be 
clearly  apparent  to  patrons  who  examine 
the  array  of  volumes  now  on  the  shelves, 
and  the  missing  portion  will  be  added 
promptly  upon  its  arrival. 

The  plan  as  outlined  and  carried  into  ef- 
fect contemplated  a  home  collection  of  mu- 
sic, adapted  to  the  skill  and  proficiency  of 
the  average  performer.  The  largest  space 
has  been  given  to  the  literature  of  the  more 
commonly  used  instruments:  piano,  violin, 
violoncello,  and  the  like.  Music  for  groups 
of  instruments,  "chamber  music,"  from 
duets  to  octets  and  even  nonets,  in  va- 
rious instrumental  combinations,  form  a 
large  division.  A  selection  of  the  standard 
concertos,  for  study  purposes,  will  also  be 
found.  Full  orchestral  scores  and  works 
involving  a  degree  of  virtuosity  required 
only  in  professional  artists  have,  for  the 
present,  at  any  rate,  been  omitted.  A  full 
set  of  "miniature  scores"  of  symphonies 
and  other  orchestral  music  will  be  added 
as  soon  as  possible,  to  enable  music  stu- 
dents to  follow  the  rendition  of  these  com- 
positions at  the  symphony  concerts.  At 
this  time  only  a  very  few  have  been  re- 
ceived. Organ  music  is  not  yet  adequately 
represented  but  will  be  supplied  in  suffi- 
cient variety  in  the  near  future. 

In  the  vocal  section  a  large  and  varied 
collection  of  oratorios,  cantatas,  choruses 
and  part  songs,  both  sacred  and  secular, 
claims  attention,  while  the  works  of  the 
great  composers  for  solo  voice  have  been 
included  in  large  numbers.  Of  operas  the 
Library  already  had  a  fair  representation 
of  those  included  in  the  current  repertories, 
and  these  have  been  transferred  to  the 
shelves  of  the  Music  Room. 

The  volumes  have  been  arranged  on  open 
shelves  for  examination  and  selection, 
classified  according  to  a  logical  and  orderly 
system,  with  plenty  of  shelf  labels  and 
other  guides  provided  to  aid  the  visitor  in 


finding  what  he  may  seek.  A  complete  card 
cataloge  has  been  provided  in  which  each 
work  is  entered  both  under  the  name  of 
the  composer  and  under  the  class  to  which 
it  belongs.  As  soon  as  the  collection  is 
completed  by  the  arrival  of  the  volumes 
still  outstanding,  a  printed  hand-list  will 
be  issued.  For  the  present  this  is  not  feas- 
ible, and  the  following  conspectus  or  table 
of  contents  is  given  in  the  hope  that  it 
may  serve  to  give  patrons  of  the  Music 
Room  a  view  of  the  scope  and  contents 
of  the  collection  as  planned,  and  to  indicate 
in  some  measure  the  skill,  care,  experience 
and  wisdom  employed  in  its  organization. 
List  of  works  ordered  for  the  Music  Room; 
some  not  yet  received:

as  no  new  titles  have  been  added  to  the  collec- 
tion already  in  the  Library,  which  is  cata- 
logued in  the  Book  Bulletin  for  November, 


The  Foreign  Room 

In  order  that  readers  of  books  in  foreign 
languages  might  have  the  same  privileges 
of  inspection  and  examination  as  those  now 
enjoyed  by  patrons  of  the  Open  Shelves,  a 
series  of  alcoves  has  been  set  aside  for  the 
accommodation  of  groups  of  books  in  the 
various  continental  European  tongues  to 
the  number  of  some  15,000  volumes.  The 
limits  of  the  space  available  make  it  impos- 
sible to  transfer  the  entire  foreign  collec- 
tion to  the  new  quarters,  but  a  division  has 
been  made  by  placing  the  most  recent 
works  on  the  new  shelves  in  what  will  be 
known  as  the  Foreign  Room,  and  leaving 
the  older  works  in  their  former  location  in 
the  stacks  where  they  may  be  called  for 
as  before.  Thus,  about  one-fifth  of  all  the 
German  books,  comprising  the  accessions 
of  the  past  ten  years,  have  been  sent  to  the 
new  room,  and  relatively  similar  allotments 
of  French,  Bohemian,  Polish  and  Scandina- 
vian books  have  been  included.  In  other 
languages,  with  smaller  collections,  it  has 
been  possible  to  find  room  for  all  the  books. 
In  this  latter  division  will  be  found  the 
books  in  Italian,  Spanish,  Russian,  Yid- 


dish,   Lettish,    Lithuanian    and    Hungarian 
and  Dutch. 

The  Foreign  Room  is  situated  on  the 
fourth  floor,  southwest  corner  of  the  main 
building  and  immediately  adjacent  to  the 
Music  Room. 

War  News 

Mayor  Harrison  is  receiving,  at  frequent 
intervals,  large  bundles  of  German  daily 
newspapers  which  are  forwarded  by  the 
mayor  of  Berlin  with  the  request  that  they 
be  made  accessible  to  the  general  public. 
These  papers  have  therefore  been  placed  on 
file  in  the  reading  room  of  the  Public  Li- 
brary, where  they  may  be  freely  consulted. 

The  Library  is  also  receiving  numerous 
pamphlets,  broadsides  and  other  publica- 
tions from  officials  and  organizations  in  the 
several  countries  now  at  war,  embodying 
statements  and  arguments  regarding  their 
respective  contentions  and  their  views  of 
the  issues  involved.  These  are  not  only 
important  and  valuable  as  documentary 
contributions  to  the  literature  of  a  great 
historic  event,  but  are  interesting  indica- 
tions of  the  importance  placed  by  the  na- 
tions themselves  upon  the  current  of  Amer- 
ican public  opinion.  These  publications 
may  be  seen  in  the  Civics  Department. 


Library  Story  Telling 
In  fourteen  branches  of  the  Public  Li- 
brary weekly  story  hours  were  held  for 
children  during  the  past  year,  covering 
periods  of  from  eight  to  forty-nine  weeks. 
A  total  of  30,453  children  attended  these 
story  hours,  the  average  attendance  varying 
from  twenty  to  one  hundred  and  ninety- 
nine  at  one  session.  Classes  of  stories 
told  include:  animal  stories,  fairy  tales, 
stories  of  noted  people,  myths,  historical 
stories,  holiday  stories  (i.  e.,  the  events  com- 
memorated by  the  various  national  holi- 
days) and  stories  from  the  Iliad,  Odyssey 
and  other  classic  sources. 


Thanksgiving  Day 

The  circulating  department  of  the  Li- 
brary will  be  closed  on  Thanksgiving  Day, 
Thursday,  November  26th.  The  reference 
and  reading  rooms  will  be  open  from  9:00 
a.  m.  to  6:00  p.  m.  The  hours  in  the 
branches  on  that  day  will  be  from  two  to 
six  p.  m. 


The  branch  formerly  located  at  23rd 
street  and  Millard  avenue,  in  the  old  Bur- 
lington station,  and  known  as  Lawndale 
Branch,  has  been  transferred  to  more  suit- 
able and  commodious  quarters  at  3527-29 
W.  12th  street,  and  the  name  changed  to 
Douglas  Park  Branch.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  are  in  the 
Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

•     -     -     -     Librarian 
Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room  and  Foreign  Room,  fourth  floor,  Wash- 
ington street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 


Package  Libraries  for  the  Study  of  Civics 


In  cooperation  with  a  movement  fostered 
by  the  Association  of  Commerce  for  the 
study  of  civics  in  the  high  schools,  and 
similarly  furthered  by  the  Civics  Extension 
Committee  in  the  upper  grades  of  the  ele- 
mentary schools,  the  Chicago  Public  Li- 
brary has  undertaken,  as  rapidly  as  the  ma- 
terial can  be  obtained,  to  provide  package 
libraries  for  use  by  those  engaged  in  the 
work.  These  will  consist  principally  of 
pamphlet  material,  magazine  separates, 
brochures  issued  by  clubs  and  other  or- 
ganizations, documentary  publications  is- 
sued by  the  federal,  state  and  municipal 
governments,  and  other  related  material  in 
printed  and  typewritten  form,  each  package 
library  dealing  with  one  subject  or  main 
subdivision  of  a  subject,  selected  or  passed 
upon  by  a  recognized  authority.  Large 
envelopes,  expansive  in  design  to  accommo- 
date a  considerable  collection  of  pamphlets 
of  ordinary  size,  will  be  used  to  contain 
groups  of  related  material,  and  the  package 
libraries  will  be  subject  to  call  from  school 
principals  and  social  students  and  workers 
for  periods  varying  according  to  need.  If 
sent  to  schools,  they  will  be  retained  there 


for  reference  by  the  pupils  during  the  term 
when  civics  is  being  taught. 

On  some  of  the  subjects,  material  is 
scarce  and  difficult  to  get.  When  possible, 
the  package  libraries  will  be  duplicated,  so 
that  a  number  of  high  schools  or  eighth 
grades  may  be  served  at  the  same  time 
along  parallel  lines. 

It  is  expected  that  a  number  of  the  pack- 
age libraries  will  be  ready  for  circulation 
about  the  middle  of  January.  Some  of  the 
topics  on  which  material  is  now  being  gath- 
ered and  grouped,  include  the  following: 

Public  recreation  (playgrounds,  bathing 
beaches,  dance  halls). 

Chicago  beautiful  plans. 

Housing  conditions  in  Chicago. 

Woman  suffrage. 

Commission  form  of  government  and  city 
manager  plan. 

Initiative  and  referendum. 

Child  welfare. 

Each  package  library  will  be  provided 
with  a  select  bibliography  of  book  material 
on  the  subject  to  be  found  in  the  library, 
call  numbers  of  the  books  being  noted  for 
each  title,  as  a  matter  of  convenience. 


170 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Plans  are  also  under  way  whereby  similar 
aids  may  be  provided  for  the  study  of  Chi- 
cago— its  beginnings,  its  industries,  its  pub- 
lic institutions,  and  its  history.    Tentatively, 
the  following  outline  has  been  formulated, 
based  largely  on  the  suggestive  topics  se- 
lected for  study  by  Principal  Kent  of  the 
Eugene    Field    School: 
The   Glacial   Period. 
The  Three   Stages  of  "Lake   Chicago." 
That  Which  Underlies   Chicago. 
The  French  and  English  in  the  Chicago 

Region. 

The  Northwest  Territory. 
The  Indians  and  the  Chicago  Region. 
Fort   Dearborn,   The   Massacre,   War    of 

1812,   etc. 

Illinois  as  a  Territory  and  State, — Capi- 
tals, etc. 
Chicago  in  Historical  Steps, — Annexation, 

etc. 

The  Illinois-Michigan  Canal. 
The  "Fight  for  Life  in  Chicago." 
The  Sanitary  District  and  Canal. 
The    Chief   Lines   of   Industry: 
Steel  and  Iron. 

Manufacture  of  Agricultural  Machinery. 
The    Packing   Industry. 
Elevator,     Milling,    etc.    (The     Empty 

Boat). 
Electrical        Goods        (Hydro-Electric 

Power). 

The    Great    Chicago    Fire. 
The  World's  Columbian  Exposition. 
Parks  and  Playgrounds. 
Homes  and  Housing. 
The  Chicago  City  Government. 
The  Courts  and  Their  Work. 
Cook  County, — Naming,  Size,  Area,  Rela- 
tion to  Chicago,  etc. 
The  Plan  of  "Chicago  Beautiful." 
Educational  System  and  Institutions: 
Illinois  Institutions. 
Cook  County   Institutions. 
Chicago   Institutions: 
Museums,  Libraries,  Hospitals,  Private 
Schools,  Universities,  Social  Centers, 
Settlements,  Historical  Bodies.  Civic 
Organizations,    Philanthropic   Organ- 
izations. 

In  a  current  issue  of  Chicago  Commerce, 
the  official  organ  of  the  Association  of  Com- 
merce, the  scope  of  the  new  movement  is 
thus  summarized: 

"To  organize  branch  Civic-Industrial 
committees  in  the  high  schools  of  Chicago 
leading  to  a  first-hand  study  by  students  _of 
the  civic-industrial  problems  of  the  city 
and  real  work  in  making  a  portion  of  Chi- 
cago a  better  place  in  which  to  live  and  in 
which  to  labor — this  is  a  new  and  interest- 
ing undertaking  of  the  Civic-Industrial 
committee  of  the  association." 

"The  plan  provides  that  each  High  School 
Civic-Industrial  club  shall  acquire  intimate 


and  accurate  knowledge  of  Chicago,  its  in- 
dustries and  government;  of  the  various 
businesses  and  professions  appertaining 
thereto;  and  of  the  manufacturing,  trans- 
portation and  other  business  organizations 
that  are  associated  with  the  industries  of 
the  city." 

"It  is  planned  to  have  such  information 
disseminated  through  talks  by  prominent 
business  and  professional  men;  through  in- 
dustrial motion  pictures;  and  through  occa- 
sional excursions  to  typical  Chicago  indus- 
tries." 

"Each  club  is  to  lay  out  a  program  of 
civic  and  industrial  work  which  shall  en- 
gage its  attention  for  one  year  and  upon 
which  an  annual  report  shall  be  made.  The 
reports  from  various  schools  are  to  be  com- 
pared, given  publicity  when  they  deserve  it, 
and  possibly  judged  by  competent  individ- 
uals in  order  that  a  dignified  trophy  may 
be  awarded  to  the  school  carrying  out  the 
best  piece  of  constructive  work." 

"All  the  activities  of  the  clubs  are  to  har- 
monize with  the  ideas  of  the  Civic-Indus- 
trial committee,  in  order  that  definite  prog- 
ress may  be  made." 

"Before  plans  were  completed,  confer- 
ences occurred  between  the  committee,  Mrs. 
Ella  Flagg  Young,  and  principals  from  the 
various  high  schools,  with  the  result  that 
participating  schools  have  taken  up  the 
work  with  much  enthusiasm." 

"In  no  sense  does  the  Civic-Industrial 
committee  intend  to  dictate  to  the  clubs. 
Each  club  will  be  assisted  in  its  organiza- 
tion, helped  in  planning  its  program,  then 
'turned  loose'  on  the  job." 

"Twelve  high  schools  have  already  en- 
listed in  the  work  and  it  is  expected  that 
clubs  will  shortly  be  in  operation  in  each 
of  the  institutions  listed  below: 

Lane  Technical. 

Marshall. 

Wendell  Phillips. 

Nicholas  Senn. 

Lake. 

Harrison  Technical. 

Hyde  Park. 

Tuley. 

George  William  Curtis. 

Crane  Technical. 

Parker. 

Austin. 

Holiday  Library  Hours 

All  departments  and  branches  of  the  Li- 
brary will  be  closed  on  Christmas  Day, 
Friday,  Dec.  25th. 

On  New  Year's  Day  the  usual  holiday 
schedule  will  be  observed,  the  reference  and 
reading  rooms  in  the  main  building  being 
open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  and  the 
branches  from  2  to  6  p.  m. 

On  Christmas  Eve.,  all  branches  will 
close  at  5  :30  p.  m.

E  11321 

"The  original  of  this  tale  is  in  demotic  Egyp- 
tian, in  prose,  on  a  fragmentary  papyrus  dated 
'the  first  month  of  winter,  in  the  fifteenth  year' 
of  some  king  unnamed.  Palaecgraphical  evidence 
suggests  some  date  about  100  B.  C.  I  know  the 
tale  only  from  the  literal  translation  which  ac- 
companies the  text  in  Dr.  Griffith's  Stories  of  the 
high  priests  of  Memphis."

Branches  marked  *  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  10 
p.  .n.,  those  marked  f  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  9 
p.  m.,  all  others  are  open  from  1  to  9  p.  m.  Sun- 
days and  holidays  open  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


Volume  5. 


JANUARY,  1915. 


No.  1. 


Board  of  Directors 

Antonio  Lagorio,  President  -  -  813  Dearborn  Ave. 
Max  Henius,  Vice-President  -  1185  Fullerton  Are. 
Philip  M.  Ksycki  ....  68  W.  Washington  St 

Samuel    Despres 224    S.    Market    St. 

Charles   C.    Breyer      ....  1545   W.    Division    St. 

James    J.    Healy 80   N.    Dearborn  St. 

Frank  J.  Pokorney  -  -  -  1501  S.  Crawford  Ave. 
T.  Frank  O'Connell  -  -  -  138  W.  Washington  St. 
Oscar  G.  Mayer 1241  Sedgwick  St 


Harry    G.    Wilson 


Secretary 


Henry    E.     Legler Librarian 

Carl   B.    Roden Assistant   Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:80  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room  and  Foreign  Room,  fourth  floor,  Wash- 
ington street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 


Urgent  Needs  of  the  Library 


Finding  that  the  present  revenues  for 
library  purposes  are  insufficient  to  pro- 
vide the  necessary  number  of  branches, 
to  purchase  the  required  number  of 
books  and  to  maintain  adequately  all 
the  departments  of  the  Main  Library, 
the  Board  of  Directors  will  ask  the 
Legislature  for  such  relief  as  will  enable 
the  strengthening  and  legitimate  ex- 
pansion of  the  service.  A  folder  has 
been  issued  in  an  edition  of  10,000 
copies,  summarizing  in  condensed 
form  the  situation  which  faces  the 
Library  administration  and  showing 
by  comparative  tables  the  relatively 
meager  resources  at  command.  From 
this  brochure,  the  following  facts  are 
taken : 

With  a  population  of  approximately 
2,400,000  and  an  area  of  192  square 
miles  to  serve,  the  Chicago  Public  Li- 


brary has  the  comparatively  small  sum 
of  $370,000,  for  all  purposes,  an  amount 
which  cities  one-fourth  the  area  and 
one-fourth  the  population  receive. 

In  five  years  the  Library's  work  has 
increased  96  per  cent  and  its  revenues 
less  than  15  per  cent. 

The  Library  owns  only  three  branch 
buildings  and  possesses  only  400,000 
books  for  home  circulation. 

Fully  one-third  of  Chicago's  popula- 
tion remains  unprovided  with  branch 
library  facilities. 

With  the  small  amount  of  money 
available  and  its  inadequate  supply  of 
books,  the  Library  manages  thirty-two 
branches,  and  the  home  circulation  to- 
tals 4,000,000  issues. 

On  an  average  20,000  persons  use  the 
Library  system  every  day  of  the  year. 

These  results  have  been  made  pos- 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


sible  through  the  highest  efficiency  in 
all  departments  and  by  using  all  special 
funds,  which  are  now  exhausted. 

If  relief  is  not  forthcoming  the  Li- 
brary will  be  forced  to  close  a  number 
of  its  branches  and  several  of  its  depart- 
ments. 

It  is  estimated  that  the  inhabitants  of 
Chicago  now  number  2,300,000.  With 
its  present  equipment  and  facilities  the 
Library  can  serve  not  more  than  10  per 
cent  of  them.  The  maximum  has  prac- 
tically been  reached  at  this  time : 

Borrowers  registered    160,000 

Borrowers  in  schools 30,000 

Borrowers  at  deposit  stations . . .  25,000 
Borrowers  in  industrial  branches  20,000 


Total 235,000 

The  remaining  2,065,000  persons  who 
live  in  Chicago  may  be  divided  into  the 
following  groups  of  non-users : 

Under  6  years  of  age 320,000 

Illiterate 80,000 

No  time  to  read 300,000 

No  desire  or  opportunity  to  read.500,000 
No  convenient  library  facilities. 865,000 

The  latter  item  represents  the  field 
which  the  Library,  to  carry  out  its 
larger  purposes  and  fullest  measure  of 
usefulness,  must  develop  as  soon  as 
means  are  provided  for  the  purpose. 

Chicago  has  the  lowest  per  capita 
cost  for  library  purposes  of  the  ten 
largest  cities,  and  ranks  twenty-sixth 
in  a  list  of  the  twenty-eight  cities  pos- 
sessing a  population  of  100,000  or  more. 
The  figures  for  the  ten  largest  cities 
follow : 

Per  Capita  Expenditures  for  Library 
Purposes. 

Pittsburgh 53  cents 

Boston 51 

Cleveland 45 

Cincinnati 40 

New  York 33 

Buffalo   32 

St.  Louis  .  .  31 


Baltimore 16     " 

Philadelphia 15      " 

Chicago 15      " 

Although  the  book  collection  is  third 
largest  of  the  public  libraries  of  the 
United  States,  in  proportion  to  num- 
ber of  inhabitants  Chicago  has  fewer 
volumes  than  any  one  of  the  ten  li- 
braries located  in  the  ten  largest  cities : 

Books  Per  One  Hundred  Inhabitants. 

Boston    148 

Cleveland    82 

Pittsburgh    72 

Buffalo    71 

Baltimore   54 

St.   Louis    51 

Detroit    44 

New  York   42 

Philadelphia    28 

Chicago    24 

With  a  total  of  400,000  volumes 
available  for  circulation,  there  were 
4,000,000  issues  during  the  past  year, 
an  average  issue  of  ten  times  for  each 
volume.  To  supply  present  demands, 
the  Library  should  have  a  million  vol- 
umes for  circulation. 

In  the  last  five  years,  the  Library's 
share  of  city  taxes  collectible  has  de- 
creased from  1.42  per  cent  to  1.26  per 
cent.  On  the  other  hand  the  Library's 
work  has  increased  nearly  100  per  cent 
during  the  same  period,  the  increase  in 
home  circulation  of  books  showing  a 
gain  of  95.7  per  cent. 

The  rank  of  the  Chicago  Public  Li- 
brary among  the  libraries  of  the  ten 
major  cities  is  as  follows : 

Chicago's   Library   Rank  Among  Ten 
Largest  Cities  in  the  United  States. 

Population    2nd 

Home  use  of  books 2nd 

Number  of  users 2nd 

Number  of  volumes 3rd 

Per  capita  cost 10th 

Number  of  volumes  per  inhabitant.  10th 
Number  of  branch  buildings  owned. 10th

Ayscough,  J.     Gracechurch.     1913.     F 10760 
Gracechurch  is  the  name  of  a  little  English  vil-

tions of  the  author,  a  Catholic  clergyman. 

Benson,  R.  H.     Oddsfish!     1914.        F 11066 

A  vivid  and  stirring  historical  novel  of  England 
during  the  "popish  plot."  The  late  Mgr.  Benson's 
last  and  best  story  of  English  Catholics.

A   tale   of   sordid   American    "practical    politics."

Royal  Happiness.  1914.  F 12152 

A  royal  English  prince,  son  of  "King  John," 
visits  America,  falls  in  love  with  a  maiden  named 
Hilary  and  emerges  out  of  many  complications  into

Coulevain,  P.  de,  pseud.  The  wonderful  ro- 
mance. 1914.  F 12167

F  12250 

Story,  laid  in  Alaska  and  Canada,  concerns  the 
fortunes  of  a  "wheat  king,"  his  long-lost  son,  and 
the  boy's  foster-mother.  Not  equal  to  some  of  this 
author  s  former  work. 

England,  G.  A.     Darkness  and  dawn.     1914. 

F 12735 

A  thrilling,  sensational  story  with  a  sociological

distant  future  when  New  York  is  a  crumbling 
ruin,  and  found  a  new  civilization  with  the  half- 
human  savages  inhabiting  it.

Chicago  public  Hibrarp,  iHtrJjtgan  abenue  anb  H)asijmcr.tcm  Street 

Departments! 


anil  information  for  patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be  once 
renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be  re- 
newed but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  Is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourtk 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:80  p.  m.. 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room  and  Foreign  Room,  fourth  floor,  Wash- 
ington street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 


Story  Telling  in  the  Branches 


During  the  year  1914  "story  hours" 
were  held  weekly  in  fifteen  branches 
and  one  school.  In  the  remaining  sev- 
enteen branches  it  was  not  possible  to 
conduct  story  telling,  because  there  are 
no  rooms  there  available  for  the  pur- 
pose without  disturbing  library  users. 
The  total  attendance  was  18,515  chil- 
dren. 

The  stories  told  related  to  holidays, 
birthdays  of  noted  people,  fairy  tales, 
Norse  myths,  stories  of  King  Arthur, 
Beowulf,  Robin  Hood,  Ulysses,  Ro- 
land, Siegfried,  Fairy  Queene,  and  he- 
roes of  European  countries. 

Through  the  Chicago  Woman's 
Club,  whose  Library  Committee  has 
worked  in  splendid  cooperation  with 
the  Library  authorities,  courses  in 
story  telling  and  children's  reading 
have  been  given  by  Mrs.  Gudrun 


Thorne-Thomsen  and  Miss  Georgene 
Faulkner  to  the  members  of  the  Li- 
brary staff  who  are  working  with  chil- 
dren. As  a  result  of  this  training,  the 
number  of  story  hours  and  of  story- 
tellers has  greatly  increased  in  the  last 
few  years,  and  the  interest  in  the 
knowledge  of  children's  literature  has 
been  greatly  augmented  among  mem- 
bers of  the  staff. 

Story  telling  in  libraries  is  done  for 
the  purpose  of  helping  children  to  se- 
lect good  books  to  read.  In  a  busy 
children's  room  it  is  not  possible  to  tell 
each  child  enough  about  a  book  to 
make  him  desire  to  read  that  book. 
Therefore  it  is  a  necessary  economy  of 
time  to  tell  large  groups  of  children 
about  the  stories  in  good  books.  Sto- 
ries are  not  told  which  children  would 
ordinarily  read  of  their  own  accord,  but 


18 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


such  as  will  introduce  them  to  the  lit- 
erature of  all  ages,  that  will  mean 
something  to  them  all  their  lives  long. 

The  story-teller  easily  wins  the  chil- 
dren's confidence  because  the  children 
recognize  that  she  loves  the  same  he- 
roes, and  the  tales  that  they  love. 
With  confidence,  she  is  able  as  no  one 
else,  to  guide  their  reading  from  day 
to  day  as  they  come  to  the  library. 
For  this  reason,  it  has  been  deemed  ad- 
visable to  use  members  of  the  staff  for 
story-telling  as  far  as  possible,  instead 
of  people  not  connected  with  the  li- 
brary. 

Many  mothers  have  asked  the  Li- 
brary for  assistance  in  choosing  the 
best  stories  to  be  told  or  read  to  their 
children.  For  the  aid  of  those  who 
wish  suggestions,  the  following  list  of 
books  has  been  compiled.  The  groups 
are  arranged  in  the  order  in  which 
they  will  interest  the  children.

Total  attendance  at  all  story  hours, 
1914,  18,515. 


The  ideal  place  for  story-telling 
(when  conditions  are  right)  is  the 
home.  Miss  Frances  Jenkins  Olcott, 
in  her  book  on  children's  reading, 
says :  "Companionship  of  parents  and 
children,  and  mutual  enjoyment  of 
books  are  the  foundation  of  success- 
ful guiding  of  children's  reading.  *  *  * 
At  story-telling  time  a  child's  mind  is 
open  to  the  deepest  impressions.  His 
emotions  may  be  swayed  towards  good 
or  bad.  His  imagination  is  active, 
making  a  succession  of  mental  pic- 
tures. Through  story-telling,  he  may 
be  taught  the  difference  between  right 
and  wrong,  and  his  mind  may  be 
stocked  with  beautiful  images,  and  as 
soon  as  he  can  read,  he  may  be  given 
the  books  that  contain  the  stories  told, 
and  be  encouraged  to  read  for  him- 
self." 


Two  Legal  Holidays  in  February. 

On  Lincoln's  birthday,  Friday,  Feb.  12th, 
and  on  Monday,  Feb.  22d,  Washington's 
birthday,  the  circulating  department  of  the 
Main  Library  will  be  closed  and  the  refer- 
ence and  reading  rooms  will  be  open  from 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

The  branches  will  be  open  from  2  to 
6  p.  m.

Couperus,  L.  M.  A.  Small  souls;  tr.  by 
A.  Teixeira  de  Mattos.  1914.  F 12211 

"The  first  of  a  aeries  of  four  novels  describing 
the  fortunes  of  the  Van  Lowe  family  and  known 
in  Holland  by  the  generic  title  of  The  books  of 
the  small  souls." — Translator's  note. 

A  fine  and  skilful  piece  of  work.  The  best  novel 
of  1914. 

Dix.  B.  M.     Maid  Melicent.     1914.     F 12502 
A   pleasing   tale   of   love    and   adventure    in   the 
days  of  Puritans  and  Cavalier  in  England." 

Forman,  J.  M.  The  blind  spot;  a  novel 
1914.  F 12996 

An  interesting  story  of  a  most  excellent  man 
whose  ideal  is  "efficiency"  and  who  abhors  waste, 
whether  of  wealth,  energy  or — sympathy.  His  ef- 
forts to  uplift  society  along  these  lines  reveal  bis 
"blind  spot" — a  want  of  love  and  fellow-feeling 
for  humanity. 

Furman,  L.  Sight  to  the  blind;  a  story. 
1914.  F 13002 

A  short  story  of  social  work  among  Kentucky 
mountaineers. 

Ghosal,  Mrs.   S.   S.   K.   D.     An   unfinished 

song.     1914.  F 13230

Party  of  Bengal."     By  the  sister  of  Rabindranath 

Tagore. 

Harben,  W.  N.    The  New  Clarion;  a  novel. 

1914.  F 13511 

A  good  Harben  story  in   his  favorite  setting  of 

rural   Georgia.     The   "New   Clarion"   is  a  country 

newspaper  edited  by  the  hero. 

Herzog,  R.  Sons  of  the  Rhine  (Die  Wis- 
kottens).  1914.  F 13729 

How  'the  business"  founded  by  the  father  shapes 
the  lives  and  careers  of  the  sons.  A  translation 
from  the  German  of  a  very  successful  story  by  one

ite writers.

F 16152

Jacob  Riis'  last  book;  true  tales  of  the  New

Roberts,  M.    Time  and  Thomas  Waring;  a 
study  of  a  man.     1914.  F  17376 

Morley  Roberts,  though  not  a  "popular"  author, 
is  held  by  many  to  be  one  of  the  most  consummate 
literary  artists  among  living  English  writers.  The 
present  story  relates,  with  keen  psychological  anal- 
ysis, the  complete  change  in  moral  outlook  and 
philosophy  of  a  man  after  undergoing  a  dangerous 
surgical  operation. 

Stacpoole,  H.  de  V.    The  presentation.  1914. 

F  18351 

A  clever  historical  novel  with  plenty  of  plot, 
based  upon  the  presentation  of  Madame  Du  Barry 
at  the  French  court.

curve  and  other  stories.  1914.  F  18496 
Military  tales  first  published  some  years  ago 
without  attracting  much  attention,  but  now  re- 
garded as  striking  and  unusual.  By  the  famous 
"Eyewitness"  with  the  British  army  in  France.

F 18696

Turnbull,  M.     Looking  after  Sandy;  a  sim- 
ple romance.     1914.  F 18692 
A  wholesome,   entertaining  story   of  a  little  girl 
waif   adopted   by   a   big-hearted   family   with   whom 
she  grows  up  to  success  and  happiness. 

VacheU,  H.  A.  Quinneys'.  1914.  F 18732 
Quinney's  is  an  antique  shop,  the  former  owner 
of  which  made  a  fortune  out  of  "faking"  antiques. 
His  son's  struggle  to  keep  an  honest  shop,  his 
love  story  and  that  of  his  daughter,  make  up  a  de- 
lightful tale  of  a  quiet  sort.

anli  information  for 


Street 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be  once 
renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be  re- 
newed but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  It  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

days and  holidays  open  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  Is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  8  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday.

Made  in  Chicago 


That  Chicago  is  the  center  of  indus- 
trial and  technical  periodical  publications 
is  an  interesting  fact  brought  out  by  the 
exhibit  of  "made  in  Chicago"  magazines 
arranged  by  the  Chicago  Library  Club  at 
its  recent  meeting.  Weeklies  and  month- 
lies devoted  to  all  branches  of  industry, 
business,  commerce,  manufactures,  agri- 
culture, stockb reeding,  gardening  and 
many  other  subjects  were  brought  to- 
gether to  the  number  of  326.  Literary 
magazines  made  up  the  smallest  group 
in  the  total;  sociology,  economics  and 
kindred  sciences  were  represented  by  a 
few  titles,  mainly  publications  of  the 
universities ;  theology,  religion,  including 
a  large  number  of  denominational  papers, 
made  rather  a  better  showing.  The  fine 
arts  were  not  entirely  unrepresented  and 
political,  neighborhood  and  social  publi- 
cations comprised  a  more  numerous 


class.  Much  the  largest  portion  of  the 
exhibit,  however,  was  made  up  of  publi- 
cations devoted  to  the  trades  and  prac- 
tical arts,  and  it  is  not  unlikely  that  the 
number  of  these  might  be  added  to  by 
further  inquiry  and  search,  so  that  a 
final  total  of  500  periodical  publications 
emanating  from  Chicago  is  not  beyond 
possibility. 

The  Chicago  Library  Club  is  a  profes- 
sional organization  of  persons  connected 
with  the  various  libraries  of  the  city  and 
vicinity,  and  to  them  the  exhibit  proved 
both  instructive  and  astonishing.  It  was 
generally  felt  that  the  material  assembled 
should  be  preserved  and  the  club  has, 
therefore,  deposited  the  periodicals  in 
this  Library.  Here  it  will  have  to  remain 
as  a  deposit,  merely,  until  space  and  time 
can  be  found — probably  during  the  less 
crowded  months  of  summer — when  the 


38 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


exhibit  will  be  reorganized  and  displayed 
for  the  inspection  of  the  general  public. 
At  that  time  a  complete  list  of  the  period- 
icals included  will  be  issued,  which 
should  make  an  interesting  and  gratify- 
ing contribution  to  the  literary  history  of 
Chicago. 

Package  Libraries  on  Local  Topics 

Sixty  of  the  topics  listed  for  study  of 
local  history,  civics  and  industries  are 
comprised  in  the  series  of  package 
libraries  which  are  now  available.  They 
are  the  following,  call  numbers  being 
given  for  convenience  of  teachers  and 
others  desiring  to  use  them:  Packages 
on  other  related  subjects  can  be  as- 
sembled, so  far  as  material  is  available, 
but  requests  for  such  packages  should 
be  made  as  far  as  possible  in  advance  of 
the  time  when  they  are  to  be  used.

Special  Lists 

Two  special  lists  are  included  in  this 
number  of  the  Book  Bulletin,  one  giving 
a  list  of  interesting  public  documents, 
national,  state,  and  city.  This  list  is  in- 
tended merely  to  indicate  the  great 
variety  of  subjects  dealt  with  by  govern- 
ment agencies,  some  of  which  publish 
results  of  first-hand  investigations  whose 
importance  are  not  surpassed  by  any 
private  enterprise.  It  should  be  noted, 
also,  that  most  of  the  official  publications 
are  at  the  disposal  of  all  citizens  and  can 
be  purchased  a.t  cost  or  obtained  free 
from  the  respective  issuing  offices. 

The  second  list  is  a  short  and  care-

fully  made  selection  of  books  on  busi-

Business   English  and  Business  Correspondence 

A  selected  list  of  recent  books  on  the  ^  essentials  of  English  grammar,  spelling,  com- 
position, publication,  etc.,  with  special  application  to  modern  business  practice  and  busi- 
ness correspondence. 

Altmaier,  Carl  L.  Commercial  correspond- 
ence and  postal  information.  1912.  L  8340 

Baker,  Josephine  Turck.  Correct  business 
letter  writing  and  business  English.  1911. 

L 11992 


Special  features  are  a  complete  alphabetical  list 
of  abbreviations  and  a  list  of  compound  words 
with  rules  on  the  use  of  the  hyphen. 

Banks,  Eleonora.  Correct  business  and 
legal  forms.  A  reference  manual  for 
stenographers,  secretaries  and  reporters. 
1912.  L  8389 

Besides  brief  information  on  the  usual  points 
of  business  English,  this  book  includes  correct 
forms  for  court  documents  and  legal  papers,  with 
explanations  of  law  terms  and  foreign  phrases. 

Barrett,  Charles  R.  Business  English  and 
correspondence;  a  practical  treatise  on 
the  methods  by  which  expert  correspond- 
ents produce  clear  and  forceful  letters  to 
meet  modern  business  requirements. 
1914.  J  4653 

Belding,  Albert  G.  Commercial  corre- 
spondence. 1905.  L  8376 

Buhlig,    Rose.       Business    English.      1914. 

J3244 

To  a  very  full  treatment  of  the  subjects  usually 
covered  in  such  works,  this  practical  and  up-to- 
date  text  book  adds  a  section  on  the  underlying 
principles  of  manufacture,  distribution,  advertising, 
real  estate,  insurance  and  banking. 

Business  correspondence.    1913.       L  8231, 2 

Some  of  the  most  interesting  articles  are  on 
"The  business-getting  form  letter,"  "Correspond- 
ence as  an  adjunct  to  the  salesman,"  "Wholesal- 
ing by  mail,"  "Interhouse  and  interdepartment 
correspondence,"  "Follow-up  letters,"  "Filing,"  and 
"A  system  for  handling  correspondence." 

Fart  of  the  "Business  man's  library"  published 
by  the  System  company.  Library  has  five  copies.

J4497 

Lists  and  corrects  errors  frequently  made  in 
points  of  grammar,  punctuation,  spelling  and  use 
of  words. 

English  for  business  uses  and  commer- 
cial correspondence.     1914.  J4645

f round    as    "Problems    and    principles    of    correct 
nglish."

This   book,   with   two   preceding,    is   designed   "to

Davis,  Roy  and  Lingham,  Clarence  H.   Busi- 


ness  English  and  correspondence.     1914. 

J4678 

A  comprehensive  and  up-to-date  text-book.  The 
appendix  contains  information  concerning  postal 
regulations,  telegraphic  messages,  proof-reading,  fil- 
ing correspondence,  and  commercial  terms. 

Dwyer,  Ion  E.     The  business  letter.     1914. 

L 11984 

Concise  and  clear.  Covers  filing  and  gives  very 
full  postal  information. 

Grove,  Glen  A.  One  hundred  lessons  in 
English.  A  text  book  embracing  the  es- 
sentials of  practical  English  for  use  in

ers desiring  a  short  course.  1904.  J  4508 

Hagar,  Hubert  G.  and  SoRelle,  R.  P.  Ap- 
plied business  English  and  applied  busi- 
ness correspondence.  1914.  J  4677 

How  to  write  letters  that  win.  1911.  L  8380 
Published  by  the  System  company,  telling  "how 
to  build  business  by  letters  that  command  atten- 
tion and  bring  orders;  how  to  out  the  personal 
touch  into  a  letter,  handle  inquiries,  complaints 
and  collections." 

Lewis,  Edwin  H.     Business  English.    1911. 

L  11985 

An  excellent  book  for  the  advanced  student, 
laying  greatest  emphasis  on  principles  of  composi- 
tion. Its  logical  arrangement  and  effective  style 
are  the  best  illustrations  of  the  principles  stated 
by  the  author. 

Business  correspondence.     1914.     L 11969, 7 

Vol.  7  of  the  Library  of  business  practice,  pub. 
by  the  System  company. 

Among  the  articles  in  this  book  are  "How  to 
get  salesmanship  into  your  letters,"  "Working  out 
a  form  letter  series,"  "Tests  that  pick  your  strong- 
est jetters,"  "Building  up  retail  trade  by  mail," 
"Mailing  lists  and  the  follow-up." 

MacClintock,  Porter  Lander.  Essentials  of 
business  English.  1914.  J  4680 

A  very  readable  text-book,  dealing  in  an  easy 
and  attractive  style  with  grammatical  correctness, 
punctuation,  spelling,  diction,  arrangement,  _vocabu- 
lary,  clearness,  effectiveness,  spoken  English  and 
business  composition. 

Poole,  George  W.  and  Buzzell,  J.  J.,  ed. 
Letters  that  make  good.  1913.  L  8400 
Pt.  I  consists  of  a  series  of  papers  by  business 
men  on  the  principles  of  letter-writing  and  the 
organization  of  a  correspondence  department.  Pt. 
II  is  composed  of  fac-similes  of  over  three  hundred 
letters  "that  have  made  good,"  contributed  by  busi- 
ness men,  with  notes  and  comments. 

Smith,  C.  J.  and  Mayne,  D.  D.  Modern  busi- 
ness English.     1906.  J  4679 
The   text-book  at   present  in   use   in   the   Chicago 
public  schools. 

Stoll,  Albert.  Making  the  letter  pay,  by  A. 
Peter  Stowe,  pseud.  1913.  L  11988 

Deals  not  only  with  form  and  content  of  the 
business  letter,  but  covers  as  well  methods  of  mail- 
order business,  including  matters  of  efficiency  in 
distributing  and  mailing  and  the  filing  of  corre- 
spondence.

F  17904

counter three  winged  angels.  By  the  author  of 
"Crock  of  gold"  and  "Here  are  ladies."

Wiehe,  Mrs.  E.  M.  C.  Bellamy,  by  Elinor

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-fire  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  Is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy.

Sunday.

0  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.

floor,  open  9:80  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:10  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday.

Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:10  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday.
