Fiction,  Plays,  etc. 

The  following  titles  and  annotations  are  copied 
from  A  Brief  Guide  to  the  literature  of  Shake- 
speare, by  H.  H.  B.  Meyer,  published  by  the 
American  Library  Association. 

Aldrich,  T.   B.     Marjorie   Daw  and   other 

stories.     1885.  H  142 

"A  midnight  fantasy,"   one  of  the  stories,   turns 

on   a  visit   of   Hamlet   to    Verona,   a  meeting   with 
Juliet,  and — results. 

Bennett,  J.  Master  Skylark;  a  story  of 
Shakspere's  time.  1898.  F  7929 

An  interesting  story  for  boys  about  a  Stratford 
lad  who  goes  to  London  in  the  train  of  a  company 
of  players  and  is  there  befriended  by  Shakespeare. 

Black,  W.  Judith  Shakespeare;  her  love 
affairs  and  other  adventures.  1884. 

F2556 

This  story  of  Shakespeare's  daughter  does  not 
rank  with  Black's  best  work. 

Garnett,  R.  William  Shakespeare,  peda- 
gogue and  poacher;  a  drama.  1905. 

E9936 

Scene  is  at  Stratford,  1585.  Shakespeare  is  de- 
picted in  his  school  and  before  Sir  Thomas  Lucy 
and  lastly  set  free  by  Leicester. 

Landor,  W.  S.  Citation  and  examination 
of  William  Shakspeare,  Euseby  Treen, 
Joseph  Carnaby  and  Silas  Gough,  clerk, 
before  the  worshipful  Sir  Thomas  Libby, 
knight,  touching  deer-stealing  on  the 
19th  day  of  September  in  the  year  of 
Grace  1582,  now  first  published  from  orig- 
inal papers.  1891.  E  9641 

Charles    Lamb   is   generally   quoted    as   saying   of 


this  that  it  could  only  have  been  written  by  him 
who  wrote  it  or  by  him  about  whom  it  was  writ- 
ten. If  Shakespeare  had  written  it,  it  would  have 
had  some  dramatic  action,  which  is  what  it  lacks. 
As  it  is  it  needs  several  readings  to  get  familiar 
with  it  and  to  appreciate  it  for  the  fine  thing 
it  >s. 

Liitkenhaus,  Mrs.  A.  M.  Master  Skylark; 
a  dramatization  of  the  book  by  John 
Bennett.  1914.  E 12542 

"It  is  the  story,  ingeniously  turned  into  a  child's 
play,  rather  than  a  drama  from  the  book.  Indeed 
it  is  almost  as  simple  in  construction  as  if  the 
children  themselves  had  arranged  it  for  their  own 
amusement  from  a  book  in  which  they  had  been 
greatly  interested  and  whose  story  they  wished  to 
turn  into  a  play." 

McMahan,  Mrs.  A.  B.  Shakespeare's  love 
story,  1580-1609.  1909.  E 10164 

Turns  the  sonnets  into  a  love  story  with  Anne 
Hathaway  as  the  heroine. 

Munn,  Mrs.  M.  C.  Will  Shakespeare  of 
Stratford  and  London;  a  drama  in  four 
acts.  1910.  E 12618 

Begins  in  Stratford  at  the  time  of  the  poach- 
ing episode,  passes  on  to  London  and  depicts 
the  struggle  between  Essex  and  Southampton  and 
the  encounters  of  Shakespeare  with  the  lady  of 
the  sonnets. 

Noyes,  A.  Tales  of  the  Mermaid  tavern. 
1913.  E 11351 

A  narrative  poem  in  blank  verse  with  a  number 
of  ballads  and  lyrics  interspersed,  depicting  with 
much  grace  and  vigor  the  life  of  the  Mermaid 
tavern. 

Payn,  J.  The  talk  of  the  town.  A  novel. 
1885.  G 1168 

An  old-fashioned>  story  in  which  the  love  affair 
gets  tangled  up  with  Shakespearean  forgeries. 

Quiller-Couch,  A.  T.  Shakespeare's  Christ- 
mas and  other  stories,  by  "Q."  1905. 

F 12181 

A  picture  of  the  wild  life  of  the  Bankside  in 
Shakespeare's  time.  Introduces  the  poet,  his  fa- 
ther, and  some  of  the  players  and  authors. 

Scott,  Sir  W.  Kenilworth;  ed.,  with  an 
introduction  and  notes,  by  J.  H.  Castle- 
man.  1907.  F2162 
Kenilworth  is  sometimes  mentioned  as  a  novel 
in  which  Shakespeare  appears.  That  is  about  all 
he  does,  and  bows  in  response  to  a  complimentary 
greeting  from  the  Earl  of  Leicester.  Worthy  of 
note,  however,  is  the  scene  a  little  further  along 
in  the  same  chapter  (XVII)  in  which  Raleigh 
quotes  for  the  delectation  of  Queen  Elizabeth  a 
passage  from  Act  II  of  A  midsummer  night's 
dream.  The  passage  was  actually  written  some 
twenty  years  after  the  event,  but  what  is  twenty 
years  to  the  Wizard  of  the  North. 

Same.  808  Ev2,  411 

Everyman's  library. 

Shaw,  G.  B.  Misalliance,  the  dark  lady  of 
the  Sonnets,  and  Fanny's  first  play.  1914. 

E 12801 

"The  dark  lady  of  the  Sonnets"  is  little  more 
than  a  farce,  depicting  a  chance  encounter  be- 
tween the  dramatist,  Queen  Elizabeth  and  the  dark 
lady.  The  dramatist  might  stand  for  the  portrait 
of  Shaw  himself. 

Smith,  M.  C.  Mary  Paget;  a  romance  of 
old  Bermuda.  1900.  F9818 

A  romance  of  the  time  of  James  I.  A  large 
part  of  the  action  centers  around  the  wreck  of 
the  "Sea  Venture,"  which  supplied  Shakespeare 
with  so  many  hints  for  "The  Tempest." 


194 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Snaith,  J.  C.     Anne  Feversham.     1914. 

F 18264 

The  story  narrates  the  adventures  of  a  young 
couple  who  finally  join  Shakespeare's  company, 
where  the  girl  creates  the  part  of  Rosalind.  The 
dramatist  is  depicted  as  a  kindly  gentleman  who 
exercises  a  subtle  influence  over  all  with  whom 
he  comes  in  contact.  The  influence  is  told  about, 
but  never  realized. 

Stephens,  R.  N.  A  gentleman  player;  his 
adventures  on  a  secret  mission  for  Queen 
Elizabeth.  1899.  F  9798 

The  hero  is  a  comedian  in  the  Lord  Chamber- 
lain's company.  Introduces  other  members  of 
the  company  at  the  Globe  theatre,  including  Shake- 
speare. 

Wilde,  O.  Lord  Arthur  Savile's  crime; 
the  portrait  of  Mr.  W.  H.,  and  other 
stories.  1909.  F  7660 

"The  portrait  of  Mr.  W.  H."  turns  on  the 
attempt  to  show  by  a  forged  portrait  that  Mr. 
W.  H.  of  the  sonnets  was  Will  Hews,  a  boy 
actor  of  Shakespeare's  company.  The  theory  is 
much  like  the  one  advanced  by  Samuel  Butler 
(72). 

Pageantry  and  Pageants.

celebrations.

BOOK  BULLETIN 


195 


Cfjtcago  public  library,  #ltcf)isan  3benue  and  lyadfjington  Street 

JBepartment* 

anb  information  for  patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be  once 
renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be  re- 
newed but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of 
the  Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books 
on  the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks, 
which  must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made 
for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and 
no  other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  in- 
curring the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Harry   G.    Wilson Secretary 

Henry    E.    Legler Librarian 

Carl    B.    Roden Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance ; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room  and  Foreign  Room,  fourth  floor, 
Washington  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 


Actable  One-Act  Plays 

IThe  widespread  interest  in  the  modern  drama  has  increased  the  publication  of 
worthy  one-act  plays,  giving  the  public  aTchance  to  see  the  work  done  in  this 
branch  of  the  drama,  since  the  regular  place  is  denied  them  on  our  stage.  At 
the  same  time  amateur  actors  are  looking  for  good  short  plays  to  produce  in 
place  of  the  mechanical  farce  or  sentimental  comedy  which  has  been  in  vogue. 
For  the  purpose  of  directing  the  attention  of  amateur  actors  and  producers  to 
the  best  of  the  modern  short  plays,  the  following  list  has  been  compiled  by 
Samuel  Kaplan  of  the  Library  staff. 

Arkell,  Reginald.     Columbine;   a 


phantasy. 
E 11768 

Characters:    Four  men;  one  woman. 
A  poetic  fairy  play,  depending  for  its  success  on 
the  charm  of  the  acting.     Easy  to  act  and  produce. 

Augier,  Emile.    The  post-scriptum.    E  13363 
Characters:    Two   men;    one   woman. 
Translation     of     a     bright     and     chatty     French 
comedy. 

Bangs,  John    Kendrick.     The    Barrington's 
"at  home."  El  1795 

Characters:    Three  women;  two  men. 
A  fairly  amusing  skit  in  which  much  furniture  is 
moved  about. 

In   his:  The  real  thing,  and   other  farces.      1909. 

The  real  thing.  E  11795 

Characters:     Five   men;    two   women. 
Lacking  action,  this  play  should  act  well  because 
of  its   humorous  dialogue  and  satire.     The  point  is 
that  if  you  teach  servants  their  place,  there  will  be 
no  servant  problem. 

The  return  of  Christmas.  E  11795 

Characters:  Three  men,  two  women,  one  boy  and 
one  girl. 


A  Christmas  fable  brought  up  to  date.  There  it 
little  action.  Its  interest  lies  in  its  satire. 

In  his:   The  real  thing  and  other  farces.     1909. 

Banville,  Theodore  de.     Charming  L6andre. 

E  13366 

Characters:    Two    men;    one   woman. 
With   picturesque   costumes   and   good   acting  this 
should  make  a  thoroughly  enjoyable  entertainment. 

Baring,  Maurice.    The  Aulis  difficulty. 

El  1798 

Characters:  Agamemnon.  Iphigenia,  Clytasmnes- 
tra,  Oddysseus,  and  Calchas. 

Mildly   humorous  sketch   showing  how   Iphigenia   is 
persudaded  to  let  herself  be  sacrificed  to  Artemis. 

In  his:  Diminutive  dramas.     1911. 

The  blue  harlequin.  E  11798 

Characters:  Policeman,  Pantaloon,  Clown  and 
Columbine. 

A  capital  burlesque  of  the  Maeterlinck  play.  It 
should  act  well. 

In  his:  Diminutive  dramas.     1911. 

Catharine  Parr.  E  11798 

Characters:  A  man  and  a  woman. 

Humorous  give  and  take  dialogue  between  Henrr 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


VIII  and  one  of  his  wives,  in  which  Henry  shows 
himself   to   be,   on   the   whole,   an   amiable   husband 
rather  apt  to  be  irritated  by  trifles. 
In  his:  Diminutive  dramas. 

The  drawback.  El  1798 

Characters:  A  man  and  a  woman. 
Bright,  funny  dialogue,  showing  how  awkward  it 
is  at  times  for  one's  father  to  be  a  hangman. 
In  his:    Diminutive  dramas.     1911. 

The  fatal  rubber.  E  11798 

Characters:  Charles  VI  of  France,  the  queen,  the 
dauphin,  and  Catharine,  his  sister. 

An  amusing  little  skit  in  which  the  queen  cheats 
and  the  king  kicks  over  the  card  table. 

In  his:   Diminutive  dramas. 

The  Greek  vase.  E  11798 

Characters:  Two  men. 

A  young  sculptor,  sick  and  starving,  shatters  his 
masterpiece  rather  than  sell  it  to  a  rich  dealer  for  a 
pittance. 

In  his:    Diminutive  dramas.     1911. 

Pious  Aeneas.  E  11798 

Characters:     Two    men;    one    woman. 
In  which  Dido  tells  Aeneas  that  she  doesn't  care 
if  he  never  comes  back. 
In  his:   Diminutive  dramas. 

The  rehearsal.  E  11798 

Characters:     Seven  men. 

A    rehearsal    of    Macbeth    on    the    stage    of    the 
Globe  Theater  in  1S9S.    A  clever,  pointed  satire. 
In  his:   Diminutive  dramas. 

Barrie,  James  Matthew.    Pantaloon. 

E  11803 

Characters:  Pantaloon,  Harlequin,  Clown,  and 
Columbine. 

One  of  the  best  variants  of  the  Columbine  theme. 
Its  effectiveness  lies  in  its  whimsicality  and  in  its 
human  appeal. 

In  his:  Half  hours.      1914. 

Rosalind.  E  11803 

Characters:   Two   women;   one  man. 

A  "big,"  little  play,  comic  in  the  best  sense  of 
the  word,  with  a  profound  human  appeal.  It  tells 
the  story  of  a  great  actress  who  hides  from  her  ad- 
mirers in  order  to  discover  how  it  feels  to  be  mid- 
dle-aged. 

In  his:  Half  hours.     1914. 

The  twelve-pound  look.  E  1803 

Characters:     Two  women;  one  man. 

One  of  the  best  of  the  modern  one-act  plays. 
Concentrates  a  big  and  vital  story  into  a  half  hour 
of  stage  performance.  Requires  finished  acting. 

In  his:  Half  hours.     1914. 

The  will.  E  11803 

Characters:  Four  men;  one  woman. 
A  short  play  in  three  parts  showing  three  genera- 
tions in  the  life  of  a  married  pair  who  have 
gradually  become  hard  and  estranged  because  of 
the  money  they  have  acquired.  Written  in  Barrie's 
bitterest  mood. 

In  his:  Half  hours.     1914. 

Bennett,  Arnold.    A  good  woman.     E  11813 
Characters:    Two  men;   one  woman. 
A  play   of  brisk  and   humorous  dialogue,   written 
in  the  absurd  vein  of  a  Gilbert-Sullivan  play.     Re- 
quires skillful  acting  to  be  effective. 
In  his:    Polite  farces.     1912. 

A  question  of  sex.  E  11813 

Characters:    Two  men;  two  women. 
The  baby  is  a   girl;   and  they  tell   uncle   it  is  a 
boy  in  order  to  get  the  money  he  promised.     Com- 
plications ensue.     The   dialogue   is   clever,   and   the 
play   should   act   well. 

In  his:    Polite  farces.     1912. 

The  stepmother.  E  11813 

Characters:     Two   men;    two   women. 
A  satire  on  the  popular  lady  novelist.     The  action 
is   farcical,   the   dialogue  absurd,   but   on   the   whole 


quite  funny.     It  should  act  well,  and  is  admirably 
suited  for  amateurs. 

In  his:    Polite  farces.      1912. 

Bernard,  Tristan.    French  without  a  master. 

E  13368 

Characters:    Five  men;  two  women. 
Mistaken  identity,  misinterpretation,  etc.,  are  here 
used  to  make  a  very  funny  farce. 

Brown,  Alice.     Joint  owners  in   Spain. 

Characters:    Four  women.  E  11869 

A   comedy   of   character,   humorous   and   pathetic. 

The  scene  is  in  an  old  ladies'  home;   three  of  the 

characters  are  inmates.     Clever  character  acting  is 

required  to  present  them  effectively. 

Caillavet,  G.  A.  de.     Choosing  a  career. 

E  13371 

Characters:    Three  men;  one  woman. 
Inept    farce,    foolish    enough    for    the    vaudeville 
stage. 

Cannan,  Gilbert.    James  and  John.    E  11880 

Characters:     Three  men;    one   woman. 

A  grim  little  play,  telling  of  four  wrecked  lives. 

In  his:    Four  plays.     1913. 

Mary's  wedding.  E  11880 

Characters:  Three  women;   two  men. 
A    little     tragedy     written     in     English    country 
dialect,  effective  not  so  much  for  its  powerful  end- 
ing but  for  its  character  study  and  its  atmosphere. 
In  his:    Four  plays.     1913. 

Chekhov,  Anton.    A  marriage  proposal. 

E 13375 

Characters:    Two  men;   one  woman. 
A  noisy_  comedy  of  little  distinction  in  its  transla- 
tion.    It  is  meant  to  satirize  the  Russian  peasant. 

Davis,  Richard  Harding.    The  Zone  police. 

E 11986 

Characters:    Four  men. 

A  conventional  bit  of  theatricalism.  The  scene 
is  laid  in  Panama. 

Dickinson,  Thomas  H.   In  hospital.  E  13013 

Characters:    Three  men;   two   women. 
Notable  for  the  reserve  with  which  it  is  written; 
contains    one   very    touching   scene. 
In:  Wisconsin  plays.     1914. 

Dix,  Beulah  Marie.    Allison's  lad.     E  12010 

Characters:     Six  men. 

The  period  is  1648;  the  characters,  Cavaliers  and 
Roundheads.  The  play  is  replete  with  oats  and 
drumtraps.  It_  ends  with  the  unique  situation  of 
three  men  casting  dice  to  determine  which  of  them 
shall  die. 

The  captain  of  the  gate.  E  12010 

Characters:    Six  men. 

A  conventional  but  stirring  little  war  play  de- 
scribing an  incident  in  Cromwell's  invasion  of  Ire- 
land. 

In  her:  Allison's  lad,  and  other  martial  interludes. 

The  dark  of  dawn.  E  12010 

Characters:    Four  men. 

A  quadruple  coincidence  serving  as  the  basis  for 
a  fairly  interesting  story  of  the  Thirty  Years'  War. 

In  her:  Allison's  lad,  and  other  martial  interludes. 
1910. 

The  hundredth  trick.  E  12010 

Characters:    Four  men. 

A  little  war  play  possessing  considerable  dramatic 
power.  The  scene  is  laid  in  Munster  in  the  time 
of  Queen  Elizabeth. 

In  her:  Allison's  lad,  and  other  martial  interludes. 

The  snare  and  the  fowler.          E  12010 

Characters:     Three   men. 

A  coincidence  of  the  French  Revolution,  well  told, 
in  spite  of  its  blank  verse. 

In  her:  Allison's  lad,  and  other  martial  interludes. 
1910. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


The  weakest  link.  E  12010 

Characters:    Four  men. 

A  romantic  playlet  of  the  Hundred  Years  War 
written  in  blank  verse.  It  is  interesting,  although 
most  of  the  dialogue  is  concerned  with  what  is  hap- 
pening off  stage. 

In  her:  Allison's  lad,  and  other  martial  interludes. 
1910. 

Dunsany,  Lord.    The  glittering  gate. 

£  12036 

Characters:    Two  burglars,  both  dead. 
Scene:    Before  the  gate  of  heaven.     They  pry  it 
open  with  a  jimmy  and  find   nothing  on   the   other 
side. 
In  his:    Five  plays.     1914. 

The  lost  silk  hat.  E  12036 

Characters:    Five  men. 

The  poet  does  not  want  the  hat  recovered  because 
he  thinks  marriage  kills  romance. 
In  his:    Five  plays.     1914. 

Ellis,  Mrs.  Havelock.  The  mothers.   E  12095 
Characters:    Two  women;   one  man. 
A  study  of  love,   with   the  psychology  somewhat 
muddled.     Written  in  the  Cornish  dialect. 
In  her:   Love  in  danger.     1915. 

The  pixy.  E  12095 

Characters:     Three   men. 

A  study  of  character,  making  a  fresh  and  ap- 
pealing though  somewhat  sombre  one-act  play.  It 
should  act  well,  in  spite  of  its  static  quality.  The 
conversation  is  in  the  Cornish  dialect. 

In  her:   Love  in  danger.     1915. 

The  subjection  of  Kezia.  E  12095 

Characters:     Two  men;  one  woman. 

A  simple  love  story,  quite  undramatic,  told  in 
English  country  dialect.  Would  be  charming  if 
acted  by  the  right  people. 

In  her:   Love  in  danger.     1915. 

Ervine,    St.    John     G.     The    magnanimous 
lover.  E 12101 

Characters:    Three  men;  two  women. 

The  man  comes  back  after  ten  years,  and  finds 
that  the  woman  refuses  to  be  made  "respectable." 
The  scene  is  laid  in  Ireland.  The  story  is  told  with 
restraint.  It  is  one  of  the  best  products  of  the 
Irish  theater. 

In  his:  Four  Irish   plays.      1914. 

The  Orangeman.  E  12101 

Characters:  Three  men;  one  woman. 
Presentation  of  the  old  antagonism  between  fath- 
ers and  children,  this  time  between  a  father  who  is 
an  ardent  Orangeman  and  a  son  who  is  not.  Not 
likely  to  interest  people  unfamiliar  with  the  Ulster 
situation. 

In  his:  Four  Irish   plays.     1914. 

Fitzmaurice,  George.   The  pie-dish.    E  12144 

Characters:    Four  men;  two  women. 
Long-winded    satire    in    the    Irish    dialect.      One 
of  the  Abbey  Theater  plays. 
In  his:    Five  plays.     1914. 

Gale,  Zona.    The  neighbours.  E  13013 

Characters:     Six   women;   two   men. 
An  excellent  study  of  American  country  life  writ- 
ten with  humor  and  feeling. 
In:   Wisconsin   plays.      1914. 

Giacosa,  Guiseppe.    The  wager.          E  13391 

Characters:     Four   men;   one   woman. 

A  romantic  playlet  of  the  fourteenth  century. 
The  kind  of  play  in  which  the  father  gives  his 
daughter  to  the  winner  of  a  game  of  chess.  Of 
course,  the  right  man  wins. 

Gibson,  Wilfred.    The  ferry.  E  12229 

Characters:    Two  men;  one  woman. 

Thinking  he  hears  his  wife  calling,  the  aged 
ferry-man  crosses  the  flooded  river  and  does  not 
return.  A  poetic  tragedy  told  with  simplicity  and 
impressiveness. 

In   his:  On  the  threshold.     1907. 


-On  the  threshold. 


E 12229 


Characters:     Two  women;  one  man. 
Almost  a  dramatic  monologue.     Poetic  in  theme 
and  style;  not  meant  primarily  for  the  stage. 

Winter  dawn.  E  12229 

Characters:    Two  women;  one  man. 
A  fifteen  minute  tragedy,  meant  to  be  read  rather 
than  acted. 

In  his:    On  the  threshold.     1907. 

Womenkind.  E  12228 

Characters:    Three   women;   two  men. 

On  her  wedding  day  the  wife  leaves  her  husband 
because  she  finds  that  he  has  been  unfaithful  to 
her.  The  subject  is  handled  with  considerable 
dramatic  power.  Written  in  blank  verse  that  very 
often  develops  into  true  poetry. 

Goodman,  Kenneth  Sawyer.  Back  of  the 
Yards.  E 12248 

Characters:    Three  men;  two  women. 

A  one-act  play  that  tells  a  good  story,  holds  the 
interest  throughout,  and  succeeds  in  truthfully  re- 
flecting a  phase  of  Chicago  life. 

Barbara.  E  12246 

Characters:    Two  men;  one  woman. 
Comedy  of  absurdities  meant  to  scintillate. 

Dust  of  the  road.  E  12261 

Characters:    Three  men;  one  woman. 

On    Christmas    morn,    Judas    appears    to    another 

soul    who    is    about    to    perjure    himself    for    thirty 

pieces  of  silver  and  saves  him. 

Ephraim  and  the  winged  bear.    E  12245 

Characters:  Three  men;  three  women;  a  winged 
bear. 

A  Christmas  nightmare.  A  respectable  citizen 
lets  his  weaknesses  get  the  better  of  him  and  be- 
comes thereby  a  more  amiable  human  being. 

The  game  of  chess.  E  12247 

Characters:    Four  men. 

Interesting  melodrama,  effective  when  well  acted, 
but  quite  meaningless. 

Goodman  and  Stevens.  Holbein  in  Black- 
friars.  E  12250 

Characters:    Six  men;  two  women. 

Complications  peculiar  to  the  stage  arise  when 
Henry  VIII  sits  for  his  portrait.  A  mildly  interest- 
ing play. 

Greene,  Clay  M.  The  dispensation.   E  12265 

Characters:    Four  men. 

In  which  the  Pope  permits  one  of  his  priests,  to 
marry    and    become    a    soldier    because    the    priest 
argues  that  he  can  best  serve  the  church  by  fight- 
ing for  it. 
The  Star  of  Bethlehem.  E  12265 

Characters:    Five  men. 

The  Christmas  spirit  settles  down  on  an  ex- 
clusive club  and  four  of  its  members  become  goody- 
goody. 

In  his:    The  dispensation  and  other  plays.    1914. 

Gregory,  Lady  Augusta.     Coats.       E 12272 

Characters:    Three  men. 

A  farce  with  a  good  deal  of  fun  and  satire  in  it. 

In  her:    New  comedies.     1913. 

The  full  moon.  E  12272 

Characters:    Five  men;   three  women. 
Long,   wearisome,   and   talky.     It   is   a   sequel   to 
Hyacinth  Halvey. 
In  her:    New  comedies.     1913. 

The  gaol  gate.  E  12270 

Characters:    Two  women;   one  man. 
A   bleak   Irish   tragedy   written   with    brevity   and 
directness. 
In  her:    Seven  short  plays.     1909. 

Hyacinth  Halvey.  E  12270 

Characters:    Four   men;   two  women. 

Rambling  little  play  full  of  fun,  in  which 
Hyacinth  Halvey  finds  it  impossible  to  destroy  his 
reputation. 

In  her:  Seven  short  plays.     1909. 


The  jackdaw.  E  12270 

Characters:    Four  men;  two  women. 
An   Irish   satire,   mildly    humorous   and    fearfully 
long   winded. 
In  her:  Seven  short  plays.     1909. 

McDonough's  wife.  E  12272 

Characters:    Two  women;   one  man. 
The  tragedy   of  a  poor,   Irish  piper  who  forgets 
his  pride  when  his  wife  is  to  be  buried. 
In  her:    New  comedies.     1913. 

The  rising  of  the  moon.  E  12270 

Characters:   Tour  men. 

An  unusual  situation  has  been  used  to  build  up 
a  subtly  humorous  and  stirring  little  play  of  Irish 
patriotism.  Has  been  very  successfully  done  by  the 
Abbey  Players. 

In  her:    Seven  short  plays.     1909. 

Spreading  the  news.  E  12270 

Characters:   Seven  men;  three  women. 
An  Irish  comedy,  undramatic,  on  the  whole,  but 
rich  in  satire  and  local  color. 
In  her:    Seven  short  plays.     1909. 

The  travelling  man.  E  12270 

Characters:    A  man,  a  woman,  and  a  child. 
A  dramatization  of  an  old  Irish  legend. 
In  her:    Seven  short  plays.     1909. 

The  workhouse  ward.  E  12270 

Characters:    Two  men;  one  woman. 
A  capital  comedy,  rich  in  fun  and  human  nature. 
Easy  to  stage  effectively. 

In  her:  Seven  short  plays.     1909. 

Hankin,  St.  John.    The  burglar  who  failed. 

E  12284, 3 

Characters:    Two   women;   one   man. 
Whimsical  sketch  in  which  our  sympathies  are  en- 
listed  in   behalf   of  a  burglar   who   cannot  make   a 
success  at  his  profession. 
In:    Dramatic  works,  v.  3. 

The  constant  lover.  E  12284, 2 

Characters:    A  man  and  a  woman. 
An   amusing   episode   in   the   life    of   a    nice   boy 
who  is  never  out  of  love. 
In:  Dramatic  works,  v.  2. 

Hervieu,  Paul.    Modesty.  E  13396 

Characters:    Two  men;  one  woman. 
Fifteen  pages  of  conversation  about  getting  mar- 
ried. 

The  dear  departed. 

E 12332 

Characters:     Three  men;  two  women;  one  girl. 
A   play   that   shows   human    nature   almost   at    its 
worst     Written  with  a  keen  sense  of  the  theater. 
In  his:    Five  one-act  plays.     1913. 

Fancy  free.  E  12332 

Characters:    Two   men;   two  women. 
An    absurdity    full    of    machine    made    epigrams. 
Funny  when  well  done. 
In  his:    Five  one-act  plays.     1913. 

The  fifth  commandment.  E  12332 

Characters:    Two  men;   one  woman. 

An  excellent  play,  handling  a  big  idea,  telling  its 
story  effectively  through  a  series  of  interesting 
situations  and  creating  some  very  clear-cut  char- 

In  'his:    Five  one-act  plays.     1913. 

The  master  of  the  house.  E  12332 

Characters:    Four  men:  two  women. 
A    gruesome    play,    with   no    discernible    meaning, 
effective  because  of  its  theatricalism. 
In  his:   Five  one-act  plays.     1913. 

Phipps.  E  12332 

Characters:    Two  men;   one  woman. 
A  comedy  in  which  Stanley  Houghton  fails  to  be 
as  clever  as  Oscar  Wilde. 

In  his:    Five  one-act  plays.     1913. 


Houghton,  Stanley. 


Jones,  Henry  Arthur.     The  goal.       E  12426 

Characters:     Four    men;    two    women. 
A    pretty,    sentimental    play,    depending    for    its 
success  on  the  ability  of  the  actors. 
In  his:    The  Theater  of  ideas.     1915. 

Her  tongue.  E  12426 

Characters:    Three  men;  two  women. 
Farce   of  comparatively   high  order.     Very  amus- 
ing.    Easy  to  do  well. 

In  his:  The  theater  of  ideas.     1915. 

Leonard,  William  Ellery.  Glory  of  the 
morning.  E 13013 

Characters:  Two  men;  one  woman;  a  boy,  and  a 
girl. 

The  white  man  leaves  his  squaw  to  go  back  to 
France,  taking  one  of  the  children  with  him.  A 
slow  moving  play  with  impossible  characters  and 
dialogue. 

In:    Wisconsin  plays.     1914. 

Mackaye,  Percy.     Chuck.  E  12565 

Characters:    Three  men;  one  woman. 
A  little  sketch,   fresh  with  the  spirit  of  the  out- 
doors. 

In  his:    Yankee  phantasies.     1912. 

Masefield,  John.     Mrs.  Harrison.       E  12587 
Characters:    Three  men;   one  woman. 
An   interesting   one-act   play   somewhat   mixed   in 

mood,  for  it  begins  a  comedy  and  ends  unexpectedly 

a  tragedy. 

In  his:    The  tragedy  of  Nan.     1912. 

Maurey,  Max.     Rosalie.  E 13404 

Characters:    Two  women;  one  man. 
A  bit   of  social   satire   that  is   delightfully   funny 
from   beginning  to  end. 

Mayne,  Rutherford.    Red  turf.  E  12572 

Characters:    Four  men;  one  woman. 

A  one-act  tragedy,  simple,  vivid,  and  well  writ- 
ten, leaving  the  reader  or  spectator  with  a  better 
understanding  of  Ireland. 

In  his:    The  drone  and  other  plays.      1912. 

The  troth.  E  12572 

Characters:     Three   men;   one  woman. 
A  one-act  play  of  Ireland  in  famine  time  that  is 
tensely  dramatic  from  beginning  to  end. 
In  his:    The  drone  and  other  plays.     1912. 

Meilhac  and  Halevy.     Indian  summer. 

E 13406 

Characters:    Two  men;  two  women. 
Pretty,     sentimental     comedy,    very     popular     in 
France.     Admirably    suited    for   amateurs. 

The  cheat  of  pity. 

E 12598 

Characters:    Two   men;  one  woman. 
An  interesting  situation  set  forth  with  skill.  More 
valuable    dramatically    than    most    of    Middleton's 
plays. 

In  his:    Tradition,  and  other  plays.     1913. 

— Embers.  E  12597 

Characters:    Two  men;   two  women. 

The  older  man  tells  that  which  has  never  before 
passed  his  lips._  Thereby,  he  "saves"  the  younger 
man.  Conventional  and  rhetorical. 


Middleton,  George. 


-The  failures. 


E  12597 


Characters:    A  man  and  a  woman. 

He  comes  to  her  when  she  is  a  widow.  He  is 
bitterly  disappointed  in  her  because  she  "bore  one 
man's  name  and  loved  another."  After  fifteen 
minutes  of  dialogue  they  are  reconciled,  and  "she 
feels  his  tears  and  kisses  on  her  hand." 

In   his:    Embers,  and   other  plays.      1911. 

— The  gargoyle.  E  12597 

Characters:     Two   men. 

A  long,  discursive  dialogue  without  much  dra- 
matic interest. 

In  his:    Embers,  and  other  plays.     1911.

In  his  house.  E  12597 

Characters:    Two  women;   one  man. 
An   interesting   play   to    read,   even    though   it   is 
thoroughly    unconvincing. 

In  his:    Embers,  and  other  plays.     1911. 

Madonna.  E  12597 

Characters:     Three   men;   one   woman. 
On    the    eve    of    their    wedding;,    the    father    tells 
the  young  couple  the  story  of  his  life. 
In  his:   Embers,  and  other  plays.     1911. 

The  man  masterful.  E  12597 

Characters:    Two  women. 

Another  dialogue  on  the  subject  of  illicit  love, 
which  this  author  thinks  is  dramatic,  but  never  uses 
in  a  dramatic  way. 

In  his:   Embers,  and  other  plays.     1911. 

Mothers.  E  12598 

Characters:    Two  women;  one  man. 
A  dialogue  between  the  mother  and  the  wife  of  a 
worthless  young;   man. 

In  his:   Tradition,  and  other  plays.     1913. 

On  bail.  E  12598 

Characters:  Two  men;  one  woman. 
A  short  story  told  in  dialogue  form. 
In  his:  Tradition,  and  other  plays.  1913. 

Their  wife.  E  12598 

Characters:     Two  men. 

The  man  she  divorced  and  the  man  she  married 
meet  and  discuss  their  wife. 

In  his:   Tradition,  and  other  plays.     1913. 

Tradition.  E  12598 

Characters:    Two  women;   one  man. 

A  very  talky  play  in  which  very  little  happens. 

Waiting.  E  12598 

Characters:    A  man,  a  woman,  and  a  child. 
Here    we    find    a    child    uniting    two    hearts    that 
have   long   been   pining   for   each   other. 
In  his:    Tradition,  and  other  plays.     1913. 

Musset,  Alfred  de  and  Augier,  fimile.  The 
green  coat.  E  13411 

Characters:    Three  men;  one  woman. 

A  comedy  of  artist  life  in  the  Latin  quarter  done 
in  the  conventional  way,  but  amusing  enough  to  be 
worth  presentation. 

Noyes,  Alfred.     A  Belgian  Christmas  Eve. 

E 12676 

Characters:    Nine  men;   one  woman;   one  child. 
The  horrors  of  war  presented  in  a  gripping  little 
drama. 

Rivoire,  Andre.    The  little  shepherdess. 

E  13418 

Characters:    Two  women;  one  man. 
A  charming  pastoral  sketch. 

Schnitzler,  Arthur.    The  mate.  E  12794 

Characters:    Four  men;  one  woman. 

Drama  of  very  high  merit  constructed  with  ex- 
traordinary cleverness.  The  story,  one  of  illicit 
love,  is  told  backwards,  each  revelation  coming  as 
a  fresh  surprise  and  adding  to  the  dramatic  in- 
terest of  the  play. 

In  his:  The  green  cockatoo,  and  other  plays.  1913. 

Shaw,  George  Bernard.  The  Dark  Lady  of 
the  Sonnets.  E  12801 

Characters:    Two  men;  two  women. 
A  delightful  travesty  in  which  Shaw  tries  to  make 
famous  immortals  behave  like  human  beings. 

Press  cuttings.  E  12802 

Characters:    Three  men;   three  women. 
The  English  government  is  lampooned  in  a  wildly 
extravagant  and  very  funny  satire. 

Sinclair,  Upton.    The  second-story  man. 

E  12811 

Characters:     Two   men:    one   woman. 
A     burglar,     victim     of     the     capitalistic     system, 
breaks   into   the   home   of   a   lawyer   who   defrauded 


him,  and  instead  of  shooting  him,  preaches  to  him 
on  the  wickedness  of  capitalism. 
In  his:    Plays  of  protest.     1912. 

Stevens  and  Goodman.    Ryland.        E  12864 

Characters:    Five  men;  two  women. 
A  fairly  interesting,  slow  moving  comedy  dealing 
with   historical   celebrities. 


Strindberg,  August. 


Debit  and  credit. 

E  12831 

Characters:    Six   men;   three  women. 

The  man  who  has  succeeded  because  of  the  help 
others  have  rendered  him  is  made  the  subject  of 
one  of  Strindberg's  characteristically  unpleasant 
plays. 

In  his:    Three  one-act  plays. 

Facing  death.  E  12826 

Characters:    Three  women:  two  men. 

A  bitter  and  sordid  tragedy  in  which  Strindberg 
expresses  his  hatred  of  women  in  the  strongest  of 
terms. 

In  his:    Plays.     1912. 

Motherlove.  E  12833 

Characters:     Four   women. 

A  suggestive  play  on  an  interesting  theme  for  ex- 
perienced actors  and  an  experienced  audience. 

The  outcast.  E  12831 

Characters:    Two  men. 

Interesting  dialogue  full  of  dramatic  surprises. 

In  his:    Three  one-act  plays. 

Another  copy.  E  12826 

The  stronger.  E  12828 

Characters:    Two  women. 

An  interesting  and  curious  dramatic  experiment, 
a  play  in  which  there  are  two  characters  and  only 
one  speaks.  It  makes  very  good  drama. 

Another  copy.  E  13103 

Another  copy.  E  12835, 2 

Sudermann,  Hermann.    The  far-away  prin- 
cess. E 12842 
Characters:     Seven  women;   two  men. 
A    charming    comedy    in    which    Sudermann    suc- 
ceeds in  putting  freshness  and   humor  into  an   old 
theme. 

In  his:    Roses.     1909. 

Fritzchen.  E  12843 

Characters:    Five  men;   two  women. 
One    of    the    best    of    modern    one-act    plays;    a 
powerful    tragedy,    creating    in    the    beginning    an 
oppressive    feeling    of    impending    calamity,    ending 
in   a   terrible  denouement   which  makes  an   extraor- 
dinary scene  theatrically. 
In  his:    Morituri.      1910. 

The  last  visit.  E  12842 

Characters:    Five  men,  three  women. 
The  air  of  mystery  hanging  over  this  piece,_the 
manner    in    in    which    the    suspense    is    maintained 
throughout,   and    its   effective   climax,   make   this   an 
admirable   play   theatrically. 
In  his:    Koses.      1909. 

Margot.  E  12842 

Characters:     Three   men;   two   women. 
Splendid    drama,    thought    provoking,    suggestive, 
containing  true  human   character. 
In  his:    Roses.     1909. 

Streaks  of  light.  E  12842 

Characters:    Two  men;   one  woman. 
A   lurid   and   exotic   little  tragedy   on   the   subject 
of  husband,  wife,  and  lover. 
In   his:    Roses.      1909. 

Teja.  E  12843 

Characters:    Nine   men:   two  women. 
Tragedy   of   a   Gothic   king  who   failed   in   his   at- 
tempt  to   invade   Italy. 
In   his:   Morituri.      1910.

In  his:    Five  little  plays.     1913.

Characters :    Two  men ;  _  one  woman. 

A  play  in  which  three  intensely  djsagreeable  peo- 
ple get  themselves  involved  in  disagreeable  but 
highly  dramatic  situations. 

In  his:    Five  little  plays.    1913.

Characters:    A  man  and  a  woman. 
A  picture  of  the  most  abject  poverty. 
In  his:   Five  little  plays.     1913. 

A  marriage  has  been  arranged.    E  12859 

Characters:    A  man  and  a  woman. 

Two  sophisticated  people  in  a  witty  dialogue. 

In  his:   Five  little  plays.     1913. 

The  open  door.  E  12859 

Characters:    A  man  and  a  woman. 

In  which  the  wife  and  the  lover  confess  their 
love  in  sparkling  dialogue  under  conditions  that 
are  decidedly  interesting. 

In  his:   Five  little  plays.     1913. 

Synge,  John  M.    Riders  to  the  sea.    E  12882 

Another  copy.  E  13101 

— Another  copy.  E  12007 

Characters:     Three   women;    one   man. 

A  tragedy  of  the  very  highest  order  in  which

protagonist. 

-The  shadow  of  the  glen.  E  12882 
Another  copy.                                   E  12880 

Characters:    Three   men;   one   woman. 

"There  is  no  one-act  play  in  the  language  for 
compression,  for  humanity,  and  for  perfection  of 
form  to  put  near  'The  shadow  of  the  glen.' " 


Wilde,  Percival.     Dawn.  E  13004 

Characters:    Two  men,  one  woman,  one  child. 
A    melodrama,    interesting   enough,    but   meaning- 
less.     The    play    ends   with    an    explosion    of    nitro- 
glycerine and  a  scene,  presumably  in  heaven. 
In  his:    Dawn,  and  other  plays.     1915. 

The  finger  of  God.  E  13004 

Characters:    Two  men;  one  woman. 

Just  as  he  is  about  to  run  off  with  embezzled 
funds,  "a  girl"  comes  to  him  and  says  that  she 
trusts  him,  after  which  he  resolves  to  stay  and 
"face  the  music." 

In  his:    Dawn,  and  other  plays.     1915. 

The  house  of  cards.  E  13004 

Characters:     A   man  and   a   woman. 
A  tricky  and  clever  bit  of  theatricalism  good  for 
ten  minutes  of  suspense  and  a  thrill  of  surprise. 
In  his:    Dawn,  and  other  plays.     1915. 

The  noble  lord.  E  13004 

Characters:      Two    men;    one    woman. 

An  amusing  skit,  consisting  mainly  of  a  dialogue 
between  a  lady  who  pretends  to  drown  and  a  lord 
who  saves  her. 

In  his:    Dawn,  and  other  plays.     1915. 

Playing  with  fire.  E  13004 

Characters:    Two  women;  one  man. 

Quaint  and  mechanical  dialogue  between  a  boy 
and  a  girl  who  are  very  much  in  love  with  each 
other. 

In  his:    Dawn,  and  other  plays.     1915. 

The  traitor.  E  13004 

Characters:     Seven   men. 

An  exciting  little  melodrama  of  the  Boer  war. 
The  climax  is  clever,  but  like  the  rest  of  the  play, 
unconvincing. 

In  his:    Dawn,  and  other  plays.     1915. 

Yeats,  William  Butler.     Cathleen  Ni  Houli- 
han. E  13061 
Characters:    Three  men;  three  women. 
A  beautifully  written  allegory  of  Irish  patriotism. 
In  his:    The  Unicorn  from  the  stars.     1908. 


What's  in  the  Magazines 


II.     The  Singing  Choir  of  New  Poets 

We  are  the  music-makers, 

And  we  are  the  dreamers  of  dreams, 
Wandering  by  lone  sea-breakers. 

And  sitting  by  desolate  streams; 
World-losers  and  world-forsakers, 

On  whom  the  pale  moon  gleams: 
Yet  we  are  the  movers  and  shakers 

Of  the  world  forever,  it  seems. 

Arthur  O'Shaugnessy. 

Either  because  at  least  two  journals 
devoted  principally  to  modern  poetry 
are  published  here,  or  that  a  surpris- 
ingly large  number  of  modern  poets 
dwell  here,  Chicago  has  become  the 
center  of  the  renaissance  of  poetry 
which  just  now  is  especially  manifest 
in  magazine  literature.  Two  books  of 
poetry  which  have  had  a  circulation  al- 
most comparable  with  that  of  the  best 
sellers,  are  the  product  of  present-day 
Illinois  poets — the  Congo  by  Vachel 
Lindsey,  and  Spoon  River  Anthology 


by  Edgar  Lee  Masters.  Mr.  Masters 
wrote  under  the  pen-name  of  Webster 
Ford  when  his  contributions  appeared 
in  the  periodical  press.  Both  books 
have  been  widely  discussed  and  com- 
mented upon  in  this  country  and 
abroad,  and  both  promise  to  be  the 
forerunners  of  a  new  note  in  verse — or 
at  least  in  verse  form. 

Other  local  poets  whose  verses  ap- 
pear frequently  in  the  magazines,  or 
who  have  recently  issued  sheaves  of 
verse  in  book  form,  include: 

Charles  G.  Blanden 
Carl  Sandburg 
Eunice  Tietjens 
Mary  Aldis 
Maurice  Brown 
Donald  Robertson 
Harriet  Monroe 
Maxwell  Bodenheim 
Edith  Wyatt 


Thomas  Wood 
Stevens 

Alice  Oliver  Hen- 
derson 

E.    Preston    Dargan 

Rita  Benton 

Frances  Shaw 

Max  Michelson 

Dorothy  Dudley 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


Harold  Childs  Walter  Heymann 

Florence     Kiper       Willa  Sibert  Gather 
Frank  Charlton     Lawrence 

Edholm 

Some  of  their  verses  have  a  distinctly 
Chicago  flavor.  In  a  current  issue  of 
the  Bookman,  Agnes  Lee  writes  of 
The  Divers,  with  its  poignant  recurrent 
lines: 

Down  again,  and  down  again, 
And  up  with  living  or  dead  again! 
For  we  are  divers, 
The  Eastland  divers. 

The  poem,  which  is  a  pitiless  protest 
against  greed  and  the  inhumanity  to 
which  it  gives  birth,  begins : 

Divers  of  bodies, 
What  did  you  see, 
Amid  the  waters, 
The  sluggish  waters, 
The  swaying  company — 
You,  the  divers, 
The  Eastland  divers? 

In  different  vein,  are  Carl  Sandburg's 
Chicago  poems.  The  initial  one  bears 
the  title  Chicago,  which  is  given  this 
unlovely  characterization : 

Hog  Butcher  for  the  World, 

Tool  Maker,  Stacker  of  Wheat, 

Player    with    Railroads,    and    the    Nation's 

Freight  Handler; 
Stormy,  husky,  brawling, 
City  of  the  Big  Shoulders. 

Two  other  poems  of  Chicago,  both 
distinctive  as  to  subject  and  form,  are 
these : 

The  Chicago,  by  Charlton  Lawrence 
Edholm,  in  Everybody's. 

Street  in  Packingtown  (Chicago)  by 
Willa  Sibert  Gather,  in  The  Century. 

The  Chicago 
Charlton  Lawrence  Edholm 

They  have  bound  me  with  bridges, 
With   tunnels  burrowed   under  me! 
Incessant,  unresting, 

All  day  and  all  night 

Traffic  roars  over  me, 

And  my  uplook  to  the  blessed  sky 

Is  barred  with  girders,  cables,  stacks. 

My  banks,  with  docks  close  hedged, 

Inexorably 

Hem  me  in. 

Vacantly, 

Through  smoke  and  floating  smudge, 

The  Sun  looks  down  upon  me 


Like  the  bleared  eye  of  an  old,  old  man. 
No  outcast  of  the  gutters 
Slinks  by  more  soiled  than  I, 
Polluted  within  and  without! 

But  on  my  shackled  breast  I  bear 

Corn  and  iron,  lumber  and  coal. 

The  little  children  of  India  eat  of  my 
wheat; 

My  lumber  shelters  the  stricken  of  Mes- 
sina; 

Ten  million  wheels  are  set  a-whirl  with 
my  coal; 

The  iron  that  burdens  me  forms  a  ready 
tool, 

Fit  for  the  hand  of  man. 

What  singer  can  sing  of  me  one  low-keyed 
song? 

The  Hudson,  the  Rhine,  the  Danube,  the 
Nile, 

All  these,  all  have  their  poets, 

As  beautiful  women  their  lovers. 

Fringed  with  vineyards  and  stately  gar- 
dens, 

Castles  and  temples  are  their  jewels, 

And  song  is  theirs  by  right! 

But  I? 

Soiled  am  I  and  brackish 
As  sweat  on  the  brow  of  a  workman! 
But  the  broad  ships  that  weight  my  breast 
Are   like   iron   medals   with   these   words 

wrought: 

"FOR  SERVICE." 
Therein  alone  is  my  glory: 
I  serve;  I  serve. 

Street  in  Packingtown 

(Chicago) 

Willa  Sibert  Gather 

In  the  gray  dust  before  a  frail  gray  shed, 
By  a  board  fence  obscenely  chalked  in  red, 
A  gray  creek  willow,  left  from  country  days, 
Flickers  pallid  in  the  haze. 

Beside  the  gutter  of  the  unpaved  street, 
Tin  cans  and  broken  glass  about  his  feet, 
And  a  brown  whisky  bottle,  singled  out 
For    play    from    prosier    crockery    strewn 

about, 

Twisting  a  shoestring  noose,  a  Polack's  brat 
Joylessly  torments  a  cat. 

His  dress,  some  sister's  cast-off  wear, 

Is  rolled  to  leave  his  stomach  bare. 

His  arms  and  legs  with  scratches  bleed; 

He  twists  the  cat  and  pays  no  heed. 

He  mauls  her  neither  less  nor  more 

Because  her  claws  have  raked  him  sore. 

His  eyes,  faint-blue  and  moody,  stare 

From  under  a  pale  shock  of  hair. 

Neither  resentment  nor  surprise 

Lights  the  desert  of  those  eyes — 

To  hurt  and  to  be  hurt;  he  knows 

All  he  will  know  on  earth,  or  need  to  know. 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


But  there,  beneath  his  willow-tree, 
His  tribal,  tutelary  tree, 
The  tortured  cat  across  his  knee, 
With  hate,  perhaps,  a  threat,  maybe, 
Lithuania  looks  at  me. 

Notable  also  among  the  most  recent 
productions  of  the  Chicago  poets,  and 
dealing  with  a  Chicago  subject,  is  the 
poem  occupying  two  pages  in  a  late  num- 
ber of  the  New  Republic  entitled  "The 
Loop,"  by  Edgar  Lee  Masters. 

Of  Chicago  periodicals  interesting  in 
this  connection :  Miss  Harriet  Monroe's 
monthly  magazine  Poetry  has  been 
published  regularly  for  two  years,  a 
group  of  poetry  lovers  having  sub- 
scribed liberally  to  enable  publication 
for  a  period  of  five  years  without  re- 
gard to  general  support;  The  Little 
Review,  edited  by  Miss  Margaret  An- 
derson, has  appeared  more  or  less  regu- 
larly for  more  than  a  year,  and  has 
given  much  prominence  to  poetry ;  The 
Trimmed  Lamp,  which  began  as  an  art 
brochure,  has  been  enlarged  and  in- 
cludes much  original  poetry ;  at  in- 
tervals The  Dial  devotes  several  pages 
to  reviews  of  current  poetry.  It  is  also 
significant  that  one  of  the  local  book- 
stores has  maintained  a  "Poetry  Table" 
for  a  year  past,  in  response  to  increas- 
ing demands  for  that  type  of  literature. 
Substantial  prizes  have  been  awarded 
through  Poetry  and  The  Trimmed 
Lamp,  by  anonymous  donors,  for  origi- 
nal contributions  of  verse  meeting  cer- 
tain tests. 

Elsewhere,  too,  there  is  a  notable 
tendency  to  revival  of  interest  in  mod- 
ern poetry.  Of  the  general  magazines, 
two  maintain  regular  departments  on 
the  subject: 

Current  Opinion — Voices  of  the  liv- 
ing poets,  among  well-known  names 
represented  recently  in  this  grouping 
being  Rupert  Brooke,  Sara  Teasdale, 
Don  Marquis,  Edith  Thomas,  Vachel 
Lindsay,  Antonio  Giovannitti,  Richard 
LeGallienne. 

Literary    Digest  —  Current    Poetry, 


among  recent  examples  quoted  being 
poems  by  Joyce  Kilmer,  Robert  Frost, 
Bliss  Carman,  Arthur  Guiterman, 
Charles  Hanon  Towne. 

The  English  Review  gives  leading 
position  to  poems  in  each  monthly  is- 
sue, John  Masefield's  poems  "The  Daf- 
fodil Fields,"  and  "Dauber"  having 
first  appeared  in  print  on  the  first 
pages  of  this  magazine.  The  Egoist, 
a  monthly  magazine  published  in  Lon- 
don, fosters  "the  newest  and  best  ex- 
perimental English  and  American 
poetry."  Previous  to  the  war,  the 
Poetry  Book  Shop  in  London,  had  on 
its  shelves  books  of  poetry  only.  Since 
the  war's  beginning  a  large  volume  of 
poetry  inspired  by  the  great  conflict 
has  appeared,  some  of  it  in  the  form  of 
broadsides,  chapbooks  and  garlands 
sold  at  from  twopence  to  sixpence.  In 
general,  however,  literature  has  lan- 
guished. D.  H.  Lawrence  recently 
wrote:  "How  is  poetry  going  in 
America?  There  is  none  in  England; 
the  muse  has  gone  like  the  swallows  in 
winter." 

On  this  side  of  the  great  water,  the 
reverse  situation  is  true.  A  Poetry 
Society  of  America  has  been  organized, 
with  monthly  meetings.  The  Century. 
Scribner's  and  other  leading  magazines 
are  giving  increased  prominence  to 
poetry.  So  are  certain  lesser  periodi- 
cals, as  for  instance  Reedy's  Mirror, 
and  the  Bellman,  and,  among  other 
newspapers,  the  Boston  Transcript. 
Alfred  Noyes  is  lecturing  on  poetry  at 
the  universities,  and  John  Masefield  is 
coming  to  lecture.  The  second  annual 
issue  of  William  Stanley  Braithwaite's 
Anthology  of  Magazine  Verse  has  just 
appeared.  In  this  resume  for  1915,  the 
compiler  has  indexed  530  poets  and 
about  1500  poems.  In  a  summary  of 
thirteen  selected  magazines,  he  lists 
772  poems,  and  310  of  them  are  called 
poems  of  distinction. 

One  of  the  curious  tendencies  ap- 
parent in  this  great  output  of  melody 
in  minor  chords,  is  the  growth  of  the 
Imagist  School.

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate ;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  are  in 
the  Document  department.     *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

BOOK  BULLETIN

Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
draw  books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card 
thus  issued,  any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non- 
fiction  card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw 
one  book,  not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued 
on  the  regular  card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the 
same  time,  enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction 
books,  or  one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on 
the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made 
for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction, 
of  the  Librarian.

Harry    G.    Wilso* Secretary 

Henry    E.    Legler Librarian 

Carl    B.    Roden Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  "Bound  Newspapers  Room,  irst  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance ; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington  street  en- 
trance ;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8  :30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 


Helps  for  Parents  and  Teachers 

In  the  course  of  a  year  many  score  requests  come  by  mail  to  the  Library 
from  parents  and  teachers  who  wish  to  secure  book  titles  suitable  for  boys  and 
girls  of  certain  ages.  Many  more  inquiries  come  by  telephone.  With  a  view  to 
giving  first  aid  to  bookbuyers  for  juvenile  readers,  the  publication  of  a  series 
of  brief  booklists  suggested  for  young  people  is  begun  in  this  issue  of  The 
Bulletin.  Two  lists  are  herewith  given :

What's  in  the  Magazines 


III.    The  Fine  Arts 

Magazines  devoted  to  the  arts  and 
crafts  are  currently  on  file  in  the  Arts 
and  Crafts  Room,  fifth  floor  west,  of 
the  Central  Library.    These  are  many 
in  number,  and  include  some  sumptu- 
ously illustrated.    As  there  are  in  Chi- 
cago thousands  of  art  students  engaged 
in  developing  such  talent  as  they  pos- 
sess, naturally  there  is  large  demand 
for  related  magazine  literature.    There 
are  other  local  reasons  why  this  cur- 
rent material  in  print  bears  interest  for 
readers  in  Chicago.    The  Art  Institute 
is  recognized  in  its  policies  as  the  most 
democratic  establishment  of  its   type 
in  the  world,  as  well  as  the  one  whose 
attendance  outranks  that  of  any  other 
in  the  United  States.     Chicago  is  the 
home   of   sculptors,   portrait   painters, 
etchers,  and  arts  craftsmen  whose  work 
and  whose  names  are  known  far  be- 
yond the  borders  of  the  state.    Among 
the   eminent  sculptors   may   be   men- 
tioned   Lorado   Taft,    whose    Midway 
Studio  is  the  rallying  point  for  a  group 
of  promising  pupils ;  Leonard  Crunelle 
and  Charles  Mulligan.  Ralph  Clarkson, 
Louis  Betts,  Lawton  Parker  and  Wil- 
liam  Penhallow   Henderson   have  na- 
tional reputations  as  portrait  painters, 
while  the  canvases  of  Karl  A.  Buehr, 
Charles    Francis    Brown    and    Wilson 
Irvine  have  made  their   reputations   as 
artists  in  the  East  as  well  as  in  the  Mid- 
dle West,  and  the  child  portraiture  of 
Adam   Emory  Albright  has   won   de- 
served recognition   from  connoisseurs 
elsewhere  as  well  as  here.    Earl  Reed's 
etchings  of  the  south  shore  dunes,  and 
his  poetic  interpretation  of  them,  have 
established    his    fame,    and    there    are 
other  etchers  whose  distinctive  work 
has  been   given  deserved  recognition. 
Recently,    the    Stockyards    Colony   of 
Artists  came  into  existence  in  an  as- 
sociated endeavor  that  led  to  the  open- 
ing of  a  gallery  in  the  stockyards  re- 
gion, and  now  the  art  lover  makes  pil- 
grimages from  the  numerous  studios 


on  the  boulevard  to  the  busy  region 
which  is  a  world  of  its  own  on  the 
south  side. 

The  following  periodicals  giving  spe- 
cial attention  to  art  subjects  may  be 
consulted  in  the  Arts  and  Crafts  Room, 
the  nature  of  the  periodicals  being  in- 
dicated by  a  selection  of  articles  which 
have  appeared  recently: 
Plakat.  Bi-monthly.  Berlin. 

Deals  with  posters,  advertisements,  poster  stamps, 
etc. ;  many  colored  plates. 

Connoisseur.  An  illustrated  magazine  for 
collectors.  Monthly.  London. 

English  seals.  Some  unpublished  Lawrence  por- 
traits. Fuddling  cups  and  puzzle  jugs,  with  some 
notes  on  Wincanton  Delft.  The  Oxenden  collec- 
tion. Courtship  and  Matrimony  in  Staffordshire 
pottery.  The  art  of  the  cofferer.  Gold-splash 
bronzes. 

Burlington  magazine.  Monthly.  London. 
A  magazine  for  connoisseurs  and  col- 
lectors. 

Tapestries  at  Eastnor.  Russian  periodicals  (Re- 
views of  articles  upon  architecture,  paintings, 
monuments,  etc.,  in  Russia).  T'ang  pottery  and 
its  late  classic  affinities.  Persian  domes  before  1400 
A.  D.  Notes  on  pictures  in  the  Royal  collections 
(England). 

Art  and  archaeology.  An  illustrated  mag- 
azine, published  every  other  month  at 
Baltimore,  Md.,  and  Washington,  D.  C, 
by  the  Archaeological  Institute  of  Amer- 
ica. 

Masterpieces  of  aboriginal  American  art.  A  re- 
cently discovered  painted  tomb  of  Palestine.  The 
Roman  Theatre.  Thomas  Jefferson  and  the  origins 
of  the  classical  revival  in  America.  Taylor  Hall: 
The  new  art  building  at  Vassar  College. 

The  Print  collector's  quarterly.  Boston. 
Deals  with  etchings,  colored  prints,  mez- 
zotints and  engravings  of  all  schools  and 
periods,  together  with  biographies  of 
artists,  engravers,  etc. 

John  La  Forge,  illustrator.  Rembrandt's  Amster- 
dam. William  Blake,  and  his  water-color  drawings 
in  the  Museum  of  Fine  Arts,  Boston.  German 
wood  cuts  of  the  Fifteenth  Century. 

Kunstwart.     Semi-monthly.     Munich. 

Organ  of  the  Durer  Bund  devoted  to  elevation 
of  popular  taste  in  fine  arts,  literature,  music, 
life.  Unique  in  its  objects  and  methods.  Edited 
by  Ferdinand  Avenarius,  well-known  advocate  of 
good  art  and  good  taste. 

Moderne  Bauformen.  Monthly.  Stuttgart, 
Germany. 

Devoted  to  architecture  and  house  decoration. 
Not  much  reading  matter.  Mostly  plates,  some  in

On  Washington's  Birthday,  Tuesday,  Feb. 
22d,  the  circulating  department  will  be 
closed.  Reading  and  reference  rooms  will 
be  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.;  branches 
from  2  to  6  p.  m.

adventurer  for  Italian  independence.

of  the  art  world).

or  needle  tapestry.  Early  American  crafts — Deco-

Modernist  school  of  interior  decoration.  American 
etchers  of  the  present  day.

BOOK  BULLETIN. 


31 


Stbranj,  Mulligan  Auntu*  and  HJaalftngtiw 


and  Jhrfurmatimi  for  JJatmus 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
draw  books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card 
thus  issued,  any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non- 
fiction  card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw 
one  book,  not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued 
on  the  regular  card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the 
same  time,  enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction 
books,  or  one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on 
the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made 
for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington  street  en- 
trance; open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 


New  Finding  Lists  at  Reduced  Cost 


With  a  view  to  as  wide  a  distribution 
as  possible  of  the  Library's  finding  lists, 
a  material  reduction  in  the  prices  of  all 
of  them  has  been  authorized  by  the 
Board  of  Directors.  An  important  series 
of  issues  has  been  projected  for  1916; 
the  first  of  these  has  just  come  from  the 
press,  being  Part  I  of  Books  Added, 
1911-1915,  a  five-year  cumulation  of 
the  fifty  monthly  lists  which  have 
appeared  in  the  Book  List  during  the 
years  above  included.  Inasmuch  as 
the  book  titles  added  in  1915  have  been 
included,  the  annual  cumulation  will  be 
omitted  this  year.  The  quinquennial 
cumulation  will  comprise  11,000  titles, 
and  will  be  issued  in  three  parts,  as 
follows : 

Part  I — Fiction,  embracing  2500  titles, 
with  names  of  authors  and  titles  ar- 
ranged alphabetically.  Now  ready  for 
distribution. 

Part  II — Juvenile,  comprising  1500 
titles,  with  authors  and  titles  in 
one  alphabetical  arrangement.  Ready 
March  15. 


Part  III— Non-fiction,  7000  titles, 
grouped  in  classes  as  follows:  Phil- 
osophy, Religion,  Social  Sciences,  Lan- 
guage, Literature,  History,  Biography, 
Travel,  Useful  Arts  and  Fine  Arts. 
This  part  will  be  supplied  with  an  ad- 
mirable author,  title  and  subject  index 
in  one  alphabet.  Ready  about  April  1. 

About  April  15  will  be  issued  an 
important  indexed  finding  list  compris- 
ing all  the  titles  in  the  Library  on  the 
subjects  of  Philosophy,  Religion  and 
Medicine. 

The  first  two  select  topical  lists  of  a 
number  planned  for  1916  have  just 
been  issued.  They  are : 

William  Shakespeare,  1616-1916;  giv- 
ing collected  editions  in  the  Library, 
including  translations  into  German, 
French,  Italian,  Bohemian,  Danish, 
Dutch,  and  Yiddish;  separate  works, 
commentaries,  and  works  about  the 
great  dramatist;  32  pages. 

Actable  One-Act  Plays  (135  titles); 
16  pages. 

At  intervals  during  the  year,  these 
publications  will  be  followed  by  sepa- 


34

rate  lists  of  books  in  foreign  languages, 
books  for  the  blind,  etc.

What's  in  the  Magazines 


IV.  Actor  Folk  and  Play  Writers 
Evidence  of  the  great  interest  mani- 
fested in  the  mimic  life  portrayed 
behind  footlights  is  to  be  found  in  the 
place  and  space  given  by  many  general 
magazines  to  the  new  dramas  that  are 
written  and  produced,  the  personnel  of 
the  actors  and  authors,  and  within 
recent  times  the  related  gossip  of  the 
cinema  world.  Regular  departments 
of  dramatic  comment  and  criticism 
contributed  by  authorities  of  national 
reputation  are  maintained  by  some  of 
the  monthly  and  weekly  magazines, 
and  numerous  periodicals  are  devoted 
exclusively  to  these  subjects.  So  exten- 
sive is  the  ephemeral  literature  of  the 
drama,  spoken  and  pictorial,  that  a 
quarterly  dramatic  index  is  issued  to 
serve  as  guide  to  articles,  reviews  and 
pictures  appearing  in  fifty-nine  period- 
icals. These  references  are  gathered 
annually  in  the  form  of  a  Dramatic 
Index  issued  as  a  volume. 

At  least  two  general  magazines  print 
each  month  a  summarized  narrative  of 
the  leading  play  of  the  day,  usually 
with  liberal  extracts  from  the  text; 
others  print  descriptions  and  excerpts. 
Among  the  recent  articles  which  have 
appeared  recently  are  the  following: 
Plots— 

A  thousand  years  ago.    Munsey's 

Adored  one.     Graphic 

At  bay.     Leslie's  Weekly 


Lure.     Everybody's 

Her  own  money.    Delineator 

Never  say  die.     Graphic 

Half  an  hour.     Everybody's 
Text— 

At  the  chasm.    Poet  Lore 

Pied  Piper  of  Hamelin.     Etude 
Excerpts — 

Moloch.    Theatre 

Seven  Keys  to  Baldpate.  Current  Opin- 
ion 

Prunella,  or,  Love  in  a  Garden.  Current 
Opinion 

Marie-Odile.    Hearst's  Magazine 
Description — 

Interlopers.     Graphic 

Along  came  Ruth.    Life 

Grumpy.     Collier's 

Mr.  Wu.     Graphic 

Philanderers.    Collier's 

Criticisms  of  plays  produced  for  the 
first  time  in  Chicago  appear  in  the  local 
press  regularly  with  complete  cast  of 
characters.  The  Theatre,  with  excel- 
lent half-tone  illustrations  of  represen- 
tative scenes  from  new  plays,  also 
gives  the  complete  casts.  Among  some 
of  the  new  plays  thus  depicted,  and 
noted,  may  be  mentioned  the  follow- 
ing: 

Yeoman  of  the  guard 

Nobody  home 

A  full  house 

The  hyphen 

Stolen  orders 

The  eternal  Magdalene 

Our  Mrs.  McChesney 

Among  the  plays  which  have  been

printed  with  complete  text  in  period- 
icals of  recent  date  are  the  following: 

Play-Book: 

Wakuwapi;     the     Hunting — an     Indian 

story,  by  Hartley  Alexander 
The  March;   a  tragedy  in  one  act,  by 

Laura  Sherry 
Orange   Blossoms,   by   Phillips   Chyno- 

weth

Drama: 

Squabbles    of    Chioggia,    by    Goldoni, 

translated  by  Charles  Lemmi 
Henry  IV  of  Germany,  by  Ernest  von

Kayoi  Komachi,  by  Ernest  Fenellosa 

The  Theatre  has  been  giving  ex- 
tended mention  to  the  new  dramatists 
and  their  work,  notably  the  product  of 
Prof.  Baker's  famous  school  of  play- 
wrights at  Harvard.  Among  recent 
articles  are  the  following: 

Max  Marcin.    House  of  glass 
Frederick  Ballard.    Young  America 
Cleves  Kinkead.    Common  clay 

The  Dramatist  Quarterly  attempts 
an  outline  of  plots  of  all  recent  plays, 
the  file  for  the  past  two  years  includ- 
ing seventy-six  plots.  Each  analysis 
states  the  problem  in  three  lines,  the 
two  specimen  extracts  which  follow 
indicating  the  general  type : 

The  Third  Floor  Back 

1.  A  symbol  of  brotherly  love  encounters 

selfish  souls. 

2.  He   insists  on  seeing  only  the  best   in 

them. 

3.  His  faith  usually  rehabilitates  them. 


A  Bachelor's  Romance 

1.  A  bachelor  awards  a  prize  to  his  rival. 

2.  The  money  demoralizes  him. 

3.  The  bachelor  gets  the  girl. 

An  interesting  feature  of  the  dra- 
matic evolution  now  in  progress  is  to 
be  found  in  the  currently  printed  ar- 
ticles about  the  diminutive  play-houses, 
whose  productions  in  dramaturgy  are 
analogous  to  the  movement  of  the 
cubists  and  futurists  in  art,  and  the 
imagistes  in  poetry.  Among  them  may 
be  mentioned  Maurice  Browne's  Little 
Theatre  in  Chicago,  with  its  seating 
capacity  of  99;  Mrs.  Edith  Stewart's 
Bluebird  Theatre  in  Milwaukee;  the 
Little  Playhouse  in  Cincinnati;  the 
Bandbox  Theatre  of  New  York;  the 
Portmanteau  Theatre,  recently  set  up 
in  Evanston ;  Providence's  Talma 
Theatre;  Boston's  Toy  Theatre;  of 
special  local  interest,  the  Outdoor 
Theatre  of  the  Lake  Forest  Players; 
and,  perhaps,  of  special  importance  in 
the  development  of  the  experimental 
drama,  the  47  Workshop  at  Harvard, 
managed  by  Prof.  George  Pierce 
Baker. 

It  is  not  surprising  that  a  large  circle 
of  readers  is  interested  in  the  current 
literature  of  the  drama.  The  Drama 
League,  which  originated  in  Evanston, 
is  now  a  national  organization  with 
branches  in  all  the  leading  cities,  and  a 
membership  of  many  thousands.  The 
theater-going  public  is  large,  and  the 
moving  picture  public  still  larger.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Civil  Service  Examinations 


Examinations  were  recently  held  by 
the  Civil  Service  Commission  in  four 
of  the  six  grades  of  Library  service. 
Three  were  promotional  examinations 
from  one  grade  to  the  next  higher, 
while  that  for  Grade  I  was  for  original 
entrance.  Following  are  the  questions : 

Grade  I.    Junior  Library  Assistants 

1.  What  and  where  is: 

Culebra  Cut 

Saloniki 

Tuskegee 

Dardanelles 

Alsace 

2.  Characterize  briefly: 

James  Whitcomb  Riley 
Claude  B.  Kitchen 
Henry  Ford 
Booker  T.  Washington 
Robert  Lansing 

3.  (a)  Who  constitute  the  legislative  branch 

of  the  city  government? 

(b)  What    official    heads    the    executive 
branch  of  the  city  government? 

(c)  What    are    the    chief    duties    of    the 
executive  head? 

(d)  What  comprises  the  judicial  branch 
of  the  city  government? 

4.  How    are    individual    biographies,    auto- 
biographies   and    diaries    classified    and 
numbered,  using  the  Dewey  decimal  clas- 
sification, and  the  Cutter  author  tables; 
how    are    criticisms    of    authors'    works 
treated  with  the  same  systems? 

5.  Describe  briefly  the  following  magazines, 
and  what  they  represent: 

Atlantic  Monthly 

Independent 

Dial 

Bookman 

Current  Opinion 

6.  What    is    meant    by    analytic    entries    in 
cataloging,    and   what    is    their   value    in 
branches? 

7.  Describe  briefly,  giving  essential  charac- 
teristics: 

(a)  The  Congressional  record 

(b)  Reader's   guide   to   periodical   litera- 
ture 

(c)  Carnegie  library  of  Pittsburgh — Cat- 
alog of  books  in   the   children's   de- 
partment. 

(d)  A.  L.  A.  Booklist. 

8.  (a)  What  is  meant  by  the  following,  as 

exemplified  in  Chicago:  (Employ 
about  twenty-five  words  to  describe 
each) 

(a)  Package  library 

(b)  Traveling  library 

(c)  Branch  library 

(d)  Class-room  library 


(b)  Define: 

(a)  Anthology 

(b)  Bibliography 

(c)  Reference  book 

(d)  Cross  reference 

(e)  Book  card 

9.  Name  one  novel  by  each  of  the  following: 

Samuel  Richardson 
Jane  Austen 
George  Eliot 
Anthony  Trollope 
Charles  Kingsley 
Charles  Reade 
Charlotte  Bronte 
Thomas  Hardy 
George  Meredith 
Eden  Philpotts 

10.  Give  Decimal  classification  numbers  for 
the  following  subjects: 

Engineering,  French  drama,  English 
history,  American  essays,  Bible,  Educa- 
tion, Zoology,  Painting,  Domestic  science, 
Architecture.

take     for    library    participation     in     the 
Shakespeare  Tercentenary  celebration  iri 
1916.

Shakespeare.

or
