Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
draw  books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card 
thus  issued,  any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non- 
fiction  card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw 
one  book,  not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued 
on  the  regular  card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the 
same  time,  enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction 
books,  or  one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on 
the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made 
for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy.

Sunday.

floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.

floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday.

closed  on  Sunday.

closed  on  Sunday.

6  p.  m.

trance; open  9  a.  m.  to  8 : 30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 


Wanted — One  Million  Dollars 

Many  additional  branch  library  buildings  are  needed  in  Chicago.  A  million 
dollars  would  purchase  sites  and  enable  the  construction  of  suitable  buildings 
in  sufficient  number  to  supply  the  city  with  a  system  comparable  to  those  of 
New  York,  Brooklyn,  Philadelphia,  Pittsburgh,  Cincinnati,  Cleveland,  Detroit, 
St.  Louis,  Denver,  Minneapolis,  Seattle,  Portland,  or  Los  Angeles. 

At  the  present  time,  the  Chicago  Library  possesses  fewer  branch  buildings 
than  any  one  of  the  cities  named,  fewer  than  Louisville,  Indianapolis,  Balti- 
more or  New  Orleans — and  indeed  a  less  number  than  may  be  found  in  many 
cities  of  less  than  100,000  inhabitants.  In  each  of  the  first-named  list  of  mu- 
nicipalities, attractive  and  imposing  structures  costing  from  $50,000  to  $200,000 
each,  have  been  erected,  chiefly  from  gifts  received  from  philanthropic  sources. 
New  York  owns  43  such  buildings  in  addition  to  the  Central  building ;  Brook- 
lyn, 30;  Philadelphia,  25;  Boston,  13;  Pittsburgh,  8;  Cleveland,  12;  St.  Louis, 
7;  Detroit,  9;  Los  Angeles,  15. 

The  three  branch  library  buildings  which  belong  to  the  Chicago  Public 
Library  and  their  cost,  inclusive  of  sites,  are  the  following: 

T.  B.  Blackstone  branch,  corner  Lake  avenue  and  Forty-ninth  street, 
$250,000.  Gift  of  Mrs.  T.  B.  Blackstone. 

Hiram  Kelly  branch,  corner  Normal  avenue  and  Sixty-second  street,  $75,- 
000.  The  late  Hiram  Kelly  bequeathed  $200,000  for  the  use  of  the  Public 
Library. 

George  H.  Walker  branch,  Morgan  Park,  $25,000.  Gift  of  the  late  Geo. 
H.  Walker. 

Of  the  other  branch  libraries  maintainedjjpy  tfie  Chicago  Public  Library, 
nineteen  are  in  park  field  houses,  six  are  in  scmool  buildings,  two  are  in  insti- 


50 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


tutional  establishments,  and  seven  are  in  rented  quarters.  The  last-named,  hav- 
ing been  placed  with  special  regard  to  need  and  convenience,  enjoy  the  largest 
circulations  of  books  for  home  use,  but  involve  an  annual  outlay  of  $10,000 
for  rentals  which  preferably  might  be  expended  for  book  purchases  if  the 
city  owned  the  buildings  instead  of  being  called  upon  to  pay  rent.  Another 
disadvantage  is  the  insecurity  as  to  permanence  which  attaches  to  leased  prem- 
ises. Moreover,  it  is  difficult  to  provide  adequate  library  service  in  quarters 
not  planned  for  the  purpose. 

Having  in  view  a  standardized  plan  that  shall  yield  a  maximum  of  space 
and  convenience  at  a  minimum  of  cost  for  construction  as  well  as  of  admin- 
istration, the  Library  Board  has  adopted  plans  for  the  initial  branch  library 
building  of  what  it  is  hoped  may  grow  into  a  system  of  branch  libraries  cov- 
ering the  entire  city.  Each  is  to  cost  not  to  exceed  $25,000,  including  purchase 
of  well-located  site  and  erection  of  suitable  building,  providing  approximate- 
ly 5,000  square  feet  of  floor  space  and  containing  separate  reading  rooms  for 
adult  and  juvenile  readers,  story  hour  nook,  reference  facilities  and  circulation 
department — practically  all  the  accommodations,  except  auditoriums,  included 
in  branch  library  buildings  costing  from  two  to  six  times  this  expenditure. 

The  new  building  is  to  be  located  in  the  heart  of  Woodlawn.  Plans  for 
buildings  elsewhere  will  have  to  wait  until  additional  funds  not  now  available 
can  be  secured.  In  an  article  by  Mr.  Henry  M.  Hyde  recently  printed  in  the 
Chicago  Tribune,  gifts  of  not  less  than  a  million  dollars  each  are  credited  to 
twenty-three  Chicago  philanthropists.  The  name  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library 
does  not  appear  in  the  list  of  beneficiaries. 

A  gift  of  one  million  dollars  would  provide  forty  branch  library  buildings 
for  Chicago's  population  of  two  and  a  half  million  inhabitants,  and  lesser 
amounts  would  yield  branch  libraries  in  proportion. 


What's  in  the  Magazines 


V:  The  Story  Tellers 

Few  periodicals  general  in  character 
fail  to  make  a  feature  of  fiction.  From 
the  time,  two  hundred  years  ago,  when 
DeFoe's  Robinson  Crusoe  was  printed 
serially  in  Heathcote's  Intelligencer, 
until  the  present  era  of  many  maga- 
zines, the  continued  story  has  been 
popular  with  readers,  and  profitable  for 
publishers.  Mr.  Henry  Mills  Alden, 
for  many  years  editor  of  Harper's 
Monthly,  holds  that  the  novel  and  the 
monthly  magazine  emerged  during 
the  same  generation. 

It  must  be  admitted  that  the  early- 
day  fiction  that  appeared  in  serial  form 
was  long-winded  and  tiresome  accord- 
ing to  present-day  standards.  But  in 
the  days  of  the  old  English  coffee- 
houses and  inns,  even  the  tedious  nov- 
els of  the  time  appealed  with  extraor- 


dinary interest  to  the  sympathies  of 
all  classes.  Some  of  the  finest  tales 
and  romances  of  the  Victorian  period 
first  appeared  in  the  pages  of  the  mag- 
azines— among  them  novels  by  Charles 
Lever,  Bulwer,  George  Eliot,  William 
Makepiece  Thackeray,  Anthony  Trol- 
lope,  Thomas  Hardy,  and  Charles  Dick- 
ens. These,  and  others  by  lesser  writ- 
ers like  Samuel  Lover,  William  Harri- 
son Ainsworth,  Samuel  Warren  and 
their  contemporaries,  may  be  found  in 
the  old  files  of  Blackwood's  Magazine, 
Bentley's  Miscellany,  Cornhill,  Fras- 
er's,  All  the  Year  Round  and  House- 
hold Words,  as  well  as  the  early  num- 
bers of  the  then  new  American  maga- 
zines, Harper's  and  Atlantic. 

Much  of  the  notable  fiction  contrib- 
uted by  the  well-known  writers  of  this 
and  the  previous  generation — Steven- 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


61 


son,  Meredith,  James,  Howells,  Con- 
rad, Hewlett,  Galsworthy,  Bennett, 
Mrs.  Humphrey  Ward,  Kipling — has 
been  given  to  readers  on  the  instalment 
plan  before  being  put  between  the 
covers  of  a  book.  Among  the  "best 
sellers"  of  the  past  five  years,  the  fol- 
lowing are  a  few  of  those  that  appeared 
previously  as  magazine  serials: 

Burnett.  T.  Tembarom.  Century,  Jan. 
1913-Dec.  1913. 

Locke.  Stella  Maris.  Century,  Tan.  1912- 
Dec.  1912. 

Tarkington.  The  turmoil.  Harper's  Mag- 
azine, Aug.  1914-Mar.  1915. 

King.  The  side  of  the  angels.  Harper's 
Magazine,  Aug.  1915-Apr.  1916. 

Parker.  The  judgment  house.  Harper's 
Magazine,  Aug.  1912-May  1913. 

Snaith.  The  principal  girl.  Atlantic  Month- 
ly, Nov.  1911-May  1912. 

Fox.  The  heart  of  the  hills.  Scribner's 
Magazine,  Apr.  1912-Mar.  1913. 

Wharton.  The  custom  of  the  country. 
Scribner's  Magazine,  Jan.  1913-Nov.  1913. 

Galsworthy.  The  dark  flower.  Scribner's, 
Apr.  1913-Oct.  1913. 

Smith.  Kennedy  Square.  Scribner's,  Nov. 
1910-Oct.  1911. 

Beach.  The  ne'er-do-well.  Everybody's, 
Oct.  1910-Sept.  1911. 

Ward.  The  case  of  Richard  Meynell.  Mc- 
Clure's,  May  1911-Nov.  1911. 

Johnson.  Stover  at  Yale.  McClure's,  Nov. 
1911-May  1912. 

Wells.  Marriage.  American  Magazine, 
Nov.  1911-Oct.  1912. 

Bennett.  The  regent.  American  Maga- 
zine, Dec.  1912-July  1913. 

In  the  realm  of  the  short  story,  the 
American  magazine  is  predominant. 
In  a  review  of  the  short  stories  of  1915 
contributed  by  Mr.  Edward  J.  O'Brien 
to  a  Boston  paper,  he  admitted  that 
during  the  past  year  he  had  read  more 
than  two  thousand  American  short 
stories  in  a  critical  spirit.  He  added, 
in  his  review : 

"In  reaffirming  this  year  the  signifi- 
cant position  of  the  American  short 
story  as  compared  with  the  English 
short  story,  I  am  more  impressed  than 
ever  with  the  leadership  manifested  by 
American  artists  in  this  literary  form. 
During  the  past  year  I  have  read  over 
two  thousand  American  short  stories 
in  a  critical  spirit,  and  they  have  made 


me  lastingly  hopeful  of  our  literary  fu- 
ture. A  spirit  of  change  is  acting  on 
our  literature.  There  is  a  fresh  living 
current  in  the  air." 

"Of  the  2200  short  stories  which  I 
examined  thoroughly  in  my  study  of 
forty-six  periodicals,  I  find  that  683 
stories  possessed  distinction,  269  stor- 
ies possessed  high  distinction,  and  91 
stories  were  of  more  or  less  permanent 
literary  value." 

In  order  of  precedence,  based  on  the 
number  of  new  stories  of  distinctive 
merit  published  during  1915,  the  eigh- 
teen magazines  publishing  over  twen- 
ty-five short  stories  during  the  year 
rank  as  follows : 

1.  Harper's    Monthly 56 

2.  Collier's   Weekly  and  Illustrated- 

Sunday  Magazine  (excluding  13 
reprints)     46 

3.  Scribner's    Magazine 37 

4.  Lippincott's    Magazine    and    Mc- 

Bride's   Magazine 36 

5.  Century   Magazine 32 

6.  Saturday  Evening  Post 29 

7.  Metropolitan  24 

8.  Every  Week 23 

9.  American    Magazine 22 

10.  McClure's   Magazine 22 

11.  The   Bellman 20 

12.  Pictorial  Review 15 

13.  Sunset    Magazine 13 

14.  Everybody's   Magazine 13 

15.  Associated      Sunday      Magazines 

(Jan.-May,     excluding     stories 

in  Every  Week,  q.  v.) 9 

16.  Ladies'  Home  Journal 8 

17.  Delineator  7 

Mr.  O'Brien  picks  ninety-one  short 
stories  for  the  "Honor  Roll,"  of  which 
Katherine  Fullerton  Gerould  has  con- 
tributed five  stories,  Wilbur  Daniel 
Steele  four  stories,  Lord  Dunsany,  Ben 
Hecht  and  Fannie  Hurst  three  stories 
each,  and  Lincoln  Colcord,  Rupert 
Hughes,  Walter  J.  Mullenburg,  Seu- 
mas  O'Brien  and  Robert  W.  Sneddon 
two  stories  each. 

The  best  five  stories  of  the  year  are, 
in  his  opinion: 

Zelig.  By  Benjamin  Rosenblatt.  The  Bell- 
man. 

Ultima  Thule.  By  John  Galsworthy.  The 
Delineator. 

The  Friends.  By  Stacy  Aumonier.  The 
Century. 


62 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Jeanne  the  Maid.  By  Gordon  Arthur  Smith. 
Scribner's. 

The  Weaver  Who  Clad  the  Summer.  By 
H.  M.  Lyon.  Illustrated  Sunday  Magazine. 

"One  new  periodical  and  one  new 
short  story  writer  claim  unique  at- 
tention this  year  for  their  recent 
achievement  and  abundant  future 
promise,"  says  Mr.  O'Brien.  "A  year 
ago,  a  slender  little  monthly  magazine 
entitled  The  Midland  was  first  issued 
in  Iowa  City.  It  attracted  very  little 
attention,  and  in  the  course  of  the  year 
published  but  nine  short  stories.  It 
has  been  my  pleasure  and  wonder  to 
find  in  these  nine  stories  the  most  vital 
interpretation  in  fiction  of  our  national 
life  that  many  years  have  been  able 
to  show." 

"One  new  short  story  writer  has  ap- 
peared this  year  whose  five  published 
stories  open  a  new  field  to  fiction,  and 
have  a  human  richness  of  feeling  and 
imagination  rare  in  our  over-sophisti- 
cated literature.  I  refer  to  the  fables 
of  Seumas  O'Brien.  At  first  one  is 
struck  with  their  utter  absence  of  form, 


and  then  one  realizes  that  this  is  a  con- 
scious art  that  wanders  truant  over  life 
and  imagination.  In  Seumas  O'Brien 
I  believe  that  America  has  found  a  new 
humorist  of  popular  sympathies,  a  rare 
observer  and  philosopher  whose  very 
absurdities  have  a  persuasive  philoso- 
phy of  their  own." 

Since  the  compilation  of  his  yearly 
summary,  Mr.  O'Brien  has  picked  eight 
short  stories  as  the  best  of  200  that 
appeared  in  the  February  and  March 
magazines  of  this  year.  The  titles  are : 

The  Conqueror,  by  Dorothy  Canfield. 
American,  March. 

Kerfol,  by  Edith  Wharton.  Scribner, 
March. 

Hands,  by  Sherwood  Anderson.  Masses, 
March. 

The  Silent  Infare,  by  Armistead  C.  Gor- 
don. Scribner,  March. 

Petit  Popo,  by  Robert  W.  Sneddon.  Bell- 
man, March  11. 

Infra  Dig,  by  Donal  Hamilton  Haines.  Out- 
look, March  1. 

The  Sleuth  of  the  Stars,  by  Melville  Davis- 
son  Post.  Saturday  Evening  Post,  March  4. 

The  Pruning  Knife,  by  Andrew  Soutar. 
Pictorial  Review,  March.

Examples  of  Japanese  Water  Colors 

Some  twenty-five  examples  of  the 
work  of  Japanese  students  in  the  To- 
kyo School  of  Fine  Arts  are  on  exhibit 
in  the  Arts  and  Crafts  Room  of  the 
Chicago  Public  Library.  Most  of  them 
are  water  colors  painted  on  silk  in  na- 
tive wood  frames  and  are  in  the  form 
of  wall  panels  three  to  five  feet  high 
and  one  to  three  feet  wide.  They  por- 
tray flowers,  leaves,  fruits  of  various 
kinds,  animal  forms  such  as  cats,  dogs, 
rabbits,  birds  and  chickens,  children, 
peasant  women,  conventional  designs 
and  the  like.  They  are  the  work  of  pu- 
pils from  sixteen  to  twenty-five  years 
of  age,  and  show  the  unmistakeable 
artistic  ability  of  the  Japanese  people. 
They  will  be  of  considerable  interest 
to  all  students  of  art  and  design,  espe- 
cially to  those  who  are  admirers  of 
things  Japanese. 

These  art  panels  formed  part  of  the 
Japanese  exhibit  at  the  San  Francisco 
exposition,  and  at  the  close  of  the  fair 
were  given  by  the  Japanese  govern- 
ment to  the  American  Library  Asso- 
ciation, whose  headquarters  are  located 
in  Chicago.  They  will  be  shown  at 
the  Chicago  Public  Library  for  the 
next  few  weeks,  and  will  then  probably 
be  lent  to  public  and  college  libraries 
in  other  parts  of  the  country.  The  ex- 
hibit is  on  the  fifth  floor  of  the  library, 
southwest  corner,  and  is  open  from  9 
to  5 :30  daily,  except  Sunday. 


Exhibits 

For  Shakespeare  Day,  April  23d,  the 
Library  is  preparing  an  exhibit  of  pic- 
tures, books  and  reference  lists  which 
will  be  displayed  in  the  Central  Li- 
brary and  afterwards  in  various 
branches. 

The  picture  bulletins  prepared  for 
Baby  Welfare  Week,  March  4th  to 
llth,  attracted  much  attention  as  part 
of  the  exhibition  at  the  Boston  Store; 
they  are  still  on  view  in  the  delivery 
room  of  the  Central  Library.

Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
draw  books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card 
thus  issued,  any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non- 
fiction  card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw 
one  book,  not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued 
on  the  regular  card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the 
same  time,  enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction 
books,  or  one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on 
the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made 
for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington  street  en- 
trance ;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8 :30  p.  m.,  closed  OB  Sunday. 


Slides  on  Social  Subjects 


In  connection  with  the  "Chicago 
course"  in  the  public  schools,  many 
hundreds  of  teachers  have  profited 
from  a  splendid  collection  of  pictorial 
and  printed  material  gathered  together 
by  the  Civic  Education  Extension 
Committee.  Through  the  generosity 
of  Mr.  and  Mrs.  W.  F.  Dummer,  who 
have  done  much  to  foster  the  move- 
ment and  to  interest  teachers  in  the 
use  of  collateral  material,  a  considera- 
ble part  of  this  valuable  collection  has 
been  turned  over  to  the  Civics  Room  of 
the  Library  for  general  public  use. 

Included  in  the  collection  are  many 
maps  of  European  cities,  comprising 
the  principal  industrial  centers,  show- 
ing the  residence  zones,  the  sections 
designated  by  municipal  law  for  manu- 
facturing sites,  and  similar  apportion- 
ment of  corporate  areas  in  well-planned 
cities.  There  is  also  a  large  collection 
of  stereopticon  slides,  illustrative  of 
city  activities  in  Europe  and  the  United 
States.  The  slides  have  been  fully 


indexed  on  a  novel  plan,  showing 
pictorially  in  scrap  books  what  is 
included  in  the  various  groups.  They 
will  be  loaned  to  teachers,  lecturers 
and  organizations  which  can  use  them 
advantageously.  Among  the  subjects 
represented  are  the  following: 

Libraries 

Church  and  Social   Service,   Chicago 

Sunday  Schools,  Chicago 

Suffrage 

Immigration 

Sweated  Industries 

Social  Insurance 

Child  Labor 

Industrial  Hygiene 

Housing,  Tenements,  English  Slums,  munic- 
ipal housing 

Co-operative  Housing 

Housing  by  Employers 

Trade  Unions 

Unemployment 

Co-operation  in  England,  Co-operative 
Undertakings 

Social  Surveys 

Police 

Municipal  Courts 

Fire  Departments

History — England  in  the  time  of   Hogarth 

Other  departments  of  the  Library 
also  have  collections  of  graphic  mate- 
rial, though  not  so  specialized.  Thou- 
sands of  mounted  pictures,  classified, 
may  be  procured  in  the  Hughes  Room 
by  teachers,  artists,  designers,  or  other 


patrons  interested.  They  are  loaned  on 
cards  in  the  same  manner  as  books  are 
drawn  from  the  Library  for  home  use. 
Many  of  the  branches  possess  smaller 
collections  which  are  circulated  in  the 
same  way,  principally  through  teachers 
who  wish  to  use  them  in  the  class  room. 
In  the  Arts  and  Crafts  Room  of  the 
Central  Library,  portfolios  of  material 
may  be  consulted  locally — principally 
along  the  lines  of  industrial  design. 
Much  of  the  material  in  this  depart- 
ment is  costly,  and  some  of  it  rare. 
These  portfolios  are  not  loaned  for 
use  outside  the  building.  Among  the 
subjects  most  fully  represented  are  the 
following : 

China  decoration 

Alphabets,  sign-painting,  show  twd  writing 
Illumination 
Jewelry 
Japanese   art 
Old  printed  books 
Stained  glass 

Indians,  North  and  South  America*

Costumes 
Historic  ornament 
Oil  and  water  color  painting 
Shakespeare — facsimiles

many, Spain,  etc.

Pompei 
Etchings  and  etchers 


Shakespeare    Scenes    and    Portraiture 


In  commemoration  of  the  Shake- 
speare Tercentenary,  April  23,  the 
Chicago  Public  Library  arranged  an 
exhibit  of  prints  and  pictures  illustra- 
tive of  his  works  and  times.  Hundreds 
of  visitors  have  viewed  the  exhibit 


with  interest  daily,  and  no  doubt  many 
thousands  of  Library  users  will  view 
the  pictures  during  the  remaining 
weeks  of  this  month  that  they  will  be 
displayed. 

The  exhibit  has  been  placed  on  the 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


fourth  floor  of  the  Central  building, 
occupying  the  wide  corridor  on  the 
Washington  street  side.  In  addition 
to  twelve  double  screens,  interesting 
books  associated  with  Shakespeare 
have  been  placed  in  display  cases.  The 
headings  on  the  bulletin  boards  of  the 
Shakespeare  exhibit  will  serve  to  indi- 
cate its  character: 

Shakespeare  himself 

Stratford  town 

Famous  Hamlets 

Famous  Shakespearean  actors    (2) 

Shakespearean  theatres 

Shakespeare's  heroines 

Scenes  from  plays: 

Richard  II 

Richard  III 

Hamlet 

Merry  wives  of  Windsor 

Much  ado  about  nothing 

Midsummer  night's  dream 

Tempest 

Romeo  and  Juliet 

Twelfth  night 

As  you  like  it 

Merchant  of  Venice 

King  John 

King  Lear 

Macbeth 

Henry  IV 

Henry  V 

Henry  VI 

Henry  VIII 

Greek  and  Roman  plays 
Book  lists 
Early  editions 

Among  the  pictures  of  Shakespeare 
himself  is  the  Droeshout  portrait  which 
appeared  in  the  first  folio  of  Shakes- 
peare's work,  concerning  which  Ben 
Jonson  wrote: 

The    figure    that   thou   here    seest    put, 
It  was  for  gentle  Shakespeare  cut: 
Wherein  the  graver  had  a  strife 
With  nature,  to  outdo  the  life: 
O,  could  he  but  have  drawne  his  wit 
As  well  in  brasse,  as  he  hath  hit 
His  face,  the  Print  would  then  surpasse 
All,  that  was  ever  writ  in  brasse. 
But,  since  he  cannot,  Reader,  looke 
Not  on  his  Picture,  but  his  Booke. 

There  are  copies  of  the  Chandos  por- 
trait, the  original  of  which  hangs  in  the 
National  Portrait  Gallery  in  London; 
the  Menzel  portrait,  an  imaginary  like- 
ness done  by  a  German  artist,  and 
others. 


In  one  group  there  are  a  score  or 
more  of  famous  Hamlets,  including  a 
portrait  of  John  Lowin,  the  first  man 
who  played  Hamlet. 

Besides  these  there  are  many 
Shakespearean  actors  in  various  roles: 
Fanny  Kemble,  her  father  and  her 
brother,  Susan  Cushman,  Edwin  Booth, 
Hackett,  other  actors  of  the  past,  as 
well  as  the  favorites  of  today. 

Among  the  Shakespearean  theatres 
are  exterior  views  of  the  Globe  and 
Bankside  theatres,  the  "fore"  and 
"inner  stage,"  the  manner  of  seating 
the  audiences,  the  first  production  of 
Merry  wives  of  Windsor. 

Scenes  from  the  plays,  of  which  there 
are  more  than  a  hundred  pictures,  have 
attracted  much  attention  because  of 
their  association  with  performances  by 
favorite  actors,  and  because  of  the  cos- 
tumes that  may  be  copied  by  would-be 
players. 

Other  groups  in  the  exhibit  are 
"Shakespeare's  heroines",  Stratford 
town,  Booklists  and  magazines  devoted 
entirely  to  Shakespeareana. 

The  display  cases  contain  facsimiles 
of  the  first  three  folios  of  Shakespeare's 
plays,  published  in  1623,  1632,  and  1664  ; 
a  volume  of  the  Knight  edition,  beauti- 
fully illustrated  with  steel  engravings, 
a  volume  of  the  Boydell  edition,  pub- 
lished in  1802,  decorated  with  engrav- 
ings of  paintings  done  by  English 
artists  expressly  for  this  work ;  attract- 
ive editions  for  young  people,  various 
Hamlets,  and  miscellaneous  pamphlets 
on  Shakespeare. 

Two  lists  in  one  of  the  display  cases 
have  been  found  particularly  interest- 
ing. One  of  them  includes  sixty-nine 
names  of  plants  and  flowers  indicative 
of  the  plant  lore  and  garden  craft  of 
Shakespeare.  Some  of  the  public  parks 
are  planning  to  plant  Shakespeare  gar- 
dens, and  a  plasterine  model  designed 
by  Mr.  Jens  Jensen  is  to  be  added  to 
the  exhibit  in  the  Public  Library. 
The  flowers  and  plants  mentioned  by 
Shakespeare  are  the  following:

Another  list  of  interest  comprises 
titles  of  books  derived  from  phrases 
employed  by  Shakespeare.  There  are 
many  hundreds  of  them ;  a  few  used  by 
well-known  writers  are  given  below : 

William  Dean  Howells —

The  shadow  of  a  dream.    Hamlet 
The  undiscovered  country.     Hamlet 
A    woman's    reason.      Two   gentlemen    of 
Verona

Books  for  Boys  and  Girls 


Ages:   12  or  13 
Aldrich.     Story  of  a  bad  boy.  H  140 

Pranks  of  a  group  of  boys  in  a  New  England 
town;  supposed  to  tell  much  of  the  author's  own 
boyhood. 

Baldwin.     The  Sampo.  H  7294 

Heroic  tales  from  the  Finnish  national  epic, 
Kalevala. 

Baldwin.     Story  of  Roland.  H  1016 

Stirring  tales  of  battles  and  adventures,  of 
bravery  and  daring  in  the  time  of  CTjarlemagne. 

Bennett.     Master  Skylark.  H  7929 

A  Stratford  lad,  a  cousin  to  William  Shakespeare, 

is  stolen  by  travelling  players,  and  acts  with  them 

before    Queen    Elizabeth.      Interesting    accounts    of 

how  plays  were  given  then. 

Cervantes.     Don  Quixote,  retold  by  Judge 

Parry.  H  3413 

This  edition   of  this   one   of   the   world's   greatest 

books  includes  only  those  parts  that  boys  and  girls 

can  enjoy.     Illustrated  by  Walter  Crane. 

Clemens.   The  prince  and  the  pauper.  F  2800 

A  semi-historical  story  of  King  Edward   the  VI, 

of  England,  who,  when  a  lad,  changed  places  with 

a   poor   boy,    and    many    were   the    adventures    that 

befell  him. 

Barton.     Tales  of  the  Canterbury  pilgrims, 
retold  by  Chaucer  and  others.         F  12316 
Tales   told   by   a   party   of   pilgrims    who    set   out 
together  from  the  Tabard  Inn  in  S'outhwark. 

Barton.     Wonder    book    of    old    Romance. 

F 12317 

Stories  of  William  and  the  Werewolf,  King 
Robert  of  Sicily,  Sir  Gawain  and  the  Green  Knight, 
Guy  of  Warwick,  and  others. 

Dickens.     Christmas  carol.  F 12423 

A  story  with  a  real  Christmas  spirit  of  helpful- 
ness running  through  it. 

Dickens.     Old  curiosity  shop.  F411 

The  story  of  little  Nell  and  her  grandfather. 

Du  Chaillu.     In  African  forest  and  jungle. 

H345

Lost  in  the  jungle,  My  Apingi  kingdom.

great.

series.)  C 17936

Irish  hero  legends. 

Kipling.     Captains  courageous.  F  4728 

A  pampered,  wealthy  boy  falls  off  an  ocean  liner 
in  the  New  Foundland  banks,  is  picked  up  by  some 
fishermen,  and  has  to  live  and  work  with  them  on

life  and  unselfishness.

dog."

The  circulating  department  in  the  main  building 
will  be  closed  on  Memorial  day,  Tuesday,  May  30th. 
The  reading  and  reference  rooms  will  be  open  from 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  Holiday  hours,  from  2  to  6  p.  m., 
will  also  prevail  in  the  branches.

BOOK  BULLETIN. 


jpwbltr  iCibrarg,  Hirlfigatt 


att&  ffaaljUigtim 


ffiulra  and  JtafontrotUm  fur  -patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
draw  books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card 
thus  issued,  any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non- 
fiction  card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw 
one  book,  not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued 
on  the  regular  card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the 
same  time,  enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction 
books,  or  one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on 
the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  ink- 
posed,  whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made 
for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Carl    B.    Roden Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington  street  en- 
trance ;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8 :30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 


Class  Room  Collections 


Since  1910,  the  schools  of  Chicago 
have  been  supplied  upon  request  of 
principals  or  individual  teachers  with 
class  room  collections  for  the  use  of 
pupils.  These  collections  comprise 
groups  of  books  numbering  from  fifty 
to  sixty  volumes  each,  adapted  to  the 
average  children  of  the  several  grades. 
They  are  retained  in  the  class  room  for 
one  semester,  and  are  then  exchange- 
able for  a  similar  collection  embracing 
other  titles.  The  books  are  not  de- 
signed for  class  room  use,  but  are 
loaned  for  reading  at  home.  During 
the  past  year  755  class  room  boxes 
were  sent  to  152  school  buildings,  and 
there  has  been  a  long  waiting  list  of 
teachers  whose  requests  could  not  be 
met  for  lack  of  books.  The  30,990  vol- 
umes represent  a  total  of  1,512  titles, 
and  the  total  issues  for  the  year  were 
about  a  quarter  of  a  million.  The  titles 
which  follow  in  graded  groups  give  the 
following  representation  for  the  re- 
spective grades : 


First    grade. 

Second  "  . 

Third  "  . 

Fourth  "  . 

Fifth  "  .

Seventh  "  .

Training  Class  Examination 

The  entrance  examination  for  candi- 
dates for  the  Training  Class  will  be 
held  in  the  class  room,  fourth  floor,  on 
Wednesday,  June  21st,  at  9  a.  m. 
Applicants  must  be  residents  of  Chi- 
cago, must  be  in  good  health  and 
possess  satisfactory  personal  qualifica- 
tions. A  completed  high  school  course 
or  its  equivalent  will  be  the  minimum 
educational  equipment  accepted. 

Application  blanks,  which  must  be 
filed  before  the  date  of  the  examination, 
may  be  had  from  the  Director  of  the 
Training  Class,  Miss  Faith  E.  Smith, 
who  will  be  glad  to  meet  prospective 
applicants  at  any  time  during  library 
hours. 


Books  for  the  Blind 
A  new  and  complete  catalog  of  this 
department  of  the  library  has  been  is- 
sued and  is  now  on  sale  at  one  cent 
per  copy,  two  cents  by  mail.  The 
library  has  nearly  1700  volumes  of 
books  for  the  blind,  in  the  four  well- 
known  types :  American  Braille, 
American  line,  New  York  point  and 
Moon.  Books  from  this  collection  are 
loaned  to  blind  readers  anywhere 
within  the  state  of  Illinois  and  may  be 
called  for  at  the  Library  or  sent  for 
and  returned  free  through  the  mails. 
The  catalog  just  issued  comprises  forty 
pages  and  includes  a  full  alphabetical 
author  title  and  subject  index. 


Notes 

The  Book  Bulletin  is  not  issued  in 
July  and  August.  The  next  number 
will  appear  about  September  10th. 

Two  legal  holidays  occurring  in  the 
interim  before  the  next  issue  give  rise 
to  the  following  notice : 

On  July  4th,  Independence  Day,  all 
departments  and  branches  of  the  Li- 
brary will  be  closed  all  day. 

On  Sept.  4th,  Labor  Day,  the  refer- 
ence rooms  will  be  open  from  9  a.  m. 
to  6  p.  m.,  the  circulating  department 
being  closed.  The  branches  will  be 
open  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate ;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  are  in 
the  Document  department.     *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

OJljirano  -public  Hthrarg,  iflidmjau  Anruuc  ano  JUaaliuuUon  Street 


iSulrs  anh  ^Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
draw  books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card 
thus  issued,  any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non- 
fiction  card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw 
one  book,  not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued 
on  the  regular  card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the 
same  time,  enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction 
books,  or  one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on 
the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made 
for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian. 


BRANCHES 


Branches  marked  *  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  10 
p.  m. ;  those  marked  t  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  9 
p.  m. ;  all  others  are  open  from  1  to  9  p.  m. ;  Sundays 
and  holidays  open  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary ot  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room,  first  floor, 
Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
_  papers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington  street  en- 
trance; open  9  a.  m.  to  8 :30  p.  m,,  closed  on  Sunday. 


More  Privileges  for  Readers 

On  and  after  October  16th,  1916,  the  Chicago  Public  Library  will  allow  five 
books  (fiction  or  non-fiction)  to  be  drawn  on  a  Reader's  Card  issued  to  any 
adult  user,  and  the  issuance  of  Non-Fiction  Cards  will  be  discontinued. 

Juvenile  Cards  will  permit  holders  to  draw  two  books. 

As  heretofore,  books,  except  those  labeled  "Seven-Day,"  may  be  retained  two 
weeks,  and  renewed  for  two  weeks  longer.  In  justice  to  all  concerned,  no  second 
renewal,  and  no  transfer  to  another  card  will  be  allowed.  Seven-Day  books  are 
not  renewable. 

Books  must  be  presented  with  the  card  to  secure  renewal. 

As  a  new  record  system  will  go  into  effect  at  the  same  time,  patrons  are  re- 
quested to  bear  patiently  any  confusion  which  may  result  while  the  change  is  in 
process. 

The  Public  Library  desires  to  render  to  the  public  as  expeditious,  as  complete 
and  as  satisfactory  service  as  its  resources  will  permit. 

Regulations  Pertaining  to  the  Use  of  Books 

Five  books  may  be  drawn  on  an  adult  Reader's  Card,  and  two  on  a  Juvenile 
Card. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks,  and  may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same 
period.  No  book  shall  be  re-issued  to  the  same  person  on  the  day  of  its  return. 

Books  of  recent  purchase,  labeled  "Seven-day  book,"  shall  not  be  renewed 
or  transferred. 

Books  drawn  upon  vacation  cards,  or  other  special  privilege  cards,  may  be 
retained  for  such  periods  as,  in  the  judgment  of  the  Librarian,  shall  best  carry  out 


114  OHICA.OO   PUBLIC   LIBRARY 

the  purpose  for  which  special  cards  are  issued,    without   impairing   the   rights   and 
privileges  of  library  users  not  holding  special  cards. 

For  detention  of  books  beyond  the  period  prescribed  by  the  Board,  or  the 
Librarian,  fines  shall  be  charged  as  follows: 

Except  as  hereinafter  provided  for  books  drawn  on  juvenile  and  on  vacation 
cards,  for  any  work  whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  three  cents  per  day, 
plus  postage  expended  in  sending  notice  or  notices  to  the  borrower  and  the 
guarantor. 

For  books  drawn  on  juvenile  cards,  one  cent  per  day,  during  such  period  01 
detention,  plus  postage  expended  in  sending  notices  to  the  borrowers  and  the 
guarantors. 

For  books  drawn  on  vacation  cards,  one  cent  per  day  for  each  volume  overdue 
plus  postage  expended  in  sending  notice  or  notices  to  the  borrower  and  the  guar 
antor. 

When  a  book  shall  have  been  detained  beyond  the  prescribed  time,  and  it  be 
comes  necessary  to  send  a  messenger  to  the  borrower  to  secure  its  return,  an  addi- 
tional fine  of  twenty-five  cents  shall  be  charged. 

The  last  user  of  a  book  will  be  held  responsible  for  any  mutilation,  ink  stains 
or  writing,  unless  immediate  notice  of  defect  is  given  the  Library. 

Persons  not  resident  within  the  corporate  limits  of  the  City  of  Chicago,  but 
conducting  a  business  establishment,  or  having  a  definite  business  location  or  office 
in  the  city,  and  persons  regularly  and  permanently  employed  in  the  city,  shall  be 
entitled  to  draw  books  from  the  Library  upon  furnishing  proper  guaranties  as  to  the 
use  of  such  books  and  the  payment  of  charges  occurring  through  loss  of  or  dam- 
age to  books,  and  fines  charged  for  detention  of  such  books  beyond  the  pre- 
scribed period.  No  guarantor  not  an  actual  resident  of  the  city  shall  be  accepted 
on  behalf  of  such  applicants. 

The  privileges  of  the  Library  are  extended  to  persons  residing  outside  of  the 
city  limits,  who  are  attending  school  in  Chicago,  subject  to  the  regulations  that 
apply  to  actual  residents  of  the  city. 

Where  Books  Are  Issued 

Books  are  procurable  in  agencies  of  the  Library,  as  follows: 

No.  of 
Agencies 

Central  Library   1 

Branches   (General)    35 

Branches  (High  School) 5 

Branches  (Business  House)* 21 

Deposit  Stations 29 

Deposit  Collections  (Special)   32 

Delivery  Stations  63 

Class  Room  Libraries  in  Schools 848 


1,034 

The  special  deposit  collections,  business  house  branches,  class  room  libraries 
and  high  school  libraries  serve  groups  of  users  indicated  by  their  names,  but  all 
other  agencies  are  open  to  any  accredited  card  holder.  A  complete  list  of  deliv- 
ery stations  and  a  list  of  deposit  stations  appear  on  the  last  page  of  the  Book  Bul- 
letin, and  a  list  of  the  general  branches  on  the  page  preceding.

the  Document  department.     *Ser.   represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

the  story  of  Hanno,  a  boy  of  Carthage.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary ol  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy.

Notes 


Thousands  of  visitors  from  other 
parts  of  the  United  States  come  to  the 
Main  Library  annually.  A  handbook 
issued  some  years  ago  descriptive  of  the 
architectural  beauties  of  the  building, 
with  its  liberal  use  of  white  Carrara 
marble,  black  veined  Swiss  marble  and 
Connemara  Irish  marble,  and  the  beau- 
tiful mother  of  pearl  mosaics  which 
adorn  the  grand  stairways,  balustrades 
and  some  of  the  larger  rooms,  has  been 
out  of  print  for  some  time,  and  a  new 
edition  is  about  to  be  issued.  In  addi- 
tion to  the  descriptive  material  in 
earlier  editions,  this  will  contain  infor- 
mation about  the  details  found  in  some 
parts  of  the  building — including  the 
story  of  the  seal  which  visitors  en- 
counter set  in  the  floor  of  the  Washing- 
ton street  entrance. 

This  large  plaque  is  a  relief  represen- 
tation in  brass  of  Chicago's  official  seal. 

The  history  of  Chicago's  seal  has 
been  one  of  mystery  and  uncertainty. 


Its  earliest  record  has  been  traced  back 
to  1833,  when  the  present  city  was  still 
a  town.  The  design  of  the  town's  seal 
was  a  primitive  yet  faithful  copy  of  the 
obverse  side  of  the  half-eagle  gold  coin 
of  the  United  States.  Col.  TJ.V.  Owen, 
United  States  commissioner  to  nego- 
tiate with  the  neighboring  Indian 
tribes,  and  president  of  the  town  board, 
has  been  credited  with  being  the  author 
of  this,  the  first  authentic  symbol  of  the 
town's  existence. 

Upon  the  incorporation  of  Chicago 
as  a  city  in  1837,  among  the  first  ques- 
tions to  come  before  the  common  coun- 
cil was  that  of  a  municipal  seal,  some  of 
the  city  fathers  seemingly  favoring  the 
retention  of  the  old  town  seal,  while 
others,  among  whom  were  Mayor 
Ogden  and  Alderman  Goodhue,  argued 
in  behalf  of  the  adoption  of  an  entirely 
new  seal,  symbolic  of  the  newly  created 
city's  present  and  future.  The  pro- 
tagonists of  the  future  of  the  infant  city 


138 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


were  victorious,  a  committee  being 
appointed  to  draft  a  new  seal.  The 
committee,  whose  members  apparently 
were  endowed  with  considerable  her- 
aldic skill,  reported  in  July,  1837,  for 
enactment  as  a  municipal  seal,  the  de- 
vice which  later  gave  rise  to  much 
speculation  and  discussion. 

Although  the  original  drawing  is  lost, 
it  is  described  thus  in  the  ordinance: 
"The  shield  of  Chicago  shall  be  repre- 
sented by  a  shield  with  a  sheaf  of  wheat 
on  its  center;  a  ship  in  full  sail  on  the 
right;  a  sleeping  infant  on  the  top;  an 
Indian  with  bow  and  arrow  on  the  left ; 
and  with  the  motto  'Urbs  In  Horto,' 
at  the  bottom  of  shield,  with  the  inscrip- 
tion, 'City  of  Chicago — Incorporated 
4th  of  March,  1837,'  around  the  outside 
edge  of  said  seal." 

Amendments  to  the  above  ordinance 
were  made  in  June,  1854,  and  February, 
1893,  the  first  amendment  specifying 
that  "over  the  shield  an  infant  reposes 
on  a  sea-shell,"  while  the  latter  ampli- 
fies this  by  decreeing  a  "sleeping  infant 
on  top,  lying  on  its  back  on  a  shell." 
As  a  result  of  the  fact  that  no  faithful 
reproduction  of  the  seal  authorized  by 
the  ordinance  was  in  use  in  the  city's 
departments,  a  new  and  corrected  de- 
sign and  description  of  the  municipal 
seal  was  provided  for  by  ordinance  of 
March  20,  1905,  having  for  its  chief 
recommendation  heraldic  and  historic 
accuracy.  This  is  the  seal  of  the  Chi- 
cago of  our  own  time,  and  is  described 
in  the  ordinance  as  follows : 

"The  seal  provided  and  authorized 
for  the  city  of  Chicago  shall  be  on  an 
obverse  side  with  a  diameter  of  two  and 
three-eighths  inches,  the  impression  of 
which  is  the  representation  of  a  shield 
in  red,  white  and  blue ;  with  a  sheaf  of 
wheat  in  the  center,  in  gold;  a  ship  in 
full  sail  on  the  right  side;  on  top  a 
sleeping  infant  .reposed  as  in  a  shell,  in 
silver;  an  Indian  chief  with  a  bow  and 
arrow,  on  the  left  side,  standing  on  a 
promontory,  in  green ;  with  the  motto 
'Urbs  In  Horto,'  in  gold  on  a  red  flow- 


ing ribbon,  at  the  bottom  of  shield ;  with 
the  inscription,  'City  of  Chicago — Incor- 
porated 4th  March,  1837,'  in  gold 
within  a  blue  ring  around  the  outer 
edge  of  said  seal,  which  seal  represented 
as  aforesaid  and  used  with  or  without 
colors,  shall  be  and  is  hereby  corrected, 
established,  declared  to  have  been,  and 
now  to  be,  the  seal  of  the  city  of  Chi- 
cago. For  general  use,  the  plain  im- 
pression in  white  containing  the  figures 
as  given  will  be  sufficient. 

The  symbolic  meaning  of  the  new 
and  corrected  seal  is  rendered  thus: 

The  Indian,  represents  the  discoverer 
of  the  site  of  Chicago,  is  also  indicative 
of  the  aboriginal  contribution  which 
enters  into  its  history. 

The  ship  in  full  sail  is  emblematic 
of  the  approach  of  the  white  man's 
civilization  and  commerce. 

The  sheaf  of  wheat  is  typical  of 
activity  and  plenty,  holding  the  same 
meaning  as  the  cornucopia. 

The  nude  babe  in  the  shell  is  the 
ancient  and  classical  symbolism  of  the 
pearl,  and  Chicago  situated  at  the  neck 
of  the  lake  signifies  that  it  shall  be  "the 
gem  of  the  lakes."  The  infant,  repre- 
sented in  repose,  has  the  additional 
meaning  of  contentment,  peace  and 
purity. 

The  motto  "Urbs  In  Horto"  means 
"City  in  a  Garden." 

The  date,  "March  4th,  1837,"  sug- 
gests the  date  of  the  incorporation  of 
the  city." 

In  preparation  for  the  celebration  in 
Illinois  of  one  hundred  years  of  state- 
hood, the  Chicago  Public  Library  has 
begun  the  compilation  of  a  serviceable 
bibliography  of  materials  in  print,  both 
books  and  pamphlets  being  represented. 
Anticipating  the  demand  from  students 
of  Illinois  history,  schools,  and  citizens 
interested  in  a  general  way,  duplicates 
of  the  material  possessed  by  the  Li- 
brary are  being  acquired. 

On  the  fourth  floor  of  the  Washing- 
ton street  end  of  the  Library  a  large 
relief  map  of  Illinois  has  been  installed. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


139 


and  this  has  interested  many  patrons 
who  have  had  occasion  to  visit  that  sec- 
tion of  the  building. 

Preparations  are  also  under  way  for 
an  extensive  exhibit,  to  be  located  on 
the  same  floor,  of  old  publications, 
prints,  illustrations,  maps,  and  other 
graphic  material  touching  upon  the 
history  of  Illinois.  These  will  not  be 
restricted  to  the  period  of  statehood, 
but  will  comprise  as  well  historic 
episodes  within  the  picturesque  era  of 
the  French  regime,  the  brief  English 
dominion  and  territorial  days,  the  inter- 
esting stories  of  LaSalle,  Tonty  and 
Starved  Rock,  Marquette  and  the 
Indians,  Kaskaskia  and  George  Rogers 
Clark,  the  Brikbeck  settlements,  and 
other  events  of  significance.  The  Black 
Hawk  war,  the  Mormon  invasion  and 
the  Exodus  from  Nauvoo,  the  Lovejoy 
incident,  the  careers  of  Lincoln,  Grant, 
Douglas,  Logan  and  other  men  promi- 
nent in  the  earlier  state  history,  will 
also  figure  in  this  pictorial  representa- 
tion. 

In  addition  it  is  proposed  to  assemble 
for  the  use  of  clubs,  organizations  and 
schools,  a  collection  of  stereopticon 
slides  which  may  be  borrowed  without 
charge  on  suitable  conditions  for  local 
lectures. 

The  Library  will  welcome  gifts  of 
books,  prints  and  maps  that  may  prove 
interesting  in  this  connection. 


Magazines  for  Library  Readers 
The  annual  scrutiny  of  magazines  to 
be  subscribed  for  by  the  Public  Li- 
brary reveals  anew  the  immense  num- 
ber and  variety  of  these  publications 
and  the  constant  changes  taking  place 
in  this  field.  Every  new  development 
in  public  interest,  which  succeeds  in 
engaging  the  attention  of  any  consider- 
able number  of  people,  quickly  finds  ut- 
terance in  a  new  periodical  established 
to  further  the  cause.  The  Bulletin  of 
Bibliography,  itself  an  interesting  ex- 
ponent of  a  very  specialized  activity, 
maintains  a  current  list  of  "births  and 
deaths"  in  the  periodical  world.  No 


less  than  247  new  periodicals  were  here 
noted  in  1915,  while  196  were  reported 
as  having  expired  or  merged  their 
existence  with  that  of  some  other  pub- 
lication. The  present  year  has  seen  no 
material  change  in  the  vital  statistics 
of  the  periodical  world  and  it  is  one  of 
the  tasks  involved  in  compiling  the 
new  subscription  list  to  sort  out,  from 
this  annual  crowd  of  little  strangers, 
those  which  not  only  represent  a  legiti- 
mate and  reasonably  important  phase  of 
public  interest,  but  which,  also,  seem  to 
show  promise  of  enough  vitality  to  sur- 
vive at  least  during  the  period  con- 
tracted for.  Among  the  magazines 
added  to  the  list  of  those  currently  re- 
ceived— already  numbering  well  over 
thirteen  hundred  titles — are  several 
dealing  with  the  development  of  com- 
mercial opportunities  in  foreign  lands: 
"The  Russian  Review"  and  "Russia," 
and  the  "South  American"  are  timely 
additions  which  will,  it  is  hoped,  meet 
the  growing  demand  for  information  in 
these  fields.  The  realm  of  aviation  is 
entered  by  several  newcomers,  one  de- 
voting its  pages  almost  exclusively  to 
military  aircraft.  Proofreading  is  the 
subject  of  one  of  the  new  arrivals, 
Japanese  drama  of  another,  the  janitor- 
ial care  of  large  buildings  of  a  third,  the 
new  taste  in  cemetery  art  of  still  an- 
other. In  a  few  instances  favorites  of 
other  days  have  been  forced  to  retire 
because  of  a  shifting  of  public  interest. 
In  one  or  two  of  the  popular  magazines 
it  is  regrettable  to  note  a  distinct 
change  of  policy  resulting  in  a  lowered 
tone  and  a  consequent  loss  of  prestige, 
while  in  a  few  others  the  opposite  ten- 
dency has  been  observed;  magazines 
formerly  bearing  a  rather  dubious  repu- 
tation having  improved  in  standards 
and  the  character  of  their  offerings 
until  they  may  no  longer  be  ignored. 
All  of  these  elements  combine  to  make 
the  choice  of  periodicals  to  be  supplied 
to  a  large  and  highly  diversified  con- 
stituency one  of  the  interesting,  though 
not  one  of  the  easiest,  of  the  problems 
arising  in  the  day's  work.

CHICAGO   PUBLIC   LIBRARY

the  Document  department.     *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  chculate.

and  3ufnrmatum  for 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
'ook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
ity,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
og  a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
D  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
ation  and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
if  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
rill  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
Iraw  five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
ation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
nee  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
enewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
(even-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed, 
lie  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
ut  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
itations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
ooks  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
elivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
tie  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
-ibrary.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  booka  OB 


the  open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  •tacks,   which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of  non- 
fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work 
of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound  in  one  or 
more  volumes,  which  is  not  returned  within  two 
weeks  from  the  date  of  issue.  An  additional  charge 
of  two  cents  is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the 
return  of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  delivered 
to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  •  book  it 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Volume  6. 


November,  1916 


No.  9 


High  School  Libraries 


Through  the  whole-hearted  coopera- 
tion of  the  Board  of  Education,  a  splen- 
did beginning  has  been  made  in  the  in- 
stallation of  libraries  in  the  Chicago 
high  schools  for  the  use  of  students  and 
faculty.  Excellent  and  commodious 
quarters  have  been  set  aside  in  five  of 
the  high  schools,  furniture  and  other 
physical  accommodations  have  been 
supplied  by  the  School  Board,  which 
has  also  borne  the  cost  of  the  reference 
books,  cyclopedias,  dictionaries,  atlases 
and  the  like,  which  may  be  regarded  as 
part  of  the  "fixtures"  of  such  libraries 
The  Public  Library  has  supplied  care- 
fully selected  working  collections  of 
books  in  all  classes  of  literature  suit- 
able for  collateral  study  and  for  the 
general  use  of  young  people  of  high 
school  age.  The  lighter  side  of  litera- 
ture has  not  been  neglected ;  current 
fiction  as  well  as  the  classics,  good 
books  on  many  topics  not  directly 
allied  to  school  work,  and  a  wide  selec- 
tion of  periodicals  have  been  included. 
The  latter  class  includes  a  number  of 
high  grade  professional  and  trade 
magazines  in  architecture,  engineering 
and  the  like,  which  are  of  great  value 
in  the  technical  and  vocational  courses. 
Expert  librarians  have  been  assigned 
to  these  interesting  posts  from  the 
Library  staff  and  the  response,  from 
teachers  and  pupils  alike,  has  been 
most  gratifying. 

High  school  libraries  have  proven 
their  value  in  other  cities  and  have  in 
many  instances  been  developed  to  a 
high  degree  of  effectiveness.  They 
serve  a  twofold  purpose  in  making 
available  the  literary  aids  to  study, 
while,  at  the  same  time,  introducing 


the  public  library  into  the  lives  of  their 
young  patrons  at  the  habit  forming 
age  and  establishing  a  relation  to  be 
continued  in  the  years  to  come.  While 
in  some  of  the  older  high  school  build- 
ings the  arrangement  of  suitable  quar- 
ters for  a  library  has  been  found  diffi- 
cult, their  introduction  into  new  build- 
ings has  been  assured  by  the  cordial 
response  of  the  Board  of  Education  to 
the  suggestion  from  the  Library  Board 
that  such  quarters  should  be  considered 
in  all  future  plans.  This  suggestion 
was  promptly  accepted  and  blue  prints 
of  the  plans  of  the  new  Robert  Lind- 
blom  High  School,  at  W.  61st  and  S. 
Lincoln  Sts.,  which  include  a  spacious 
and  beautiful  library  room,  have  re- 
cently been  submitted  to  the  Librarian 
by  the  School  Board  architects.  Mean- 
while, at  the  Englewood  High  School 
— one  of  the  older  buildings — accept- 
able quarters  have  been  secured  by  a 
readjustment  of  rooms,  and  the  same 
plan  will  be  followed  at  the  John  Mar- 
shall High  School,  at  Adams  St.  and 
Kedzie  Ave.,  shortly  after  January  1st, 
1917.  In  all  cases  the  schools  have 
turned  over  such  collections  of  books 
as  they  possessed,  which  were  often 
well  selected,  considerable  in  numbers, 
and  showed  much  use.  The  high 
school  libraries  now  in  operation  are: 

Austin,  3,800  vols. 

Helen  S.  Babcock,  Librarian. 

Carter  H.  Harrison,  3,200  vols. 

Edith  Erskine,  Librarian. 

Lake  View  (Jan.,  1917),  5,000  vols. 
Margaret  Ely,  Librarian. 

Nicholas  Senn,  3,100  vols. 

Frances  V.  Rice,  Librarian. 

Englewood,  3,500  vols. 

Eliza  Pendry,  Librarian. 


154 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


The  Library  in  the  Campaign 
Both  the  Democratic  and  the  Re- 
publican Campaign  Headquarters, 
located  in  Chicago,  gave  ample  evi- 
dence of  their  recognition  of  the  Public 
Library  as  a  source  of  supply  for  cam- 
paign ammunition.  Their  well  organ- 
ized information  bureaus  were  almost 
constantly  in  touch  with  the  Library, 
especially  the  Civics  Department, 
either  by  telephone  or  messenger  serv- 
ice, and  many  curious,  interesting  and 
puzzling  problems  in  searching  out 
fugitive  facts  were  submitted  for  solu- 
tion, usually  in  great  haste  and  with- 
out any  collateral  information  to  facili- 
tate the  search.  A  letter  written  by 
Senator  T.  J.  Walsh,  chairman  of  the 
Democratic  committee,  expresses  his 
appreciation  "of  the  hearty  and  effect- 
ive cooperation  which  the  Chicago  Pub- 
lic Library  gave  us  in  our  efforts  to 
get  at  facts  for  the  conduct  of  the 
political  campaign  just  closing.  We 
have  had  occasion  repeatedly  to  call  for 
assistance  on  many  questions,  and  we 
have  nothing  but  praise  for  your  co- 
operation. The  members  of  our  Ref- 
erence and  Information  Bureau  have 
expressed  to  me  the  fine  spirit  of  serv- 
ice that  exists  in  the  Civics  Room,  to 
which  most  of  our  inquiries  were  sent." 
As  an  offset  to  the  demands  made 
upon  the  Library  the  two  headquarters 
have  been  very  generous  in  turning 
over  the  huge  accumulations  of  printed 
material  acquired  or  published  by  them. 
This  will  be  sifted  and  sorted  out  and 
much  of  it  will  be  added  to  the  per- 
manent collections  as  valuable  data  for 
future  historians. 

Some  of  the  Campaign  Questions 
Answered

governor  of  Illinois  in  1912?

Minnesota?

1911.

of  Mississippi.

in  Illinois.

tral in  Illinois  and  Mississippi.

Holiday  Hours 

On  Thanksgiving  Day,  Thursday, 
Nov.  30th,  the  delivery  room  in  the 
central  building  will  be  closed ;  read- 
ing rooms  will  be  open  from  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m.,  and  branches  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Volume  6. 


December,  1916 


No.  10 


4. 


7. 


Radical  Reorganization  of  the  Library  System 

By  unanimous  vote  of  the  Library 
Board,  initial  steps  will  be  taken  at 
once  to  put  into  effect  a  radical  Library 
Reorganization  Plan  in  Chicago  whose 
purpose  is  briefly  expressed  in  the  fol- 
lowing paragraph  taken  from  an  ex- 
planatory pamphlet  about  to  be  issued : 

Library  service  within  walking  dis- 
tance of  home  for  every  person  in  the 
City  of  Chicago,  who  can  read  or  wants 
to  use  books,  in  place  of  being  compelled 
to  ride  on  street  cars  forth  and  back,  an 
average  of  10  miles  for  more  than  one- 
half  the  population. 

The  problem,  of  which  the  above  is 
the  anticipated  solution,  is  this : 

The  corporate  limits  of  Chicago  em- 
brace nearly  200  square  miles  of  terri- 
tory. 

There  are  700,000  potential  users  of 
the  Library  who  are  now  prevented 
from  using  the  Library's  best  resources 
by  reason  of  distance. 

Means  and  methods  of  expanding  the 
Library's  service  through  Regional  and 
Auxiliary  branches,  and  to  render  the 
book  collections  more  elastic  through 
daily  local  deliveries  in  all  sections  of 
the  city,  are  outlined  in  the  following 
summarized  introduction,  with  accom- 
panying map,  which  will  appear  in  the 
pamphlet  now  in  press : 


Under  the  Reorganization   Program  of  the 
Library  Board,  there  will  be  established : 
1.    Five  Regional  Branches,   situated    as    fol- 
lows : 

A — Ravenswood 
B— Garfield  Park 
C — 'Loop 
D — Englewood 
E — South    Chicago 

Seventy  Auxiliary  or  Local  Branches, 
equally  distributed  where  most  needed,  and 
where  largest  groups  of  population  live. 


2. 


There  are  now  35,  unequally  distributed. 
3.  Sixty  Deposit  Stations,  in  more  sparsely- 
settled  sections,  or  as  many  as  may  be 
necessary  to  supply  places  not  otherwise 
served.  There  are  now  28. 
One  hundred  Industrial  and  Commercial 
Branches,  or  as  many  more  as  business 
concerns  are  willing  to  equip  and  maintain. 
There  are  now  21. 

Twenty-two  High  School  Branches,  if  suit- 
able quarters  are  provided  by  the  school 
authorities.  There  are  now  5. 
Three  thousand  Class  Room  Libraries,  or 
as  many  as  may  be  needed  (traveling  col- 
lections of  50  volumes  each  supervised  by 
teachers  and  exchanged  twice  a  year). 
There  are  now  848. 

One  hundred  Special  Deposits,  (or  more, 
if  needed),  supplied  to  Y.  M.  C.  A.  houses, 
Eleanor  Clubs,  Organizations  of  Foreign 
Groups,  Women's  Clubs,  Institutions,  Spe- 
cial Groups  like  Telegraph  Messengers, 
Postal  Clerks,  etc.,  etc.  These  deposits  are 
traveling  collections  of  50  to  100  volumes, 
exchanged  monthly,  bi-monthly  or  quar- 
terly. There  are  now  29. 


Each  Regional  Branch  will  have: 

A  Floating  Collection  of  50,000  volumes  for 
use  of  Auxiliary  or  Local  branches,  as  needed, 
through  daily  delivery  service. 

A  Reference  Collection  suitably  balanced, 
for  use  of  Research  Workers,  Students,  Busi- 
ness Men,  Women's  Clubs,  etc.,  supplemented 
by  daily  delivery  from  the  Main  Library  of 
special  material  not  duplicated  in  the  Regional 
branches. 

Trained  assistants  to  conduct  story  hours, 
reference  librarians,  to  assist  club  worker*, 
teachers,  etc. 

Suitable  collections  of  books  in  foreign 
languages,  for  redistribution  to  local  centers 
where  foreign  groups  are  located. 

Automobile  delivery  truck,  with  garage  for 
housing,  to  distribute  books  daily  in  agencies 
of  the  district. 


Some  Striking  Improvements  Possible  Through 

the    Reorganization    Plan : 
The   Chicago    Public   Library   will    be    in    a 
position  to  serve  well  700,000  persons  who  now 
get  no  library  service  by  reason  of  distance, 
or  who  are  but  indifferently  served. 


170 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


A  maximum  journey  of  32  miles  to  consult 
special  reference  material  or  to  draw  music 
scores  for  home  use  will  be  reduced  to  an 
average  of  less  than  one  mile. 


Automobile  delivery  routes  will  be  reduced 
from  32  miles  per  round  trip  to  six  miles, 
enabling  a  unit  delivery  five  times  greater  than 
now,  and  without  additional  cost. 


The  Reorganization  Plan  can  be  put  into  effect  if  the  City  Council  will  appro- 
priate, and  the  voters  ivill  approve,  a  Bond  Issue  of  $500,000,  to  be  expended  in 
the  next  Five  Years.

Books  Read  by  Boys  at  the  County  Jail 


In  July,  1916,  through  the  interested 
cooperation  of  Mrs.  Gertrude  Howe  Brit- 
ton,  80  books,  specially  chosen  for  the 
purpose,  were  loaned  by  the  Library  to 
the  Bureau  of  Social  Service  for  the  boys 
confined  in  the  County  Jail.  It  was  called 
the  Good  Hope  Library. 

At  the  same  time,  a  collection,  care- 
fully selected,  was  furnished  for  the 
Psychopathic  Hospital,  and  was  termed 
the  Good  Cheer  Library. 

Last  week,  through  the  cooperation  of 
Principal  O.  J.  Milliken,  a  hundred  books 
termed  collectively  the  Good  Luck  Li- 
brary were  sent  to  the  Chicago  and  Cook 
County  School  for  Boys  (formerly  the 
John  Worthy  School  in  the  Bridewell) 
for  the  use  of  the  boys  detained  there. 

Mrs.  Britton  has  just  sent  to  the  Li- 
brary an  interesting  report  of  the  use 
made  of  the  Good  Hope  Library  at  the 
County  Jail,  prefacing  her  report  with 
the  following  comment:  "This  report 
convinces  me  that  this  little  branch  li- 
brary serves  a  real  need,  and  that  any 
effort  which  we  may  put  forth  in  getting 
good  books  to  these  unfortunate  boys 
which  they  will  read,  is  very  well  worth 
while.  It  will  interest  you  to  know  that 
during  the  four  months  these  books  have 
been  in  use  not  a  single  one  has  been  de- 
faced or  lost." 

Miss  Kate  Falconer,  who  has  charge 
of  this  little  library,  adds :  "You  will  no- 
tice that  a  number  of  the  books  are  read 
more  frequently  than  others  and  that 
some  of  the  books  have  never  been  called 
for  at  all.  I  find  that  whenever  a  boy 
gets  a  book  and  likes  it  he  tells  the  others, 
and  on  the  next  library  day  four  or  five 
of  them  are  ready  to  ask  for  the  same 
book.  They  like  best  the  modern  stories 
of  adventure  like  The  Barrier  by  Rex 
Beach  and  books  from  which  a  motion 
picture  has  been  filmed  like  Sir  Nigel  and 
Michael  Strogoff.  I  wish  we  might  get 
from  the  library  more  books  by  Rex 
Beach  and  Jack  London  and  several  vol- 
umes of  poetry.  Boys  have  asked  me 
for  Scott's  poems  and  Longfellow's 
poems.  You  must  remember  they  are  in 


their  cells  twenty  hours  a  day  and  there 
are  few  distractions  so  the  boys  are  glad 
to  read  a  higher  type  of  book  than  they 
would  read,  I  am  sure,  on  the  outside." 
These  books  were  to  be  given  out  to 
the  boys  awaiting  trial  in  the  County  Jail, 
and  it  was  hoped  that  they  would  not 
only  prove  a  diversion  to  these  boys,  but 
would  be  instructive  as  well.  The  books 
were  carefully  selected  so  as  to  appeal 
to  boys  of  that  age  (17  to  21),  and  after 
using  them  six  months  the  following  lists 
will  serve  to  show  the  reading  tendency 
of  boys  of  that  age :
