Exhibits  and  Bulletin  Boards 


Thousands  of  visitors  have  viewed 
with  interest  the  series  of  exhibits  which 
have  been  installed  in  floor  cases  and  on 
bulletin  boards  since  early  in  the  year  in 
the  spacious  corridor  between  the 
Woman's  Study  Room  and  the  Thomas 
Hughes  Room  for  Young  People  on  the 
fourth  floor.  Teachers  have  brought 
their  classes  to  study  them,  including  ex- 
cursionists from  Evanston,  Ravinia, 
Maywood  and  other  near-by  towns. 
These  exhibits  of  prints,  posters,  books 
and  other  material,  included: 

Shakespeare  Exhibit — Portraits;  Stratford 
town ;  Shakespearean  theatres ;  Famous 
Shakespearean  actors ;  Early  editions ;  Fa- 
mous Hamlets ;  Shakespeare's  heroines ;  Illus- 
trations of  the  Greek  and  Roman  plays; 
Romeo  and  Juliet;  Henry  IV;  Henry  V  and 
Henry  VI;  Henry  VIII;  Richard  II  and 
Richard  III ;  King  Lear  and  King  John ;  Mac- 
beth; Hamlet;  Merchant  of  Venice  and 
Othello;  As  you  like  it;  Twelfth  night;  Merry 
wives  of  Windsor ;  Tempest  and  Midsummer 
night's  dream ;  Attractive  editions  for  young 
people ;  Book  lists. 

Red  Indians  Exhibit — Nature  worship;  Cus- 
toms; Home  life;  Hunting;  The  Indian  war- 
rior; Tribes;  The  Pueblo  country;  Indian  por- 
traits; Famous  Indians;  The  Red  Man  in 
Illinois. 

Napoleon — Members  of  Napoleon's  family; 
The  Empress  Josephine ;  Contemporaries  of 
Napoleon ;  Napoleon's  funeral ;  Caricatures ; 
Battles  of  Napoleon's  time ;  Events  and  scenes 
in  Napoleon's  life;  Portraits  of  Napoleon. 

Other  exhibits  for  the  immediate  fu- 
ture include: 


See  Chicago  First — A  series  of  a  thousand 
postal  card  views;  Historic  Chicago;  River 
Views  (miniature  etchings  by  Earl  Reed)  ; 
Sky  Scrapers  and  Cliff  Dwellings;  Two  Great 
Universities;  Parks  for  the  People;  Street 
Glimpses  and  By-Paths;  Busy  Marts  and  In- 
dustries. 

Benjamin  Franklin,  the  Many  Sided — The 
Boy  and  Struggling  Printer's  'Prentice;  The 
Man  of  Science;  The  Diplomat  and  States- 
man; The  Philosopher  and  Humorist. 

Poets  and  Writers  in  Caricature,  including , 
a  section  devoted  to  the  Chicago  Group — 
Theodore  Dreiser;  Edgar  Lee  Masters;  the 
McCutcheons,  John  and  George  Barr;  Vachell 
Lindsay;  Edna  Ferber;  Hamlin  Garland;  etc., 
etc. 

Cartoons  by  Chicago  Newspaper  Artists: 

Chicago  Tribune— Claire  Briggs;  John  T. 
McCutcheon;  Sidney  Smith;  Frank  King. 

Chicago  Herald— Frank  Willard;  William 
DeBeck;  H.  A.  MacGill. 

Chicago  American — T.  A.  Dorgan  (Tad)  ; 
C.  D.  Batchelor;  Clifford  Sterret;  Hal  Coff- 
man. 

Chicago  Examiner — Harry  Murphy;  T.  E. 
Powers;  Jean  Knott. 

Chicago  Daily  News — Fontaine  Fox ;  Luther 
Daniels  Bradley;  Bud  Fisher. 

Chicago  Daily  Journal— John  Carsell;  Rob- 
ert Edgren;  Maurice  Ketten. 

Chicago  Evening  Post— Clark  DeBau;  Sat- 
terfield;  R.  McCann. 

Historic  Illinois — In  preparation  for  the 
centennial  celebration  of  statehood  in  1918. 


The  wing  frames  on  the  third  floor 
(Circulation)  and  the  case  end  bulletins 
in  the  Open  Shelf  Room  are  also  being 
utilized  for  exhibits  in  miniature,  and

"little  lists."     Among  the  lists  recently

War — The  sea  and  the  air;  Women  and  the

There  are  bulletin  screens  in  the  Civics 
Room  for  posting  notices  of  civil  service 
examinations  (city,  county,  state,  fed- 
eral), and  special  material  that  the  Room 
possesses  to  aid  intending  applicants, 
thousands  of  whom  flock  to  this  source 
of  supply.  Notices  of  interesting  mate- 
rial issued  by  Uncle  Sam  on  a  hundred 
different  topics  are  also  bulletined  from 
time  to  time. 

A  map  display,  frequently  changed  to 
give  timeliness  to  the  exhibit,  is  also  lo- 
cated in  the  Civics  Room. 


The  series  of  panels  in  the  long  corri- 
dor leading  to  the  Reading  Room  are 
used  for  revised  lists  of  magazines,  se- 
rials and  newspapers  (alphabetical  and 
classified),  daily  weather  reports,  and 
general  information  of  timely  interest. 


The  Library  and  the  Financial  World 


Frequent  inquiries  are  received  by  the 
Library  concerning  its  resources  on  the 
subjects  of  investment  and  speculation. 
In  the  Civics  Department  there  can  be 
found  on  file  some  of  the  most  up-to- 
date  and  most  authoritative  literature  on 
this  subject.  The  latest  magazines,  such 
as  The  Wall  St.  Journal  (Daily),  The 
Chicago  Economist  (weekly),  The  Lon- 
don Economist  (weekly),  and  The  Mag- 
azine of  Wall  St.  (bi-weekly)  are  on  file 
in  this  room.  The  names  of  these  peri- 
odicals indicate  what  sort  of  information 
can  be  found  within  the  covers. 

T6  supplement  these,  various  manuals, 
such  as  Moody's,  Poor's,  and  Moody 's 
Analysis  can  be  consulted  here.  The  lat- 
est annual  report,  the  status  of  bond  is- 
sues and  the  names  of  the  officials,  of 
thousands  of  corporations  are  listed  in 
these  yearbooks.  The  Cumulative  Digest 
of  Corporation  News,  a  Moody  publica- 
tion, gives  information  about  dividends, 
assessments,  and  stock  listings,  in  tabloid 
form,  of  the  leading  corporations  of  the 
United  States,  with  references  to  the 
source  of  information.  In  addition,  the 
government  itself  issues  various  bulletins, 


which  are  worth  the  time  of  the  business 
man  to  consult.  The  Federal  Reserve 
Board  Bulletin,  a  monthly  publication, 
besides  noting  important  banking  data, 
gives  a  summary  of  business  conditions 
in  the  twelve  reserve  districts.  The  new 
Federal  Loan  Board  has  issued  in  the 
last  few  weeks  bulletins  on  rural  credit. 
For  the  man  interested  in  the  grain  mar- 
ket, the  Library  receives  a  weekly  news 
letter  and  a  monthly  report  of  crop  con- 
ditions, and  for  information  on  the  vege- 
table and  fruit  market  there  is  a  daily 
bulletin.  Valuable  comparative  figures 
may  be  obtained  from  all  of  the  annual 
reports. 

The  student  of  finance  and  banking 
problems  will  find  the  most  recent  books 
and  pamphlets  reserved  for  reference  use 
in  this  department,  with  duplicate  copies 
of  many  to  be  circulated  for  home  use. 
The  daily  newspapers  are  diligently 
searched  for  items  on  financial  topics. 
These  are  clipped  and  filed  with  the  pam- 
phlets on  the  same  subject.  A  few  of 
such  subjects  are  listed  below.

Have  You  a  Music  Card? 

Since  the  opening  of  the  Music  Room 
in  the  Main  Library,  two  years  ago,  its 
use  has  steadily  increased.  Every  ac- 
credited card-holder  is  entitled,  upon  ap- 
plication, to  a  Music  Card,  entitling  the 
bearer  to  draw  for  home  use,  one  piece 
of  bound  music  (operatic  score,  sym- 
phony, cantata,  collections,  etc.),  and  five 
pieces  of  sheet  music.  Bound  volumes 


may  be  retained  four  weeks,  and  sheet 
music  two  weeks.  Since  the  opening  of 
the  department,  51,705  pieces  have  been 
issued  for  home  use.  In  this  total,  the 
several  parts  for  duets,  trios,  quartettes, 
etc.,  are  counted  as  one,  although  sep- 
arately bound. 

Importation  of  music  scores  from  Eu- 
rope has  practically  ceased  during  the 
great  war,  but  the  Library  has  secured 
(with  some  difficulty)  a  number  of  copies 
of  the  operatic  novelties  added  to  this 
season's  list  at  the  Auditorium,  and  these 
are  being  issued  as  usual  on  Music  Cards, 
except  that,  during  the  Grand  Opera  sea- 
son, they  are  subject  to  the  regulations 
governing  Seven-Day  books. 

With  the  extension  of  the  Civic  Music 
Association's  activities  to  include  a  series 
of  ten  Sunday  afternoon  concerts  by  the 
American  Symphony  Orchestra  in  school 
auditoriums  in  different  parts  of  the  city, 
it  is  expected  that  the  impulse  among  the 
people  for  the  study,  as  well  as  the  en- 
joyment, of  music,  will  make  increased 
demands  upon  the  resources  of  the  Li- 
brary, and  preparations  are  under  way  to 
meet  this  demand. 

At  the  instance  of  Director  Oscar 
Mayer,  who  has  taken  an  active  interest 
in  the  Music  Room  since  its  inception,  a 
letter  has  been  addressed  to  representa- 
tives of  Italian,  Bohemian,  Polish,  Lith- 
uanian, Ruthenian,  Russian,  Scandinav- 
ian, Hungarian,  Lettish,  and  other  mu- 
sical organizations  in  Chicago,  inviting 
their  cooperation  in  securing  an  adequate 
representation  of  their  national  music. 

A  New  Library  Training  Class 

An  examination  for  entrance  to  the 
Training  Class  will  be  held  December  30 
at  9  o'clock.  The  minimum  educational 
requirement  for  admission  to  the  exam- 
ination is  a  complete  high  school  course. 
Questions  include  history,  literature,  and 
general  information. 

The  class  will  open  February  5,  and 
continue  for  at  least  six  months.  No 
salary  is  paid  to  members  of  the  Train- 
ing Class. 

In  order  to  obtain  a  position  in  the  Li- 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


175 


brary,  a  candidate  must  pass  a  Civil  Serv- 
ice examination,  which  is  usually  given 
soon  after  the  close  of  the  course  in  the 
Training  Class.  If  one  is  appointed  to  a 
position  after  passing  this  examination, 
the  salary  begins  at  forty  dollars  a  month. 
An  application  blank  will  be  sent  to 
anyone  who  will  indicate  her  desire  to 
take  the  entrance  examination  on  Decem- 
ber 30. 

Library  Not  Open  Christmas 
Every  department  of  the  Main  Library 
will  be  closed  on  Monday,  Dec.  25,  and 
likewise  all  branches.  On  the  Sunday 
before  Christmas  the  branches  will  also 
be  closed,  but  the  Reading  and  Reference 
Rooms  of  the  Main  Library  will  be  open 
to  the  public  as  usual  from  9  a.  m.  to  6 
p.  m. 

On  New  Year's  day  all  branches  will 
remain  closed.  The  Circulation  depart- 
ment of  the  Main  Library  will  also  be 
closed.  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms 
open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 


The  Library  on  Its  Own  Feet 

By  the  provisions  of  an  act  passed  by 
the  last  Legislature,  it  was  planned  to 
consolidate  a  number  of  local  so-called 
taxing  bodies,  and  to  change  their  gov- 
erning boards  in  various  ways.  One  pro- 
vision proposed  to  abolish  the  Library 
Board,  vesting  the  control  and  adminis- 
tration of  the  Library  in  the  City  Coun- 
cil, presumably  through  one  of  its  com- 
mittees. A  number  of  civic  organiza- 
tions, two  weeks  prior  to  the  date  set  for 
the  referendum,  undertook  an  energetic 
campaign  against  interference  with  the 
Library.  The  voters  decisively  disap- 
proved the  proposed  Consolidation  Act, 
the  adverse  majority  reaching  a  total  of 
110,386.  Both  men  and  women  voters 
expressed  disapproval  as  shown  by  the 
summary  of  the  count: 

Men      Women       Total 

Yes   128,731       58,172     186,903 

No 193,978     103,311     297,289 

Majority  against 110,386

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year 
to  cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Jan- 
uary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy.

Sunday.

closed  on  Sunday.

Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Jook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
:ity,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ng  a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
n  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
ation  and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
>f  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to 
draw  five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
:ation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
jnce  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 

enewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 

ut  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
lelivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may  use 
he  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on 


the  open  shelves  or   the   books  in   the  stacks,   which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawr 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of  non- 
fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work 
of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound  in  one  or 
more  volumes,  -which  is  not  returned  within  two 
weeks  from  the  date  of  issue.  An  additional  charge 
of  two  cents  is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the 
return  of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  delivered 
to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until  it  Is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  i« 
drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear  and  all 
losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfactior 
of  the  Librarian.
